text: TLDR Founders 2025-06-13 Scale AI’s next phase 🚀, Zynga founder Mark Pincus' news startup 🤖, Dalton Caldwell exits YC 👋 📈 Headlines & Trends Dalton Caldwell exits YC after 12 years (1 minute read) After advising 1,000+ startups across 25 batches, Dalton Caldwell is leaving YC to co-found Standard Capital - an AI-native Series A firm with Paul Buchheit and Bryan Berg. Startup Revenue Growth Rates Are Slowing (5 minute read) Startup growth is cooling off - Q1 2025 data from 9,000 companies shows revenue growth dropped 3-5 percentage points, with top performers falling 8-10 points. While Q1 typically underperforms after Q4 highs, the real story is that growth rates have been declining since the 2021-2022 boom when cheap capital fueled expansion. Every sector got hit - consumer, SaaS, fintech, even AI. Scale AI Announces Next Phase of Company's Evolution (4 minute read) After the news broke over the weekend, Scale has officially announced its next phase with Meta investing $14.3B. Scale's founder Alexandr Wang will join Meta's AI initiatives. Chief Strategy Officer Jason Droege will step in as Scale's Interim CEO. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How (And When) to Raise Money if You're Pre-revenue (6 minute read) Short answer: don't raise pre-revenue - it's usually easier to just get paying customers first. However, if you're building hardware like Rivian, network effects like Uber, or heavily regulated products like Coinbase, you might actually need cash upfront. If that's you, what works is: teams with deep industry expertise, 75k+ free users who can't live without your product, viral social media launches, or top accelerators writing $500k checks. Just know that getting your first $1,000 in revenue is still probably easier than any of these alternatives. The Two Most Important Sentences In Sales (2 minute read) Every first sales call drives toward one moment: "I get it, and I think we can help." You can't say "I get it" without actually learning something - ask questions and uncover what's broken. You can't say "I think we can help" unless you know how - recognize their pattern and match it to past wins. It's a forcing function: learn first, promise second. When placed right, it creates a curiosity gap where buyers lean in because they actually want to hear what's next. You need a better testing strategy (7 minute read) A/B testing in tech often fails to deliver meaningful results, with only 10% of tests tied to revenue-beating controls. Teams frequently follow outdated practices and fail to implement successful outcomes, wasting resources. The industry needs a shift towards data-driven strategies and stakeholder alignment to improve testing effectiveness. ⚒️ Tools & Resources All Voice Lab (Tool) All Voice Lab, built for creators and developers, provides ultra-realistic AI voice generation and cloning with multilingual support. RapidMCP (Tool) RapidMCP lets you convert your REST API into an AI-ready MCP server in minutes, no code changes or backend modifications required. Lyra (Tool) AI-native video conferencing that turns every call into a real-time canvas and deal room. 🎁 Miscellaneous Is All Micromanagement Bad? (15 minute read) The "don't micromanage" advice has created managers so scared of hovering that they under-manage instead. Lattice's Jack Altman says micromanagement is a powerful tool when used to model excellence or bring attention to neglected areas. Rippling's Matt MacInnis advocates to "go and see" when data looks weird. Stripe built review systems with "red pen holders" who marked up anything going to 100+ users. The key is knowing when to dive deep and when to zoom out. Google AI Mode: First Thoughts & Survival Strategies (6 minute read) Google's new AI Mode is actually pretty good, and that's the problem. Users get comprehensive answers without clicking through to websites, cutting publisher traffic by 50%+. Good news: only 1% of users currently use it. Bad news: that won't last. Start thinking of survival strategies, like diversifying your traffic through other forms of media or building a brand so strong that people find you directly. Frontier AI Labs: the Call Option to AGI (10 minute read) From Meta plowing $14B into Scale AI to OpenAI's 80% price drop on its o3 model, the pace of AI development has gone parabolic. At the foundational model layer, startups, investors, and tech giants are going all in. For frontier AI labs to succeed, they must execute a multi-pronged strategy: capture advertising revenue from incumbents and build a defensible enterprise business while investing in moonshot endeavors. ⚡️ Quick Links Where Funded Founders Went To School (4 minute read) Top-tier universities like Stanford, Harvard, MIT, and UC Berkeley consistently produce the most venture-backed startup founders. Ex-Netflix VP Mike Verdu and Zynga founder Mark Pincus form AI games startup Playful.AI (3 minute read) Former Netflix VP Mike Verdu and Zynga founder Mark Pincus launched Playful.AI to transform game development using AI. How AI Redefines User Experience (3 minute read) AI now enables apps to understand and respond in English, shifting how we interact with software by letting users delegate tasks through voice commands. Instead of rigid relationships between data, interface, and logic, we're moving to intelligent agents that dynamically orchestrate distributed systems. LLMs become Models (grown not built), Apps become Views (just interfaces, not data stores), and Agents become Controllers (making real-time decisions across systems). text: TLDR Founders 2025-06-18 Robotaxi market 🚕, public cloud software earnings ☁️, founder micromanagement 💻 📈 Headlines & Trends PostHog vs. The Industry (3 minute read) PostHog raised $70 million from Stripe by breaking every SaaS rule: open-source products, generous free tiers, no outbound sales, price cuts instead of increases, and protection against viral usage spikes. While Salesforce hikes prices 6% annually, PostHog proves there's room for startups that do the exact opposite of what private equity-backed software companies typically do. Waymo+Uber Market Dynamics as Tesla Tests the Robotaxi Waters (4 minute read) Waymo captured 25% of San Francisco rideshare despite premium pricing, but Austin tells the real story. The Waymo+Uber exclusive partnership there has stolen 7 percentage points of market share from Lyft in months, while consumer surveys show 77% won't use Tesla FSD and 48% believe it should be illegal - a tough backdrop for Tesla's rumored June 22nd robotaxi launch. A Look Back at Q1 '25 Public Cloud Software Earnings (4 minute read) Cloud software's net new ARR dropped 28% year-over-year in Q1, but guidance tells a different story. 67% of companies guided above consensus for Q2 (up from 50% last quarter), while 92% beat Q1 estimates with a median 2.2% beat, suggesting the worst of the slowdown may be behind the industry despite headline growth numbers. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The Fear-Avoidance Model: Why Buyers Freeze at "Buy Now" (3 minute read) Even savvy buyers with compelling products freeze at checkout buttons after one bad purchase experience. Research shows fear of pain creates stronger avoidance than actual pain itself. One company breaks this psychological loop with $1 trials instead of free ones - customers cross the payment barrier while risking almost nothing, then sunk cost bias drives engagement. Different Kinds of Smart (5 minute read) Long Term Capital Management's collapse highlights that intelligence comes in various forms, some of which aren't easily measured. Successful problem-solving in business requires connecting interdisciplinary dots, balancing confidence with paranoia, and effective storytelling. Moreover, acknowledging different perspectives and delaying gratification by strategic distraction is critical in making informed and enduring decisions. Is All Micromanagement Bad? Here's How the Best Founders and Operators Balance Details and Delegation (24 minute read) Micromanagement is not inherently bad. Effective leaders use it strategically to model standards and drive focus in key areas. Founders and executives advocate balancing detailed involvement with delegation, focusing on data discrepancies, system reviews, and empowering teams through feedback. Leaders can leverage micromanagement wisely to support team autonomy while maintaining quality and clarity on priorities. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Get your portcos in front of 6+ million tech professionals (Sponsor) Get your B2B SaaS portcos instant access to millions of tech professionals across our 12 interest-based newsletters. text: TLDR Founders 2025-06-20 Mark Zuckerberg’s hiring spree 🚀, 2025 VC performance 📊, onboarding Claude 💻 📈 Headlines & Trends VC Fund Performance Q1 2025 (3 minute read) Recent venture funds are struggling to return cash with just 37% of 2019 funds and 30% of 2020 funds generating any distributions for LPs by Q1 2025. The market has shifted toward smaller funds, with 42% of 2024 vintage funds under $10 million versus 25% in 2020. The median IRR for 2017 funds sits at 11.5%, with massive variation from 5% at the 25th percentile to 28.3% at the 90th. Meta Aims to Hire AI Investors Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross (2 minute read) Meta wants to partially buy out VC fund NFDG and hire its partners Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross to lead AI efforts. The move - part of Zuckerberg's "founder mode" push that includes nine-figure signing bonuses to poach OpenAI talent - would give Meta minority stakes in AI startups like Perplexity and Safe Superintelligence without getting insider information. It is reportedly willing to walk away from restructuring talks and let the partnership expire in 2030 rather than bend to demands. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How We Onboarded Claude (3 minute read) This YC company treats Claude like any new engineer, with a formal onboarding doc (CLAUDE.md) checked into source control that gets updated whenever Claude does something dumb. The result is Claude now handles chunks of the company's engineering work, creating GitHub issues and PRs while following strict workflows to avoid its favorite failure modes like committing to main, claiming it tested code it can't run, and ignoring documentation completely. How Vanta Went From 1 To 100 (4 minute read) Vanta hit $10M ARR before its Series A by staying under the radar, then scaled to $100M+ by moving upmarket with design partnerships and enterprise sellers. Its CPO spends daily time using the actual product and its CRO runs weekly forecast reviews debugging every part of the GTM funnel. Vanta structures product teams around three time horizons, from immediate growth to moonshot exploration. How to fight back against AI tourists (4 minute read) AI demos go viral then churn hard. One growth leader saw 70-80% opt-out rates on experimental AI revenue. Annual plans boost net revenue retention by 10-20 percentage points versus monthly, but forcing them backfires with 10-14% refund rates. The winning tactics include making annual the default pricing display, offering exclusive AI credits upfront, and targeting upgrades in month two when customers are 3-4x more likely to convert. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Lovable 2.0 (Tool) Lovable 2.0 is a smart multiplayer AI workspace with chat agents, team collaboration, and built-in security scanning. VCs now face explosive noise at the top of funnel plus compressed time to product-market fit, forcing a shift from Pre-Seed → Seed → Series A to just two stages: Pre-PMF (bootstrapped) and Post-PMF (massive rounds in hyper-competitive markets). Founders Fund: The Disciples (17 minute read) Peter Thiel's Founders Fund thrived despite Thiel not being a traditional manager due to the contributions of key figures Napoleon Ta and Lauren Gross. Ta led successful growth investments while remaining low-profile, and Gross expanded the firm's AUM into billions. Thiel's network also birthed Valar Ventures and Mithril Capital, each playing unique roles in Thiel's broader investment strategy. Is Harvey built to last? (3 minute read) Harvey parlayed early GPT-4 access into a $100M+ valuation through genius branding over product quality. It won deals with 50-100 seat pilots that generated press releases, but multiple firms reported that Legora beat it in head-to-head product comparisons. Harvey's raise-and-burn strategy worked until challengers like Legora kept shipping better features faster than Harvey could catch up. ⚡️ Quick Links Metrics don't matter: Scale edition (3 minute read) Scale raised its Series A while still in Y Combinator after pivoting during the batch, breaking every fundraising "rule" about milestones and metrics. Ruthless prioritization while the dog pees on the floor (4 minute read) Time is zero-sum, so you can only do 1-10% of what needs doing, and it forces a binary choice between 10x tasks that transform your company and 0.1x tasks you must eliminate or delegate. The hardest part isn't choosing priorities, but explaining why you're letting important fires burn while focusing on what matters most - which looks insane to employees who don't understand the complete strategic picture. This exemplifies how a venture's pursuit of scale creates a "fungible worldview" where individual choices matter less. Assessing the Real Impact of Automation on Jobs (3 minute read) MIT economist David Autor found that automation exposure doesn't equal job loss by tracking task changes from 1977 to 2018 - Uber drivers saw 249% employment growth despite wage stagnation while proofreaders earned more but fewer jobs existed. The key insight is expertise fungibility: experts can do nonexpert work when displaced, but automation's impact depends on whether it removes routine tasks while adding abstract ones or strips away what makes a job specialized. ⚡️ Quick Links The Shape of the Essay Field (3 minute read) People don't know things for three reasons: it's unimportant, they're obtuse, or they're inexperienced. Crusoe Energy proved this by raising just $1.59B in equity to build massive data centers by securitizing generators and GPUs separately. Mastering structured finance turns capital intensity into a competitive advantage since physical assets create deeper moats than expensive customer acquisition in crowded software markets. The Waitlist Delusion (7 minute read) Waitlists should only exist when you have genuine supply constraints (hard product limits, quality issues, or service capacity), not for hype or brand building. Data shows waitlisted signups convert at 3x worse rates than immediate access, with performance decaying sharply over time, making extended waitlists a growth killer disguised as a growth tactic. When AI Has Better Taste Than You (5 minute read) AI is conquering not just our capabilities (skills) but potentially our taste (preferences), leaving only human agency—our will to act on our values—as our unique advantage. While AI can learn to predict and replicate even "great taste" through pattern recognition, only humans possess the subjective drive to determine what problems matter and what's worth pursuing. ⚡️ Quick Links The Preemptive Funding Offer (2 minute read) Entrepreneurs should weigh such offers' pros and cons, considering future funding potential and strategic fit with investors. Beauty Is Objective (3 minute read) Beauty in design follows universal principles—not arbitrary personal preference—like hierarchy, symmetry, and proportion, which create coherence and trust. AI Makes Bad Managers (3 minute read) Relying on AI for performance assessments prevents managers from developing crucial skills. Smart executives will automate low-stakes work to preserve cognitive energy for high-impact decisions while occasionally letting teams over-invest in craftsmanship where it truly matters. ⚡️ Quick Links You Want to Hate AI Slop But You Absolutely Love It (3 minute read) A study of 17,000 people found we prefer AI-generated poems over human ones when tested blind but immediately change our opinions once we learn the true authorship. The brutal reality is you have months (not years) to pick your platform, bet, and build something defensible before the gates close, because as Cursor's demolition of GitHub Copilot shows, the innovation replication cycle has compressed from 24 months to just 3-6 months. text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-02 Figma submits S-1 📃, Grammarly acquires Superhuman 📧, how Granola grows 📈 📈 Headlines & Trends Grammarly acquires AI email client Superhuman (2 minute read) Grammarly acquired the AI email client Superhuman to enhance its productivity suite. Superhuman's team, including CEO Rahul Vohra, will join Grammarly to integrate AI agents into email functionalities. The acquisition follows Grammarly's recent $1 billion funding boost from General Catalyst. Figma Files Registration Statement for Proposed Initial Public Offering (2 minute read) After confidentially submitting its S-1 to the SEC in April, Figma has now made it public and will list on NYSE under ticker "FIG"—marking the first major design tool IPO. Figma hasn't revealed share price or offering size yet, but the move signals confidence in the market for collaborative design software after years of speculation about when it would go public. The Great Differentiation (7 minute read) When AI made it trivially easy to copy any website design or marketing campaign, many companies started doing things that can't be copied—like Robinhood filming product announcements at French châteaus or Stripe building an actual Irish pub for its podcast. The lesson is counterintuitive but powerful: in an age where digital copying is free, the only way to stand out is to create experiences so physical, so expensive, or so authentically weird that competitors can't fake them even if they tried. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How Granola AI Grows (4 minute read) You look at Granola and think "another AI meeting notebook," then see 10% weekly growth and get confused. How did it win in a market with 20+ competitors? Granola realized everyone was solving the wrong problem. Competitors assumed people wanted perfect AI transcripts, but Granola noticed something: people actually like taking notes - it helps them pay attention. They just miss stuff. So instead of replacing note-taking, the company enhanced it. VCs loved it because it worked invisibly (no embarrassing bot joining calls) and made their scattered notes actually useful. The Brand That Turned Water Into $1.4 Billion (4 minute read) Mike Cessario watched musicians at Warped Tour refilling Monster Energy cans with water to stay hydrated - they wanted to look cool, not healthy. So he asked, 'What's the dumbest name for water?' Liquid Death. Now people crack open "Mango Chainsaw" and "Berry It Alive" because holding that skull-covered can makes them feel badass. Turns out making water feel rebellious is worth $1.4 billion - who knew boring products just needed better packaging? Do You Need a CAIO? The Rise of the Chief AI Officer in 2025 (17 minute read) Organizations are increasingly appointing Chief AI Officers (CAIOs) to lead cross-functional AI strategy. This role requires bridging technology with business, overseeing governance, and managing AI-related risks and innovations. Companies benefit from a CAIO to ensure AI efforts align with broader business goals and compliance standards. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Daytona (Tool) Daytona is a secure, high-performance cloud platform built for running and scaling AI-generated code with sub-90ms startup times. HelixDB (Tool) Get more relevant context for AI with a graph-vector database. Clado (Tool) Deep Research for people. 🎁 Miscellaneous Why Investors Don't Care About Your Business (5 minute read) VCs keep passing on profitable businesses because they're not looking for good companies - they need potential 100x returns. Since IPOs dried up, everyone's chasing AI startups and hyping valuations through secondary sales. If you take VC money, their moonshot business model becomes yours, whether you like it or not. No more painting by numbers (5 minute read) Building with AI is hard because it breaks every mental model we have about software—you can't assume it will work consistently, design paradigms don't exist yet, and users don't understand what it can do. The end result is that the decade-old "SaaS playbook" is dead, leaving both startups and incumbents with a blank sheet of paper instead of the familiar coloring book they've been painting by numbers in for years. ⚡️ Quick Links Should You “Rent” an Exec for Your Startup? A Fractional COO Weighs In (23 minute read) Hiring fractional executives provides startups with temporary, specialized leadership without long-term commitment. title: TLDR Founders 2025-07-04
text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-04 Anthropic $4B ARR 💰, $4.6T Service-as-Software opportunity 🚀, art of cold emailing 📧 📈 Headlines & Trends Anthropic Annual Revenue Reportedly Reaches $4 Billion (3 minute read) Anthropic hit $4 billion in annual revenue just as two leaders of its Claude Code product jumped to Cursor, one of Anthropic's biggest customers. The timing matters because AI companies are desperately trying to retain talent with salaries up to $690,000. Meta just paid $14.3 billion for 49% of Scale AI and poached its CEO. When your biggest customers can afford to steal your best people, vertical integration suddenly looks less like a moat and more like a feeding ground. The $4.6T Service-as-Software opportunity: Lessons from year one (11 minute read) AI-native startups are creating Services-as-Software companies to automate entire roles, positioning integration and outcomes as key differentiators over feature sets. Differentiation now relies on deeply embedding AI into customer environments, leading to a shift where pre-sales merge with post-sales, requiring extensive customization even before contracts are signed. Pricing models are evolving from access-based to outcome-based, aligning costs with the value AI delivers, signaling a major shift towards speed-to-value in a $4.6 trillion market opportunity. The $1B Land Grab for Legal Tech's Future (4 minute read) Clio bought vLex for $1 billion this week, shortly after Harvey expanded its LexisNexis partnership. Both moves reveal the same strategy - owning the full legal workflow from research to billing. The interesting part is how quickly legal data got locked up. Three companies control most legal data: Westlaw partnered with CoCounsel, Lexis with Harvey, and Clio now owns vLex. That concentration means smaller legal tech companies face a choice: find a dance partner or become one. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The Art of Cold-Emailing a Billionaire (5 minute read) Over half of billionaires have responded to this founder's cold emails, including Travis Kalanick and Eric Lefkofsky. Before writing, spend 90% of your effort figuring out the trade: what you want and what makes it worthwhile for them. Then hit four principles: establish competency fast, make your ask clear with the value to them, transparently state your self-interest, and show extra effort beyond the email. Most people think cold email is about persuasion when it's actually about understanding what the recipient values. Your Complete Guide to Discounting (3 minute read) Discounting can harm long-term value if done without purpose, decreasing margins and encouraging customer churn. Avoid discounts driven by competitor pricing or unclear product fit, which often trigger bad customer behavior like devaluing the product. Proper discount strategies focus on enhancing lifetime value, such as with big logos or strategic wedges, rather than closing deals fast. Lies per Second, Meetings per Decision Ratio, and Other Important Biz Metrics (4 minute read) Your startup is dying when it takes 15 meetings to make one decision, but traditional metrics won't tell you that. Andrew Chen tracks organizational health through weird ratios like "Meetings per Decision" and "PowerPoints per Launch" - inspired by how he measures everything unconventionally, even ranking activities by death risk ("micromorts"). These metrics catch decay early: healthy teams decide in 3-5 meetings, dying ones need 20. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Delve generated $1M in pipeline from TLDR newsletter ads (Sponsor) Delve ran 4 ads across 2 TLDR newsletters and brought in 66 leads, with a meaningful chunk from enterprise. The result: $1M in attributed pipeline and a 52x ROI. This case study breaks down their strategy, results, and example ads. Read the Delve case study. Phonely (Tool) Phonely AI offers AI-powered answering services for businesses. Shortcut (Tool) Shortcut is an AI-powered Excel spreadsheet. Jitter (Tool) Jitter is a collaborative motion design tool that lets you create professional animations in minutes. 🎁 Miscellaneous Stop Rushing to Resolve Two Competing Objectives (4 minute read) Most executives hear "grow fast but spend nothing" and immediately ask, "Which is more important?" That question marks you as a junior. Senior leaders know that premature resolution kills creative solutions - the real skill is operating within the tension. Instead of rushing to pick sides, they ask expansion questions: "What would we need to achieve both?" The paradox isn't a bug to fix but a feature that forces strategic thinking. Companies that win hold competing priorities simultaneously rather than settling for either/or thinking. Would you regret leaving before a massive exit? (2 minute read) Jason Oakley left Verafin in 2015 for a friend's startup, walking away from stock options that became worth millions when the company sold to Nasdaq for $2.75 billion in 2021. When friends asked if he regretted missing out on life-changing money, he said no - learning and growing mattered more than waiting around for something that might never happen. Is ChatGPT Really Rotting Our Brains? (6 minute read) MIT researchers found that students using ChatGPT showed less brain activity and couldn't remember a single sentence from essays they'd just written, while those writing without AI remembered 89% correctly. However, the study had major flaws: just 54 students writing 20-minute essays in a lab doesn't reflect real-world AI use. The key is staying mentally engaged - use AI to challenge your ideas, not replace your thinking entirely. ⚡️ Quick Links Why investing in growth-stage AI startups is getting riskier and more complicated (2 minute read) Investing in growth-stage AI startups is becoming riskier as established companies rapidly scale and new startups quickly reach significant valuations. Moonlighting at Multiple Startups (2 minute read) A founder discovered that a new hire was secretly working at 3-4 startups simultaneously with fake credentials. Marc Randolph on How It Took Netflix a Year and a Half to Find Product/Market Fit (3 minute read) Netflix failed for 18 months with mail-order DVDs until it changed one thing: no more late fees. title: TLDR Founders 2025-07-07
text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-07 NFDG x Meta 💰, Google’s AI updates 🤖, Palmer Luckey launches crypto-focused bank 🏦 🚨 Last 48 hours: Apply to the 43North accelerator for Seed stage startups (Sponsor) 43North is on the hunt for their next cohort - and applications are closing this Wednesday (July 9th) at 3PM ET. Five companies will be selected to receive a $1 million investment and join 43North's Y11 cohort in Buffalo, NY, for a year of programming and an unparalleled experience. Since 2014, 43North has invested in 74 startups alongside co-investors like Bessemer and Greycroft. These companies have gone on to raise close to $1BN with the help of 43North's extensive network, mentoring, and business support. Ready to build the next big in Buffalo, NY? Don't miss out on this opportunity. Apply before the deadline 📈 Headlines & Trends NFDG: The $1.1B VC Fund That 4X'd in Two Years—Then Got Acquired by Meta (11 minute read) NFDG, a $1.1 billion AI-focused VC fund led by Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross, achieved a 4x return in two years before being acquired by Meta. Meta's unique acqui-hire offered full NAV liquidity to LPs without governance rights over portfolio companies. The deal reflects a shift as investors prioritize direct involvement in AI development over traditional venture returns. Palmer Luckey, backed by Palantir's Lonsdale, to launch crypto-focused bank (3 minute read) Palmer Luckey, supported by Joe Lonsdale, is launching Erebor, a new crypto-focused bank, to fill the gap left by Silicon Valley Bank's collapse. Erebor is seeking a national bank charter and plans to offer services to tech sectors, including AI and crypto, using a digital-only model. The bank, which aims to be the most regulated entity in this sphere, will integrate stablecoins for fast transactions. Recap of Google's AI Updates in June (5 minute read) Google had 14 major AI announcements last month, spanning from new Gemini 2.5 models and voice conversations in Search to AlphaGenome for DNA research and weather prediction tools. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How The Smartest Founders Are Already Winning with AI (6 minute read) AI tools are outperforming human creators and changing business landscapes by offering superhuman advisory capabilities and reduced judgment anxiety for users. Traditional software interfaces and roles are becoming obsolete as rapid AI-driven product creation reshapes industry dynamics. Embracing AI as a collaborator enables success, while ignorance leads to irrelevance amidst this significant economic shift. AI, SEO, and How to Simplify Your Content Strategy (5 minute read) Apple's Eddy Cue just testified that search volume dropped for the first time in 22 years because people are using ChatGPT instead. Google's stock crashed 7%, wiping out $100 billion. However, here's what everyone's missing: when people ask AI tools buying questions, they're asking the same high-intent queries that drive SEO. "What is the best CRM software?" works the same whether you type it into Google or ChatGPT. Instead of panicking about AI killing search, just answer the questions your sales calls reveal customers actually ask. Getting Your First AEs Off the Ground (5 minute read) Hiring your first AEs before you have a pipeline is like buying a race car without building a track. Founders must prove outbound works themselves first. The biggest mistake is that founders outsource their target account list too early. Your new AEs see logos, you see patterns from dozens of customer calls. Build the list yourself, automate the outreach so AEs start with full calendars, then get out of their way. Their only job is closing deals, not fixing your CRM. ⚒️ Tools & Resources PromptPerf (Tool) PromptPerf is a tool for testing and comparing AI prompt outputs across multiple models with similarity scoring. Transkriptor 2.0 (Tool) Transkriptor is an AI-powered tool that transcribes, summarizes, and analyzes conversations into actionable insights. a0 (Tool) Build iOS mobile apps with AI. 🎁 Miscellaneous Is the "Conviction" Game in VC BS? (5 minute read) Carta ran 20,000 simulations comparing two VC strategies and found something VCs don't want to hear. A "spray and pray" fund with 100 investments beats a "conviction" fund with 20 investments almost every time - 2.6x returns vs 2.1x, and 99.7% chance of not losing money vs 81.9%. Only the top 5% of funds do better with conviction. Yet VCs still concentrate because LPs love the story of genius stock-picking. The math says most VCs would do better admitting they can't predict winners and making more bets. How to Measure AI Impact in Engineering Teams (5 minute read) The phrase, "AI improves developer productivity by 100x," is meaningless hype without data. Most engineering leaders report 0.3-1x actual improvement. DX's framework measures what matters - not just if developers accept AI suggestions, but whether that code makes it to production. Track three dimensions that follow the AI adoption lifecycle: First, utilization (are people using it?), then impact (is it actually helping?), and finally, cost (is the ROI positive?). Most importantly, measure AI agents as part of the team overseeing them, not as standalone contributors. Bundled Out of Business (4 minute read) Google just killed a $425 million company by making its product free. Marin Software spent 7 years building the perfect dashboard to manage ads across Google, Facebook, and Yahoo - agencies loved it, and it IPO'd in 2013. Then Google improved Ads Editor and Facebook launched Business Manager, both free. Marin's revenue peaked in 2015 and slid every year until this week's bankruptcy. ⚡️ Quick Links Small Is an Advantage (3 minute read) No other vendor will care about their success like you will because their success means your survival. Powered by Claude (2 minute read) Anthropic has provided a directory of 29 companies, many of them coding-focused, that are using Claude to build AI-powered products. Three Choices (2 minute read) Your business depends on three early choices: your platform (like Amazon vs Shopify) sets your costs, your investors decide your timeline and pressure, and your customers shape everything else. text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-09 Mistral $1B fundraise 🚀, Section 174 reversed 💰, CoreWeave acquires Core Scientific 💻 The 2025 State of Financial Automation (Sponsor) BILL's 2025 State of Financial Automation sheds light on how 750 US financial decision-makers and SMBs leverage trends, insights, and technology to tackle challenges and drive growth. Download our report to get the insights you need to stay ahead. 📈 Headlines & Trends Mistral is reportedly in talks to raise $1B (1 minute read) Mistral is negotiating to raise $1 billion from investors like Abu Dhabi's MGX fund and French lenders such as Bpifrance SACA. The French AI startup, known for its Le Chat chatbot and open-weight LLMs, has already raised $1.19 billion, reaching a $6.51 billion valuation. Mistral is collaborating with MGX and Nvidia to build Europe's largest AI data center, aligned with the UAE's €50 billion AI investment in France. CoreWeave acquires data center provider Core Scientific in $9B stock deal (1 minute read) CoreWeave acquired Core Scientific in a $9 billion stock deal, gaining over a gigawatt of data center capacity for AI workloads. Core Scientific's GPUs will shift from Bitcoin mining to running generative AI models. Cloud infrastructure providers are rapidly expanding data centers to meet AI demands, as shown by OpenAI's recent 4.5-gigawatt capacity deal with Oracle. Section 174 Is Reversed: All US-Based R&D Expenses Are Now Fully Deducted (3 minute read) The five-year R&D expense amortization nightmare is over. Congress just reversed Section 174, meaning you can fully deduct domestic R&D expenses immediately instead of spreading them over five years. Better yet, startups under $31M in revenue can get retroactive refunds for all amortized expenses since 2022. You have one year to file amended returns for 2022-2024 or take a catch-up deduction in 2025. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The Gruen Effect (3 minute read) Ever go to Target for toothpaste and leave with $200 worth of stuff? That's the Gruen Effect - when stores deliberately disorient you to trigger impulse buys. IKEA's maze-like layout keeps you browsing longer. Amazon's "related products" send you down rabbit holes. Notion's surprise feature pop-ups keep you exploring. Over 50% of purchases are unplanned because when you're disoriented, your brain stops asking "do I need this?" and starts thinking "ooh, that's nice." How to Talk About Your Competition (5 minute read) Competition proves there's money to be made. This CFO's competitors burned hundreds of millions educating the market while his company quietly converted those educated buyers with a Yeti mug marketing budget. Smart differentiation beats demonization - define yourself on your terms, not theirs. Be specific about who you serve: "We're built for teams of five, not five thousand." Feedback Culture (3 minute read) This founder discovered that culture is just three things - who you fire, promote, and hire. Everything else is corporate theater. At his startup, every piece of feedback connects to a cultural value that directly affects those decisions. No vague annual reviews. He gave feedback 50+ times last year, mostly positive, always tied to specific behaviors. text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-14 Google “acquires” Windsurf founders 🔍, SaaS 2.0 2️⃣, SVB’s State of the Markets 💹 📗 Usage Based Pricing Playbook (Sponsor) Pure seat-based pricing is quickly becoming outdated as usage-based and hybrid pricing models come to the forefront. By afternoon, Google hired Windsurf's CEO and top researchers for $2.4 billion, licensing their tech without buying the company. It's the latest reverse-acquihire where Big Tech gets talent and technology while dodging regulatory scrutiny. Now they're left with 250 employees and an interim CEO while their founders build competing tools at Google. Every AI startup that lost its leaders this way has struggled to maintain momentum. SVB's State of the Markets (6 minute read) AI ate half of all venture capital in 2024. While total VC investment hit $204 billion - the third-highest year ever - AI companies grabbed 50% of all deals and 61% of megadeal dollars. They're getting 100% valuation premiums over non-AI peers at late stage. Meanwhile, everyone else is playing defense. The typical Series A company now needs $2.5 million in revenue (up 75% from 2021), but growth has cooled to 70% annually. With only 7 tech IPOs last year and 640 unicorns trapped in private markets, the liquidity crisis continues - just 25% of unicorns even meet basic IPO requirements. SaaS 2.0 (7 minute read) Every SaaS app is just an expert's playbook turned into software. Salesforce encodes how the best salespeople work into lists and rules, but it's a rough translation - like getting advice through a game of telephone. Now AI can skip the middleman. Instead of buying software that nudges you toward best practices, you get the actual expert managing your data directly. A new dating app already does this - it turns a matchmaker into an AI that manages compatibility data. Soon we won't need apps at all, just specialists who happen to be software. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Ramesh Srinivasan, senior partner at McKinsey, shares how great leaders build empathy, resilience, and purpose (5 minute read) Founders must focus on leadership skills such as fearless learning, empathy, resilience, and purpose. Great leaders adapt quickly, manage relationships, and maintain stability through challenges. Purpose-driven leadership and recognizing knowledge gaps further contribute to their effective company growth. Goodbye, MQLs. Hello, Qualified Pipeline (5 minute read) This marketing VP bet everything on 100 target accounts instead of 10,000 leads - and pipeline exploded. The trick was launching messy ABM campaigns before they were ready, which exposed what actually worked. Now, BDRs report to marketing, and everyone's compensated on qualified pipeline, not lead volume. Turns out, companies buy differently at different sizes, so they tier their approach. However, the real shift was mental - stop thinking about capturing demand and start thinking about creating it. Fixing Marketing's Missing Middle (8 minute read) Most teams focus on awareness and conversions while ignoring the messy middle where 80% of leads die. There's never enough content between "hey check us out" and "buy now" - that trust-building zone where buyers actually decide. This article breaks down how Ahrefs and HubSpot fixed this by becoming media companies. Weekly newsletters, teaching content, and showing up even when metrics look flat. The hard truth is that most companies won't do this because it takes months to see results. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Creatify Product Video (Tool) Creatify Product Video is an AI tool that converts product images into marketing videos for brands to produce content quickly and at scale. In this leaked email, he admits they'd been artificially pumping Instagram's growth by siphoning users from Facebook, and the data from Japan and Russia showed what happens next. When Instagram usage passes Facebook, the entire Facebook network collapses. His solution was radical - stop all promotion between apps, maybe even spin Instagram off entirely before regulators forced them to. He knew Kevin Systrom would never agree to slow Instagram's growth, writing "I am skeptical it is possible to both retain Kevin and do what it takes." text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-21 LLM content ranking ✍️, Sales for AI 💰, HALO Effect 😇 Stop building integrations, start shipping features. (Sponsor) Your users demand Slack, Salesforce, HubSpot integrations. Your engineers want to build the core product. Join 150+ SaaS companies, including Appsmith, Pipedrive, and CrewAI, who ship integrations in days, not months. See Paragon 2.0 in action 📈 Headlines & Trends The HALO Effect (8 minute read) Google paid $2.4B to "hire" Windsurf's team while the startup somehow keeps operating. These aren't acquisitions - companies hire the founders and license the IP while the startup continues under new leadership. Kevin Kwok calls them HALOs (Hire And License Out). Antitrust killed normal acquisitions, but that's not the real story. In AI, value isn't in products that go obsolete yearly - it's in teams who know how to ship at the frontier. Google doesn't want Windsurf's $80M revenue. It wants people who can transform models into products. HALOs close in days because they're honest about what matters - the people. The Sales Strategy Conquering the AI Market (2 minute read) AI's rapid evolution has disrupted traditional software sales strategies, making forward-deployed engineers (FDEs) crucial for success. FDEs work with clients to customize AI solutions, prioritizing outcomes over simply selling products. This approach, first modeled by Palantir, is essential for enterprise contracts over $100k due to high staffing costs. AI Agents Are Creating a New Security Nightmare for Enterprises and Startups (10 minute read) AI agents are creating security challenges with unchecked outbound API traffic, introducing complex risks in visibility, security, and cost management. The emergence of protocols like Anthropic's MCP and Google's A2A increases the risk of data breaches due to excessive permissions given to AI agents. AI gateways can regulate these risks by enforcing security measures, providing oversight, and enhancing observability in agentic traffic. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Never Make the Same Mistake Twice (3 minute read) The U.S. Armed Forces and Fortune 500 companies use After-Action Reviews to learn from every win and loss. Four simple questions turn mistakes into growth: What did I intend? What happened? Why did it happen? What will I do differently? Writing Content That Ranks with LLMs (5 minute read) Buyers stopped Googling and started asking ChatGPT - now those AI-sourced leads convert way better than traditional search traffic. One Head of GTM noticed this shift and rebuilt his entire content process. Instead of chasing keywords, he mines sales calls for actual buyer questions, then feeds competitor content into Claude to spot patterns everyone else misses. The secret to ranking with LLMs? Format everything like an AI would think. Clear question as your H1, logical follow-ups as H2s, supporting proof as H3s. LLMs don't scan for keywords - they parse structure and intent. Why Startup Founders Struggle With Adaptability (And How To Overcome The Challenge) (9 minute read) Startup founders struggle with adaptability due to their inherent rigidity, preference for past successes, and discomfort with uncertainty. Their leadership style often involves dictatorial decision-making and resistance to feedback, stifling innovation. To improve, founders should focus on listening, delegating tasks, and embracing ambiguity to enhance personal and business growth. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Zapier MCP 2.0 (Tool) Zapier MCP 2.0 is a middleware platform that connects AI apps to thousands of other apps and actions through secure integrations. remio (Tool) remio is a local-first AI note-taking tool that helps users collect, organize, and manage personal knowledge. Figma Buzz (Tool) Figma Buzz is a collaborative tool for creating and managing branded design assets at scale within Figma. 🎁 Miscellaneous SaaS vs AI-Native Software (5 minute read) Everyone claims to be AI-powered, but there's a huge difference between SaaS with AI sprinkled on top and true AI-native software. The test is simple - does the product still work if you remove the AI? Salesforce works fine without AI features. Jasper dies instantly. The modern software stack now has five layers: cloud platforms (AWS), infrastructure (MongoDB), LLM providers (OpenAI), traditional SaaS (Zoom), and AI-native apps (Perplexity). AI compute demand will grow 1,000x this decade. The infrastructure giants - cloud and LLM providers - are the new kings because they power everything else. How YouTube Won the Battle for TV Viewers (8 minute read) YouTube now gets more TV viewing than Disney's entire empire - broadcast network, a dozen cable channels, and three streaming services combined. People watch over a billion hours daily on TVs, not phones. Creators noticed and adapted - "Good Mythical Morning" hosts employ 100 people and make hour-long episodes now. Netflix analysts suggest spending $500M to poach top YouTubers, but creators say no check is big enough to abandon channels they spent years building. The Return of the Point Solution (8 minute read) The next trillion-dollar platforms are hiding as point solutions growing faster than anything from the SaaS era. Datadog started tiny when New Relic owned the market. Now Datadog's bigger than everyone who tried to kill it. AI accelerates this - Claude Code threatens entire categories overnight, yet survivors will dwarf cloud giants. We're 3 years post-ChatGPT, right where cloud was in 2010 before the boom. ⚡️ Quick Links Finding the difficult work (1 minute read) Everyone chases easy projects, which guarantees you'll be replaceable. title: TLDR Founders 2025-07-23
text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-23 Elad Gil on AI market 🤖, Figma IPO analysis 💹, how to build AI-first companies 🚀 AI broke your pricing model — and spreadsheets won't fix it (Sponsor) One user query triggers 50 model calls. You suddenly realize your "per request" pricing doesn't make sense. Users get hit with surprise bills, while your usage dashboard is still stuck two hours in the past. This is the new reality of AI pricing —and legacy billing systems can't keep up.The solution is to apply the same discipline and iterative approach you use to develop any new features. This first post in Metronome's multi-part series explains why successful companies treat pricing like a product system that's testable, observable, responsive. Pricing is your most visible product feature. Treat it like one. Read "Pricing Is a Product" here 📈 Headlines & Trends AI Market Clarity (9 minute read) Elad Gil argues the fog has lifted. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, and other giants now dominate foundation models while Harvey leads legal and Cursor rules coding. Customer support converges on Decagon and Sierra and Perplexity stakes search. Capital and compute now block new LLM entrants, yet openings remain in accounting, compliance, and security. The next breakout will hinge on bold go to market, agentic workflows, and roll‑up strategies. Figma's IPO Isn't Flashy — But That's the Point (4 minute read) Figma just set its IPO range at $25–$28 a share, valuing the company at around $16B—less than Adobe once offered, but above its 2024 secondary round. It's not a moonshot, but it's solid: $1B run rate, strong margins, and a clear growth story. While most coverage focuses on past performance, Figma is quietly packing AI features into its product, possibly adding upside for retail investors who still believe in narrative. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics This Email Strategy Changed Everything (5 minute read) Citizen, a real time safety alerts app, was losing users after it muted push notifications. Julia Knight tackled the issue by hand-collecting local emergencies, dropping headlines into a Figma template, and blasting fifteen tailored emails daily. The clicks proved so strong that the team automated triggers that now send forty million incident emails each month, bringing dormant accounts back for free, cutting paid acquisition, and even lowering iterable costs through renegotiation. How Claude Is Building A Unique Growth Engine Out of User-Created Apps (8 minute read) Claude launched AI-powered apps with a clever twist - users pay with their own Claude subscription, not the creator's. A teacher can build a SAT prep tool and share it with students, but each student must sign up for Claude to use it. No API keys, no hosting costs, no authentication headaches. The constraint becomes the opportunity. Each app spawns its own growth loop as project tools drive team invites and calculators spread through forums. While Cursor and Bolt chase developers, Claude went the opposite direction - maximum constraints for minimum friction, turning every creator into a distribution channel. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Anti-space (Tool) Antispace is an AI operating system that predicts, coordinates, and executes actions across your workspace to automate repetitive tasks. Funds that invest in female founders (Resource) A list of funds that invest in female founders. HyperArc (Tool) HyperArc is an AI-native business intelligence platform that delivers instant, auditable insights from natural language queries. 🎁 Miscellaneous The AI‑Native Enterprise Playbook (4 minute read) Now that every enterprise tool claims to be "AI-powered," what actually moves the needle? This playbook keeps it simple: pick a weird industry with painful, overlooked problems. Build for real workflows, not just demo decks. Prove your product works with clear, objective results, especially in edge cases. Let AI power more than just the product: use it across onboarding, support, sales, and GTM. How Carta's internal AI agents save thousands of hours of back-and-forth work (9 minute read) Most teams use AI to save money. Carta used it to save time where it mattered most: judgment-heavy accounting tasks that bottlenecked client service. Instead of replacing humans, it built in-house agents that could gather context, reason through complex workflows, and act autonomously. One agent now handles 25,000 monthly tasks in seconds, saving 3,500+ hours. Six Waves of Software Pricing (9 minute read) In the 1950s, your software arrived in a semi‑truck bolted to its hardware. Seventy‑five years and six pricing waves later, vendors bill for shelf‑less shelfware, meter compute like electricity, and blend seats with usage in hybrid plans. This author traces the journey from boxed SKUs to SaaS, the ASP flame‑out, CFOs fighting surprise overages, and why clear value metrics will decide how we price AI next. ⚡️ Quick Links Patterns Across 5 Years of YC Investing (2 minute read) YC investments have increasingly focused on cybersecurity, industrial/manufacturing, and B2B sectors since 2020. Your Startup Will Drive Itself (3 minute read) Picture an AI growth hacker that boosts clicks in days while accidentally killing sign‑ups. title: TLDR Founders 2025-07-25
text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-25 GPT-5 in August 5️⃣, Vanta valued at $4B 💰, AI PageRank 🔍 📈 Headlines & Trends OpenAI prepares to launch GPT-5 in August (13 minute read) OpenAI plans to launch GPT-5 in early August, offering integrated o3 reasoning capabilities and mini/nano versions available through its API. GPT-5 aims to unify OpenAI's language models and simplify usage across applications, although it won't reach AGI status immediately. The anticipated declaration of AGI could impact OpenAI's revenue agreement with Microsoft. CrowdStrike-backed compliance startup Vanta valued at $4 billion in new funding round (2 minute read) Vanta's valuation surged to $4 billion after closing a $150 million funding round backed by CrowdStrike Ventures. The startup, which helps companies manage compliance with cybersecurity frameworks like SOC 2 and ISO 27001, serves over 12,000 customers, including tech giants Atlassian and Snowflake. More investors included Wellington Management, Atlassian Ventures, JPMorgan Chase, and Sequoia Capital. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How to get recommended by ChatGPT (7 minute read) AI answer engines are becoming the most influential gatekeepers in B2B buying, with ChatGPT hitting a billion users and 2.5 billion daily prompts. Buyers now ask AI what to buy instead of researching themselves - if your product isn't mentioned, it might as well not exist. Josh Blyskal from Profound found only 11% of citations overlap between ChatGPT and Perplexity, meaning you need platform-specific strategies. Reddit is now the most-cited domain at 6% of citations, and one-in-three citations come from comparative listicles. Almost half of cited domains shift within a single month. Your Complete Guide to Revenue Leakage (4 minute read) Revenue leakage is the gap between what you should earn and what you actually collect - the ghost quietly draining dollars through missed invoices, zombie accounts still getting service, and pilot pricing that never graduates. It's a systems problem disguised as a people problem, living in the cracks between sales handoffs and billing processes. Common culprits include auto-renewals that silently fail, discounts hardcoded forever, and churned customers still accessing your product months later. Reframing AI chatbots as self-service tools (2 minute read) AI chatbots should be reframed as self-service tools rather than human support replacements due to their limitations. Achieving this involves clearly defining their purpose and implementing technical guardrails to ensure alignment. Understanding AI's capabilities and limits helps in effectively integrating them into customer support. ⚒️ Tools & Resources The Future of SaaS Pricing, Featuring Kyle Poyar (Sponsor) This episode of Metronome's Future of SaaS Pricing features Kyle Poyar, Co-Founder & Operating Partner at Tremont. Kyle shares 2025 pricing trends and how to stay competitive in the rapidly evolving SaaS landscape. Watch the webinar (ungated) AI Meeting Notes by Notion (Tool) AI Meeting Notes by Notion is a tool that transcribes and organizes meeting notes directly within Notion, synced with your calendar. PRDKit (Tool) PRDKit is an AI tool that generates structured product specs, wireframes, and go-to-market assets from feature ideas. The Substack AI Report (Resource) Substack surveyed 2,000 publishers and found 45% use AI - mostly men over 45 in tech/business categories who rely on it for research and editing, not generating full posts. 🎁 Miscellaneous The General Theory of Enshittification (4 minute read) Enshittification isn't just about social media - it's the natural lifecycle of any business with network effects. Companies offer great value to build their user base, then exploit that base once locked in. Uber's "earnings inflection strategy" jumping from losses to profits by squeezing customers proves this was always the plan. What Founders Teach Us (3 minute read) Founders are river people, not villagers - they thrive in uncertainty while others choose stability. They see differently: they're fearless enough to hear "no" constantly but keep asking, they actively seek contrarians to challenge their thinking, and they know execution beats strategy. They ignore the right feedback, focus obsessively on recruiting A-players, and carry unshakable optimism despite knowing 90% of startups fail. Zero-sum Thinking and the Labor Market (7 minute read) College graduates now have higher unemployment rates than the overall workforce for the first time since 2018 - the opposite of how this is supposed to work. Young men are struggling most, with 50,000 healthcare jobs going to women graduates while men gained far fewer positions across all sectors. People are applying to 1,000 jobs and getting rejected by algorithms in what's become a casino economy where getting hired feels like winning the lottery. This broken system feeds zero-sum thinking - the belief that my success requires your failure - which has replaced the Sputnik generation's positive-sum worldview where everyone could grow together. ⚡️ Quick Links Behind-the-scenes with one of the fastest growing wellness startups (3 minute read) Fay built a platform that connects tens of thousands of clients with dietitians covered by insurance and raised $75M from Goldman Sachs and General Catalyst. PageRank in the Age of AI (2 minute read) The internet's content distribution is about to mirror online advertising's auction system, but without the ads. You need to have a point of view (3 minute read) Rising executives often freeze when asked "What should we do?" because they're afraid of being wrong. title: TLDR Founders 2025-07-28
text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-28 Trump's AI Action Plan 🇺🇸, AI bubble 🫧, Reddit growth strategy 🗣️ High Observability costs? You are likely paying for useless telemetry data (Sponsor) Coinbase's $65M observability bill showed how fast costs can grow. They're not alone: observability costs are spiraling out of control across industries. Your systems generate a constant stream of data. Much of it is essential, but a surprising amount is just noise from routine operations that add no value. Dash0 lets you easily block this costly noise with a simple, visual tool before it costs you. Start free with Dash0 and take control of your observability budget. 📈 Headlines & Trends Palantir joins list of 20 most valuable U.S. companies, with stock more than doubling in 2025 (2 minute read) Palantir's stock surge has made it one of the 20 most valuable U.S. companies, with its market cap reaching $375 billion, surpassing Home Depot and Procter & Gamble. Investor confidence is driven by its robust AI segment and its strengthened ties with the U.S. government, reflected in a 45% revenue increase from its government business. Despite being valued at 273 times forward earnings, the company remains small in revenue compared to peers in the top 20. Inside Trump's Ambitious AI Action Plan (4 minute read) Trump scrapped Biden's AI safety rules to let markets "win the AI race" - but forgot to include a game plan. The 28-page blueprint backs open-source AI and cuts regulation while keeping technical standards. Good for researchers who need tool access. Bad for implementation: 103 vague actions with no deadlines, no funding, and a third don't even name who's responsible. They'll track job losses but ignore AI fraud and discrimination. Stanford HAI says without details, even good ideas like regulatory sandboxes will die in bureaucracy. Investors Are Suddenly Pulling Out of AI (3 minute read) Investors who poured $104.3 billion into AI startups in 2025 are now seeing lower returns upon exiting, with just $36 billion in cashouts reported. Despite this disparity, AI funding continues to surge, led by massive investments in companies like OpenAI and Scale AI. There are concerns of an "AI bubble" echo, with some major players like Microsoft pulling back, signaling caution around overvaluation risks. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics 5 Harsh Truths of Managing Product Feedback (9 minute read) Managing product feedback is challenging due to its messy, disorganized nature, and often low-quality submissions. It requires significant effort to gather, organize, and prioritize feedback, differentiating between valuable signals and irrelevant noise. Despite these challenges, understanding customer feedback is vital for building products that users love and can significantly impact a company's success. Max MRR: Your growth ceiling (5 minute read) Churn rate literally sets your maximum company size - divide new MRR by churn to see your ceiling. Buffer discovered this brutally: at 6% churn and $8,400 new MRR monthly, it was mathematically capped at $140k forever. Even tripling marketing couldn't help because exponential churn always catches linear growth. When COVID pushed churn to 7%, revenue crashed 35% overnight. Here's the magic: cutting churn from 6% to 3% doubles your ceiling instantly. Buffer finally grasped this, reduced churn, and growth exploded. Should My Company Invest in a Reddit Strategy? (4 minute read) Reddit isn't just about Google rankings anymore - it's training the AI that your customers use to research products. One First Republic Bank user found something surprising. Reddit visitors converted just as well as Google visitors. However, paid Reddit ads completely failed. The difference is authenticity - Reddit users instantly reject anything that feels like marketing. The real value is using Reddit as a research lab. You can see exactly what customers ask about your competitors when no one's watching. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Golpol AI (Tool) Turn your docs into an explainer video. Mastra (Tool) The TypeScript agent framework. Nayla (Tool) Nayla is an AI-powered browser and workspace that connects tabs and apps to automate tasks, extract data, and assist during calls. 🎁 Miscellaneous Cursor Acquires Koala to Challenge GitHub Copilot (2 minute read) Anysphere acquired AI-powered CRM startup Koala to develop a dedicated enterprise-readiness team as it battles GitHub Copilot for large Fortune 500 contracts. Unreleased OpenAI Model Achieves Gold Medal on International Math Olympiad (3 minute read) OpenAI's latest experimental reasoning model solved 5 of 6 problems on the 2025 International Math Olympiad, earning 35/42 points. The model won't be released for several months. Data is actually not a great VC-backed business (3 minute read) Getting value from data is still incredibly hard, making data businesses terrible VC investments. Auren Hoffman predicted exponential demand, but fewer than 100 hedge funds buy alternative data today. After 1,000 SaaS unicorns, there's been one DaaS unicorn: ZoomInfo, which never took VC. Data companies grow slowly with predictable revenue - perfect for PE, not VCs needing explosive growth. ⚡️ Quick Links What Founders Should Never Sign, and How to Push Back Without Killing the Deal (3 minute read) Good investors expect pushback - accepting garbage terms makes you look clueless. Finding Leverage [Theory of Constraints] (3 minute read) Working harder on the wrong thing is why you're stuck - systems move at the speed of their slowest part. Figma aims at $16.4 billion valuation as tech IPOs bounce back (4 minute read) Figma is targeting a $16.4 billion valuation in its NYSE IPO, aiming to raise up to $1.03 billion by selling shares between $25 and $28. title: TLDR Founders 2025-07-30
text: TLDR Founders 2025-07-30 Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei’s story 🚀, Quince valued at $4.5B 💰, Linear’s path to PMF 💻 The software's value proposition is changing (Sponsor) Value used to be about what software could offer (think Microsoft Office licenses). Then it was about what it enabled (Adobe Creative Cloud). With AI, we are entering a new era where the value of software is what it actually does (think OpenAI charging by tokens used to generate content). Companies must be ready for this change, but many monetization models are stuck in the past .  Read more about how the three eras of software value are now calling for companies to evolve—and the cost of staying behind—on the Metronome blog . P.S. Later this month, Metronome will release a full whitepaper on this topic: TheMonetization Operation Model. Get early access 📈 Headlines & Trends Nvidia-backed Fireworks AI races to $4B valuation with Lightspeed, Index Ventures (3 minute read) Fireworks AI surged to a $4 billion valuation with its high-performance AI inference technology, optimized for PyTorch and open-source models. The company provides faster and more cost-effective deployments than traditional cloud providers, with partnerships including Google Cloud and NVIDIA bolstering its position. Upcoming funding will support further development of "compound AI systems" and global expansion, positioning Fireworks for a potential IPO. The Making Of Dario Amodei (32 minute read) Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic, has been vocal about AI's rapid progression and its potential risks and opportunities, predicting significant job impacts while advocating for AI safety measures and regulations. Anthropic, valued at $61 billion, has shifted focus from consumer applications like ChatGPT to supplying AI models to businesses, primarily through APIs, which has driven fast revenue growth. Amodei's dedication to AI safety stems from personal tragedy, and he navigates industry skepticism while pursuing rapid AI advancements, believing in the transformative potential of AI, particularly in medical and scientific fields. Quince, A Bright Spot In Struggling D2C Fashion Space, More Than Doubles Valuation To $4.5B With Second Raise In 6 Months (2 minute read) Quince, an affordable luxury DTC brand, raised $200 million at a $4.5 billion valuation, doubling in six months. Iconiq Capital led the round, boosting Quince's total funding to $461.5 million. Despite challenges in the D2C sector, Quince stands out with fast-growing revenue and popularity among Gen Z and millennials due to its affordable pricing. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Linear's Path to Product-Market Fit (5 minute read) Karri Saarinen hated Jira so much at Airbnb that he built a Chrome extension to redesign it - 100 coworkers installed it. That hack became Linear, now worth $1.25B. The Finnish founders spent a year validating through bar conversations before building. They gated access obsessively, handpicking 10 users weekly from 10,000 waitlist signups. While competitors chase flexibility, Linear ships opinionated defaults. It hit profitability in 2021 with just 80 employees by choosing quality over growth metrics. The Mere Urgency Effect — Why We Choose Tasks That Feel More Urgent (3 minute read) You abandon writing a $5,000 sales page to answer a dinner text because your brain prioritizes time-sensitive tasks over important ones. Researchers proved this by offering people five Hershey's Kisses or three - when they added a 10-minute deadline to the smaller reward, participants choosing it jumped from 13% to 31%. The Million-Dollar Experiment: How GitHub Boosted Conversions with a Single Flow Change (8 minute read) Most developers didn't even know GitHub had paid features - they thought it was just open source. When Thibault Imbert watched users sign up, they picked free by default because GitHub hid what paid plans offered. Inspired by Grammarly's transparent signup flow, his team redesigned everything. Instead of hiding paid features, they showed exactly what was free versus paid, segmented by user type, and wrote copy like developers talking to developers. The results shocked them - 35% more paid conversions, worth over $1 million in ARR. They were so surprised they double-checked the analytics. CEO Nat Friedman loved it because it felt natural, not pushy. The lesson is counterintuitive - being honest about pricing actually increases conversions when you respect your users' intelligence. ⚒️ Tools & Resources How Delve turned 4 newsletter ads into $1M pipeline (Sponsor) Delve ran just 4 ads in TLDR newsletters. The payoff? 66 high-quality leads (including enterprise companies) $1M in pipeline 52x ROI We break down Delve's exact strategy, detailed results, and share the actual ads that crushed it. Read the Delve case study. Gadget (Tool) Gadget is an AI-powered IDE that streamlines app development with built-in infrastructure, tools, and deployment support. Shadow (Tool) Shadow is a meeting assistant that captures insights and turns decisions into actionable tasks to streamline follow-ups. NotionApps 2.0 (Tool) NotionApps 2.0 is a no-code platform for building web apps, portals, and internal tools on top of Notion data. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Chainsmokers' Mantis VC closes $100M third fund (1 minute read) Mantis VC, co-founded by The Chainsmokers, raised $100 million for its third fund, 25% more than its previous $80 million fund. Despite its celebrity status, Mantis focuses on B2B startups, with investments in companies like Chainguard and Rogo. It leverages its extensive network from private shows for Fortune 500 companies for valuable customer introductions. How the System of Record Dies (4 minute read) Everyone thinks AI agents will kill systems of record - why log in Salesforce when agents do everything? David Peterson thinks that's backwards. Agents need constraints and permissions, making systems of record essential. However, the pricing power shifts. Companies will pay for work, not data storage. When incumbents throttle APIs to maintain revenue, customers revolt. The winners won't be today's systems desperately locking down data - they'll be whoever builds ground-up "systems of intelligence" without legacy baggage. AI is too expensive for unlimited subscriptions (2 minute read) Anthropic got backlash for adding usage limits to Claude Max, following similar issues with Cursor and Windsurf. It's always coding tools that face pricing revolts. The problem is simple - coding requires flow state, not paywalls interrupting a 5-hour feature build. Running out of credits in an SEO tool works because it's discrete tasks. However, limits mid-debug ruin the IDE experience. These companies aren't being deceptive, they're stuck. AI costs too much for unlimited subscriptions that developers want. Like expensive mobile data in 2003, this will look ridiculous once cheaper models make unlimited usage possible. ⚡️ Quick Links Why Seed Rounds Are Growing as Startups Shrink (1 minute read) Writing $1M checks doesn't move the needle for a $500M fund - that's why smaller seed rounds are vanishing. Your Most Profitable Features Are the Ones You Forgot About (1 minute read) 80% of features in the average software product are never used, yet founders keep shipping them anyway. Defaults Matter (2 minute read) Your product's default settings determine success because AI agents won't customize anything - they use whatever works out-of-the-box. title: TLDR Founders 2025-08-01
text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-01 Figma IPO 💹, fal raises $125M 💰, timing IPO windows 🪟 Build Faster, Smarter, Leaner—with APIs Doing the Heavy Lifting (Sponsor) It's time to ditch the system silos and slow workarounds. See how API integration can help your business work smarter—not harder. From cutting out manual work to accessing real-time insights across your systems, this guide explains how APIs help build a backbone for lean, agile operations. You'll learn: The fundamentals of APIs and how they work How connected systems create efficiency and accuracy Why APIs and data security go hand in hand Key considerations when evaluating and implementing APIs Download your guide to breaking down silos, staying connected, and scaling smarter. 📈 Headlines & Trends Timing IPO Windows (5 minute read) After 2021's ZIRP era, tech executives swore they'd never miss another liquidity window - yet here we are with barely any IPOs despite markets at all-time highs. Since the election, speculative stocks like ARKK have outperformed the Mag7 by 5x. Companies keep waiting for the "perfect" IPO window while multiples march back toward 2021 levels, and recent IPOs like Rubrik and Circle show strong performance. The few who prepared early are capitalizing - Figma's is massively oversubscribed. In an industry where barriers to competition are falling and public markets are begging for new names to bid on, waiting for the perfect moment is the exact mistake everyone promised they wouldn't repeat. Figma's top VCs are sitting on $24 billion worth of stock after massive IPO pop (7 minute read) Following its massive IPO debut, Figma's value surged to nearly $68 billion, with initial investors like Index Ventures, Greylock, Kleiner Perkins, and Sequoia holding $24 billion in stock. The IPO raised $1.2 billion, with most proceeds going to existing investors, although a lock-up period restricts major sales until January. This marks a shift for the tech IPO market, previously slow due to inflation and market conditions, with Figma standing out as a significant success story. AI infrastructure company fal raises $125 million, valuing company at $1.5 billion (3 minute read) AI infrastructure company fal raised $125 million in a Series C round, led by Meritech and joined by Salesforce Ventures, Shopify Ventures, and Google AI Futures fund, valuing it at $1.5 billion. Fal specializes in running multimodal models for enterprises, such as image generation AI for ecommerce and advertising. Enterprise clients use fal to create infinite ad iterations and different product images, providing significant value through diverse asset customization. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics ARR Doesn't Need a Redefinition (4 minute read) Everyone's panicking about how to measure ARR with AI's usage-based pricing, but ARR was never that clean. Snowflake is entirely usage-based yet valued as recurring revenue. Datadog mixes flat fees with variable metrics. Even traditional SaaS has messy pricing - customers can be charged by seats, active users, and usage tiers all at once. What makes revenue "recurring" isn't whether it's fixed monthly, it's whether you can predict it with confidence. Your job is to define ARR honestly for your business model, not game it to fit some perfect definition. Putting the Jobs to be Done Interview to Practice (20 minute read) Executing a JTBD (Jobs to be Done) interview process revealed that interview difficulty stems from the need for ethnographic research skills and overcoming human memory limitations. The recommendation is to focus on recent buyers and conduct partner interviews to improve accuracy. JTBD insights at Commoncog discovered two customer paths to membership, revealing a predominant conversion via paywalled articles within series, challenging prior assumptions and guiding strategic focus. Foosball and the Startup Journey (3 minute read) David Cummings shares the memory of securing office space with a foosball table early in his startup journey. He recounts signing up for a credit card to purchase a foosball table after relocating to a new office space due to cash constraints. The story highlights the evolving nature of startup culture and the importance of cherishing early entrepreneurial experiences. ⚒️ Tools & Resources ChatGPT Study Mode (Tool) ChatGPT now provides a learning experience that helps you work through problems step by step instead of just getting an answer. Opal (Tool) Google's new AI-based mini app builder. Descript (Tool) Edit your videos and podcasts just by typing. 🎁 Miscellaneous Alt Investment Platform iCapital Raises $820M At $7.5B Valuation With An Eye Toward Acquisitions (2 minute read) iCapital raised $820 million, raising its valuation to $7.5 billion. The company plans strategic acquisitions and tech investments. Co-led by SurgoCap Partners and T. Rowe Price, the round included existing backers like Temasek and UBS. iCapital's platform manages $945 billion and supports private market investments and asset management. Encore Anxiety (4 minute read) The first time they tried stand-up comedy, this author strategically picked an open mic in a neighborhood where nobody knew them - failure would be anonymous. The set went well. The second time was different - now people had expectations, and expectation brings its own terror. Success creates a special hell where you're not afraid of being a fraud, but afraid you're genuine and still can't summon that magic again. The antidote isn't caring less but caring more about truthseeking - whatever that meant before anyone was watching. Vibe Analysis (6 minute read) If vibe coding is creating software by talking to AI, vibe analysis is getting insights the same way - and it might be even bigger. There are 2 million software engineers in the US, but at least 5 million people who pull data to answer questions every day. That's 10 billion hours annually spent on reports, dashboards, and deciding which experiments to ship. ⚡️ Quick Links Sam Altman on hiring for slope, not y-intercept (2 minute read) When asked about his hardest hiring lessons, Sam Altman says the secret is simpler than most think - “hire for slope, not y-intercept." Will raising venture sell my soul? (3 minute read) A founder's letter asking if taking VC money would ruin everything they love about building their project. In theory… (1 minute read) When reality and theory disagree, update the theory - theories exist to explain what works, not dictate what's possible.
