title: TLDR Founders 2025-08-04
text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-04 Microsoft joins $4T club 💰, Will Apple buy Mistral 🍎, where to find ideas 💡 Think it's too early for a 401(k)? Think again. (Sponsor) Your new hire checklist probably looks something like this: paperwork, onboarding plan, email setup… 401(k)?!? Yup, even scrappy startups can offer retirement plans. In states like California, it's even required, with fines for businesses with employees who don't have a qualified retirement plan in place. Stay compliant and help keep your employees happy. And with Guideline , all employer fees are waived for the first year. Terms Apply. Set up takes ~9 minutes. The benefits can last decades. Learn more today. For information only. Not Investment/Tax advice. See here for important details. 📈 Headlines & Trends Welcome to the $4 trillion club (8 minute read) Microsoft's valuation hit $4 trillion after reporting Q2 earnings with $76.4 billion in revenue and strong cloud growth, marking it as the second company to reach this milestone. Meta's stock surged nearly 12% pre-market following its Q2 report, highlighting substantial revenue growth driven by its AI investments and expansive capex plans for 2025. Ambiq and Figma's IPOs showed strong market demand, with Figma reaching a $33 per-share debut valuation near $20 billion. Will Apple buy Mistral? (6 minute read) Apple might acquire Mistral, a French AI company, to enhance its AI capabilities and counter Big Tech rivals. Speculation arises as Mistral faces challenges competing with US giants like OpenAI and Anthropic. However, possible regulatory hurdles and Mistral's desire for independence may complicate the acquisition. All hail the demise of Adobe-Figma (3 minute read) Adobe tried to buy Figma for $20 billion in 2022, double its private valuation, in what looked like a classic incumbent squashing a competitor. The deal fell apart under regulatory scrutiny. Fast forward to this week - Figma went public at $55 billion, nearly triple what Adobe offered. The math on who won: Figma's backers went from almost selling at $20 billion to holding shares worth $55 billion, Figma got a $1 billion breakup fee from Adobe, and customers get actual competition instead of another Adobe monopoly. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Where to find ideas (5 minute read) A Harvard Business School graduate became an EMT for $15 an hour just to understand how emergency services really worked - and that's exactly how he found his startup idea. Another founder wanted to build software for greenhouses, so instead of scheduling interviews with greenhouse owners, he just bought one and ran it himself. These founders understood something most people miss - you can't discover real startup ideas through discovery interviews and whiteboard sessions. You need to actually become your customer. Why Your Pitch Doesn't Work (4 minute read) Pitch failures often stem from delivering what you want rather than what your audience needs. Emphasize unique differentiators instead of personal achievements, as audiences care about what sets you apart. Ensure memorability to influence decisions made after your pitch. New Media, Legacy Media & How To Do Founder PR (5 minute read) The founder of a PR agency that's worked with Perplexity and Ramp would turn down the Wall Street Journal for Lenny's Podcast - and she has a point. Emilie Gerber from Six Eastern explained that a one-sentence WSJ mention would do nothing while 60 minutes on a podcast actually drives customers. After working with over 100 tech companies, she's learned that most PR pitches fail because they make the company the center of the universe instead of a lens for understanding bigger trends. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Adoption barriers or safeguards? Choosing the right PLG model for your startup (Sponsor) Users want an easy way to work with your product before opening their wallet—but being overly-generous with free trials can kill the business. Metronome's new blog offers a framework for choosing between 5 different PLG billing models + a practical example of how Snowflake gets the balance right. Read the blog Tolt (Tool) Tolt is an all-in-one affiliate marketing platform for SaaS startups with automated payouts, fraud protection, and performance tracking. Plakar (Tool) Plakar is an open-source backup platform that secures local and cloud data with encrypted, lightweight, and easily restorable snapshots. Keystone (Tool) The first AI QA engineer to join your team. 🎁 Miscellaneous Can analysis ever be automated? (6 minute read) Four charts about internet usage sit in front of you - one made by a Nobel laureate, one by ChatGPT, one pulled from Reddit, and one cobbled together in ten minutes. There's no way to tell which is correct without checking every formula and data source yourself. That's the catch-22 of automated analysis - unlike vibe-coded apps, where bugs eventually surface, you can't test if a chart "works" just by looking at it. Shortcut claims its AI can solve Financial Modeling World Cup problems and build cap tables in minutes, but how do you trust it? There's no reality to compare it against because the spreadsheet IS reality. Are Advisors Worth the Dilution? (4 minute read) A Figma advisor who joined after the seed round might have just made $65 million from their IPO for maybe a few hours of work per month. The math is brutal - advisors typically get 0.11% equity at seed stage, vesting over 2 years instead of the standard 4 for employees, plus single-trigger acceleration on exits. Most CEOs hire advisors then forget about them. 90% of advisors aren't worth it - they take similar dilution to a VP-level hire but only give you an hour per week. Will data centers crash the economy? (6 minute read) Big Tech is spending $102.5 billion per quarter on AI data centers - more than all consumer spending contributed to GDP growth last quarter - and the scary part is how they're financing it. This looks eerily similar to the telecom bubble of 2000, except with one crucial difference: this time, banks are involved. Private credit funds, which barely existed a decade ago, are borrowing from banks to lend to data center companies, creating the exact kind of opaque debt chain that triggered 2008. If AI revenue doesn't materialize fast enough to pay back these loans, the correlations are high - they'll all default at once. ⚡️ Quick Links Three definitions of risk (and why it matters) (4 minute read) The classic definition of risk treats it as volatility, but imagine being offered a stock that either gains you $100 or $1,000,000 - technically high volatility, but zero actual risk since you win either way. Not All Growth Is Created Equal (3 minute read) AI companies are hitting $100M ARR in under a year, but some of those stories unravel just as fast - growth spikes, customers churn, the business deflates. Good Abstractions (3 minute read) We create abstractions that let us forget how certain complex systems are designed underneath. title: TLDR Founders 2025-08-06
text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-06 Clay valued at $3B+ 🚀, Small Action Models for AI agents 🤖, AI adoption at work 💻 Most Enterprise AI Apps Fail. Here's What Works. (Sponsor) Most AI apps never make it past the prototype phase because of compliance gaps, security issues, and weak infrastructure. This guide outlines how successful teams move quickly from idea to launch. WorkOS gives you the APIs and enterprise features—SSO, SCIM, RBAC, and Audit Logs—so your team can go from prototype to production, and fast. Read the guide → 📈 Headlines & Trends Small Action Models Are the Future of AI Agents (2 minute read) Using large foundation models for agent tool-calling is like using a Ferrari to deliver pizza - expensive, rate-limited, and overpowered for what's essentially a selection task. NVIDIA's research shows small language models are actually better suited for tool orchestration, and testing confirms this - a Salesforce xLAM model runs fast on a Mac M2 Pro with excellent accuracy while forcing better system design through deterministic interfaces and robust error handling. Clay confirms it closed $100M round at $3.1B valuation (1 minute read) Clay secured a $100 million Series C at a $3.1 billion valuation, led by CapitalG, confirming earlier reports. This funding follows significant rounds, including a $1.25 billion Series B six months ago. With prominent clients like OpenAI and Canva, Clay expects to triple last year's revenue to $100 million by year-end. Palantir tops $1 billion in revenue for the first time (4 minute read) Palantir exceeded Wall Street estimates, achieving over $1 billion in quarterly revenue for the first time and raising its full-year revenue guidance. The company forecasts Q3 revenue between $1.083 billion and $1.087 billion, surpassing analyst expectations. Significant growth was seen in U.S. revenues, with government contracts and commercial segments contributing to a 140% increase in total contract value to $2.27 billion. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics 25 proven tactics to accelerate AI adoption at your company (7 minute read) Companies like Shopify, Zapier, and Duolingo are using specific strategies to enhance AI adoption, focusing on organizational change rather than just technology. These strategies include explaining actionable AI tactics, tracking and rewarding AI usage, reducing approval bottlenecks for AI tools, and transforming AI enthusiasts into educators. Successful AI adoption also hinges on prioritizing high-impact tasks and integrating AI into daily workflows to drive productivity and innovation. How to Win With AI Video Ads (4 minute read) Ad expert Dan Birdwhistell (who's managed $800M for Shopify and Coinbase) found AI video ads beat human ads by 2x on conversion - here's his playbook after 50+ hours with Google's Veo3. The Efficiency Paradox: Why Over-Optimizing Could Kill Your Startup (4 minute read) Frederick Taylor, the godfather of efficiency, failed spectacularly when he first optimized a machine shop - production dropped 30% because he perfected a system for yesterday's problems while the company was still figuring out what to build. Modern founders fall into the same trap, spending weeks implementing Google-style OKRs with ten customers, or A/B testing headlines while their business model is broken. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Flowise (Tool) Flowise is an open-source visual development platform for building AI agents, multi-agent systems, and LLM workflows. Hatch (Program) Equal Ventures has launched a "Hatch" program to work with founders before they even incorporate. Appwrite Sites (Tool) Appwrite Sites is an open-source platform for deploying and scaling static and server-rendered web applications with built-in domain management. 🎁 Miscellaneous Should You Take Money from Corporate Investment Arms? (4 minute read) Stanford data shows corporate VCs now participate in over 20% of startup deals, offering patient capital, distribution channels, and even built-in exits - but they come with hidden strings. Unlike traditional VCs who reserve capital for follow-ons and help build syndicates, corporates rarely lead rounds, move slower with weeks of approvals, and often push toxic terms like ROFRs that scare off other acquirers. Another trap is when their strategic agenda conflicts with maximizing your value - they might block a great exit because the buyer is their competitor, or vanish overnight when corporate leadership changes. You Could Just Choose Optimism (3 minute read) Two old men on separate flights both ordered champagne - one complained it wasn't good enough and spent the flight grouchy, the other was genuinely happy to be drinking free alcohol while flying over the Alps. As a founder, you face this choice daily: complain about investor rejections, bugs, and competition, or stay genuinely excited about building something new. Complainers hide behind "high standards", but choosing optimism doesn't mean accepting mediocrity - it means not letting every setback poison your energy, because in startups, your mood becomes your team's mood and eventually your company's culture. AI's "Just Ship it." problem (5 minute read) Stanford studied 100,000 developers and found AI boosts simple tasks by 60% but complex work by only 5%, with most gains around 20% - plus AI-generated code increases rework. The punchline is that AI makes you throw spaghetti at the wall faster, but it doesn't make your wall bigger or help you validate whether the spaghetti stuck - and when 75% of product ideas fail anyway, shipping yourself out of business just got easier. ⚡️ Quick Links Gold Diggers (1 minute read) Some founders raise money to look for gold, others find the gold, then raise money to extract it - and you'd better be the second type. Should VCs Invest In ‘Jockeys' Or ‘Horses'? (4 minute read) VCs often debate whether to prioritize the founder or the business. Andy Rachleff explains his product philosophy of "slugging percentage, not batting average" (2 minute read) While every other job rewards batting average, startups need a slugging percentage - take tons of shots knowing most will fail because the magnitude of your wins matters way more than how often you're right. title: TLDR Founders 2025-08-08
text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-08 OpenAI GPT-5 5️⃣, AI coding startups 🚀, when founders clash ⚔️ What companies like OpenAI are doing differently when it comes to pricing (Sponsor) Everyone's scrambling to figure out the right pricing model for an age where software completes tasks autonomously. ↘️ Legacy operators use rigid pricing systems and manual processes. They struggle with execution because every change breaks something, and every experiment requires months of coordination. ↗️ Companies like OpenAI use monetization as a competitive advantage. They respond to market changes in real time, adjust pricing faster than competitors , and configure new packaging models instantly as customers request them. 👀 Read more about SaaS Pricing in the Value Era on the Metronome blog , or get early access to the whitepaper . 📈 Headlines & Trends Introducing GPT-5 (17 minute read) OpenAI's GPT-5 boasts superior intelligence and performance across coding, writing, and health-related tasks. It significantly reduces hallucinations and sycophancy, providing more accurate and reliable responses. GPT-5 Pro, a variant for complex questions, delivers even higher expertise. New functionalities allow users to customize interactions and improve real-world applications. GPT-5 Hands-On: Welcome to the Stone Age (3 minute read) GPT-5 marks the "stone age" for AI. It doesn't just use tools - it thinks with them, like humans first learning to shape stones changed everything. Testing showed it one-shotted dependency conflicts that stumped every other model by using yarn commands like Deep Research uses web search, iterating and reasoning through problems instead of just guessing. While it's worse at writing than GPT-4.5 (producing more "LinkedIn slop"), it's unequivocally the best coding model - creating entire production-ready websites with SQLite databases in one shot, where other models gave scaffolding or plans, automating software engineering from maybe 65% to 72% in one leap. The high costs and thin margins threatening AI coding startups (5 minute read) AI coding startups face high costs and thin margins due to expensive LLMs, with Windsurf failing to sustain profitability, leading to its sale. This competitive market pressures companies like Anysphere and Replit, which depend on reducing their LLM supplier costs or developing in-house models. Startup valuations have been impacted, and many struggle to remain independent amidst rising operational expenses and direct competition from suppliers like OpenAI and Anthropic. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Raising Prices Has Its Place. But It Shouldn't Be One Of Your Best Ideas. (2 minute read) Raising prices shouldn't be a primary growth strategy, especially during slow growth periods. It's often a desperate tactic that doesn't attract new customers and can drive them to competitors. The solution is to focus on acquiring more customers, not extracting more revenue from existing ones. When Founders Clash (3 minute read) It doesn't matter how much you raise or how good your product is, co-founder fights kill more startups than competition or running out of money ever will. Four months after raising from Perplexity's founder, AI startup Astra shut down - not because its product failed or money ran out, but because its co-founders couldn't agree on whether to wait for Fortune 500 deals or pivot to faster sales. The company had everything going for it: two enterprise beta clients, backing from prestigious investors. However, while one founder wanted those logos and the other wanted speed, their startup bled out during the argument. How to Build Differentiated Agentic AI Products: A Practical Guide (9 minute read) Building agentic AI products involves determining the appropriate level of system autonomy, starting small with structured workflows, and integrating domain-specific logic for differentiation. Focus on tying AI efforts to actual business value and use open-source frameworks for efficiency. Ensure reliability and effectiveness through rigorous unit and integration testing. ⚒️ Tools & Resources AI Operator by BLACKBOX AI (Tool) AI Operator is a browser-based AI agent that sees your screen and provides real-time, interactive support for coding, research, and web tasks. Tersa (Tool) Tersa is an open-source visual canvas for building and running AI workflows with drag-and-drop nodes and model integrations. Sim Studio (Tool) Sim Studio is an open-source visual canvas for building, collaborating on, and deploying AI agent workflows integrated with tools like Slack, Supabase, and Gmail. 🎁 Miscellaneous What's worth wanting? (3 minute read) Most of us chase things we don't actually want - we're copycats suffering from Mimetic Desire, unconsciously modeling our ambitions after what we see others wanting on Instagram and LinkedIn. The author realized his first startup was theater for other people, driven by founder models he looked up to and wanting to make his entrepreneurial dad proud, not what he actually wanted. Real desires are simple - work 3 hours daily on something meaningful, stay healthy, help people, and love family - but we get tricked into thin desires like viral posts and VP titles because we mistake someone else's articulation for our own resonance, so the key is developing taste by noticing what drains you versus what you do when nobody's watching. Are we ready for AI sales reps? (3 minute read) RB2B's founder replaced his two-person sales-assist team with a $99/month AI clone of himself that handled 1,683 conversations in 22 days - and the results were... exactly the same as before. The AI handles 90% of buyer questions (demos, pricing, and SOC2 docs). While it hasn't boosted conversions yet, it frees the founder to spend 30-60 minutes daily reviewing conversations for product insights instead of paying two salaries. In praise of quitting (3 minute read) A former lawyer quit right before making partner and shut down her startup when it wasn't going to have the impact investors expected - and says quitting is her superpower. Life is like tournament poker, where you get one stack of chips, so don't waste them on barely positive-value bets just because you're afraid to fold. Most founders stay too long in failing ventures because quitting feels like locking in a loss, but here's the reframe: imagine you were instantly unsubscribed from everything in your life, then ask what you'd actually subscribe to - you'll find most of your current activities wouldn't make the cut. ⚡️ Quick Links Ben Silbermann explains how Pinterest found product/market fit (2 minute read) Pinterest's one thing was making collections look really cool because if they looked lame, no one would share them with friends. The Sunk Cost Fallacy (2 minute read) We finish terrible movies because we've already watched 30 minutes, and companies blow millions on doomed projects because they've already spent so much - the sunk cost fallacy makes us throw good money after bad. Jesse Zhang on early team building (1 minute read) Decagon's CEO says the most important thing to get right before $1M ARR is your early team - every future hire will look at them and ask, "Would I be inspired to work with these people every day?" Claim your 50% startup discount and marketing exposure 📈 Headlines & Trends Meta acquires AI audio startup WaveForms (2 minute read) Meta has acquired AI startup WaveForms, bolstering its AI unit, Superintelligence Labs. Co-founders Alexis Conneau and Coralie Lemaitre have joined Meta. WaveForms focused on the "Speech Turing Test" and "Emotional General Intelligence." ChatGPT is bringing back 4o as an option because people missed it (4 minute read) OpenAI has reinstated GPT-4o as an option in ChatGPT following backlash from users mourning its replacement with GPT-5. Users missed GPT-4o's perceived personality and flexibility for various tasks. OpenAI indicated that Plus users could choose between models. CEO Sam Altman also stated OpenAI aims to address concerns over GPT-5's performance and transparency. Is the IPO Process a Scam? (3 minute read) Figma's stock popped 250% on IPO day - the highest since 1999. For those who weren't following, this wasn't actually mispricing, but artificial scarcity at work. Only 3% of shares were available to trade because investment banks deliberately keep the float tiny. Long-term investors got their allocation and held tight while traders scrambled for the few shares available. Within days, the stock crashed 50% as reality set in. Now it's easy to call IPOs a scam, but this is exactly how the system is designed to work - banks manufacture these pops to make headlines and reward their best clients with instant profits. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Millions in revenue with a <15 person team (6 minute read) Most startups die because they build what they think customers want, not what customers actually need. Vitalize Care's founders slept in hospitals for eight months watching nurses work - and discovered their mental health app was solving the wrong problem. Nurses didn't need therapy, they needed someone to fix their staffing nightmare. After throwing out their entire product and selling hospitals on Figma mockups, they hit millions in ARR with just 15 people. Now, every engineer flies to hospitals monthly because that extreme customer proximity is their superpower. How to 'test' your messaging to find Message-Market Fit (6 minute read) Message-Market Fit means your ideal buyers immediately "get it". You see high response rates on outbound, better click-through rates, and prospects understand your value before you explain it. To test properly, run 2-3 meaningfully different messaging angles across channels you're already using, aim for at least 8-12 qualified leads per variant over two weeks, then debrief with sales to see which message got people talking with fewer objections - if one variant improves conversion by 15%+, double down and bake it into all your GTM assets. Should We Offer Pilots? (5 minute read) A Chief Procurement Officer insists that not offering pilots is a "major red flag." However, the best sales teams do something counterintuitive - they default to no pilots and make buyers pay to prove they're serious. If you must offer one, take a page from CJ Edmonds at Conversion Logix, who structures pilots with continuation clauses that auto-convert to paid contracts unless cancelled within 120 days. Most buyers requesting pilots aren't actually testing functionality - they're testing whether you're enterprise-grade. So if you must offer one, define success metrics upfront, charge for it, and remember that pilots should be your last resort. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Get your portcos in front of 6+ million tech professionals (Sponsor) Get your B2B SaaS portcos instant access to millions of tech professionals across our 12 interest-based newsletters. Additional perks (i.e., discounts) are included when you sign up as a VC partner . Overlap AI (Tool) Overlap AI is a multimodal video marketing agent that edits, captions, and auto-posts content to TikTok, Reels, Shorts, and X through a simple agentic workflow. Entellegence.ai (Tool) Entelligence.ai delivers AI-powered code reviews with full codebase context, catching issues early and boosting team productivity with auto-generated docs and engineering insights. Algebras AI (Tool) Algebras AI is an open-source platform for quickly building and scaling AI apps in any language with instant outputs and no proofreading delays. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Private Market Trap (3 minute read) Money is flowing into private companies but can't get out - there's $13 trillion trapped. You should care because startups convince people to work for less money by giving them stock. That deal only makes sense if the stock can turn into cash someday. No IPOs means that stock is just paper, which means talented people may be less inclined to join startups. Europe's Great Founders Must Unretire (7 minute read) European entrepreneurs tend to retire early, missing potential decades of innovation and productivity. This contrasts with the US, where cultural norms push founders to create continually, contributing significantly to technological advancements. Europe must encourage its most successful entrepreneurs to keep building, or it risks falling behind the US and China in technology and innovation. How Debt to EBITDA Ratios are Impacting Deals in 2025 (8 minute read) Everyone obsesses over valuation multiples, but the metric that actually controls private equity deals is simpler: how much debt you can pile on. In 2021, PE firms were loading companies with 7.5x EBITDA in debt, and someone would always fund it. Today, you're lucky to get 5x, and junior debt has essentially vanished. The entire PE model depends on leverage to juice returns - when debt dries up, so do the deals. That's why take-privates of companies like PagerDuty and Olo are suddenly everywhere. With rates high and lenders spooked, PE firms can only make the math work on deeply discounted public companies. ⚡️ Quick Links Use Advanced Prompts for More AI Value (3 minute read) Advanced prompt engineering enhances AI's capability, delivering more precise insights. Why a Y Combinator startup tackling AI agents for Windows gave up and pivoted (4 minute read) Y Combinator startup Pig.dev abandoned its AI agentic tech for Windows, pivoting to develop Muscle Mem, an AI caching system. text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-13 Airbnb update 🏠, Perplexity offers to buy Chrome 💰, B2B content marketing 📚 The one thing founders don't have to worry about anymore with Stable (Sponsor) You're a founder, which means your to-do list is long. What if Stable could help take one thing off your plate for good? Your business address. Whether you're building remotely, in a coworking space, expanding or relocating, Stable gives startups a permanent business address they can use everywhere. You'll look more professional and keep your personal address off documents, protecting your privacy. Plus, you can manage all your incoming mail digitally. Want to never think about address or mail management again? Get an address here. 📍 P.S. Get 50% off the Grow plan for 1 year with code tldr50. 📈 Headlines & Trends Airbnb: 2Q25 Business Update (5 minute read) Airbnb's growth is slowing again - nights booked decelerated to 7% year-over-year, while competitor Booking grew 8% despite being twice Airbnb's size, with 309 million nights booked. Revenue grew 13% but the stock dropped 8% on weak guidance. The interesting part is CEO Brian Chesky's response. The company is pivoting to become more like Netflix than a search engine, with more bookings now coming from homepage recommendations than search. This lets it steer demand to markets with supply. After resisting for years, it is finally adding hotels and launching a loyalty program - plus authorizing $6 billion in buybacks on top of the $1 billion it just spent. The wildest bet is Services, where 60,000 people applied to offer everything from home chefs to yoga classes. What is Chrome Worth? (3 minute read) Perplexity just offered $34.5 billion for Chrome, betting on antitrust forcing Google to sell. The math reveals it is lowballing by hundreds of billions. Google pays Apple $20 billion yearly to be Safari's default search for 850 million users - that's $21 per user. Perplexity's offer values Chrome's 3.5 billion users at just $9 each. If Chrome users got Safari pricing, that's $73 billion in annual revenue. At typical tech multiples, Chrome is worth $172-$630 billion, not $34.5 billion. The Next Phase of B2B Content Marketing: Influencer Nodes (6 minute read) Lars Lofgren spent 15 years in B2B content and watched company blogs go from king to dead. SEO ruled from 2015 to 2022 until AI blew it all up. Right now, teams are crushing it by AI-ifying their workflows, but Lofgren calls BS - it's a race to the bottom where someone always trains their model better. His bet: when everyone's drowning in lukewarm AI slop, people will crave real stories from real humans. B2C already figured this out with creators. B2B is next. CMOs have three options: pay influencers, hire them, or become one. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Why Nailing Your Ideal Customer Profile Is Hard (3 minute read) Everyone knows they need an ICP, but most companies never actually nail one down. First, the data problem - you're stitching together multiple CRMs, enriching firmographic data, and slicing numbers endlessly to figure out where you win most, where deals are biggest, and where it's easiest to make money. It's part math, part art, and mostly grind. Most ICPs die here because companies lack an analytical go-to person. However, even if you crush the analysis, you hit problem two: opinions. Your fancy data now has to merge with perspectives from sales, marketing, product, and, most importantly, your CEO. Everyone has strong feelings about which customers are best. The goal isn't perfection, it's intention. Get just enough agreement to stop pondering and start executing. The New Fundraising Playbook: Seed-Strapping (3 minute read) Seed-strapping involves startups raising a single early funding round and relying on revenue for growth, with AI making this approach more feasible by accelerating product-market fit and reducing scaling costs. Notable examples include Zapier and Calendly, with recent AI startups like Aragon and Jenni.ai also adopting this model. While seed-strapping offers founder control and operational discipline, risks include losing to better-funded competitors and limitations in hiring and liquidity. The Story Behind Owner's Pivots to a Billion-Dollar Business (8 minute read) Adam Guild dropped out of 10th grade after his Minecraft server hit six figures. At 17, he saved his mom's failing dog grooming salon with SEO that worked so well, he built a $2,000/month website builder for restaurants. Then COVID wiped out everything overnight. He called panicked restaurant owners who all said the same thing: delivery apps were killing them with 30% fees. Six weeks later, he'd rebuilt everything for online ordering and changed his pricing to $1.50 per order. Demo requests went from zero to hundreds per week. He broke every rule - bundling multiple products early, copying competitors openly, and removing customization options. The owner just hit $1 billion because Guild learned SMBs don't want flexibility, they want something that just works. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Arena (Tool) Software vendor discovery, evaluation, and purchasing for the AI era. Maintouch (Tool) Full-stack organic marketing for B2B. Super (Tool) Super is an AI-powered enterprise search and assistant platform that connects all your company tools to turn data into actionable answers and custom workflows. 🎁 Miscellaneous Why AI Can't Touch Your Taste (4 minute read) Everyone has access to the same AI tools now - your intern can create presentations as good as senior consultants. Technical execution itself may no longer be a competitive advantage. However, there's a paradox: as AI democratizes building, taste becomes everything. Research shows that labeling products "AI-powered" actually hurts trust and lowers expectations. We're hitting AI fatigue fast. The teams that win won't be the ones with the best AI - they'll be the ones who know what to build, not just how to build it. That judgment of what feels right versus what's technically possible? AI can't touch that. Region Beta Paradox (3 minute read) People stay in mediocre relationships for years but thrive after brutal breakups. That's the Region Beta Paradox - we tolerate mild discomfort forever but spring into action when pain gets intense. Smart marketers use this by turning tolerable problems into unbearable ones. Chime shows your paycheck being held hostage for two weeks. Apple animates surveillance cameras hovering over your screen. Misfits Market exposes how Big Grocery wastes millions of pounds of "ugly" food. The lesson: buyers won't budge for mild discomfort. Show them why their status quo is secretly intolerable and switching becomes urgent relief, not risky change. Coverd raises $7.8M to gamify personal finance management (1 minute read) Coverd secured $7.8 million to revamp personal finance management. The platform introduces a gamified experience with high rewards, including a planned credit card offering 100% cash back on purchases. This approach aims to transform mundane financial tasks into engaging and rewarding activities. ⚡️ Quick Links Let's talk reference checks (2 minute read) Wade Foster does 20 reference calls per executive hire at Zapier - 10 from the candidate's list, 10 he finds himself. When Credits Meet ARR (3 minute read) Everyone's adding credit-based pricing to their AI features and hitting the same wall - how do you report this in ARR without making a mess? Why Most SDRs Will Be AI SDRs In 2026+ (3 minute read) 95% of human SDRs can't actually explain what they're selling. title: TLDR Founders 2025-08-15
text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-15 State of AI 2025 🤖, Cohere raises $500M 💰, CEO as Chief Editor ✍️ 🚀 Anything — The world's best agent for building products without code (Sponsor) Anything lets you ship mobile apps, websites, or both — without writing a single line of code. The new benchmark isn't T2D3 anymore, it's Q2T3 (quadruple, quadruple, triple, triple, triple). Cohere Raises $500M at $6.8B Valuation (3 minute read) Cohere secured $500 million in an oversubscribed round led by Radical Ventures and Inovia Capital, propelled to a $6.8 billion valuation. The company added Joelle Pineau (former Meta AI research lead) as Chief AI Officer and Francois Chadwick (ex-Uber) as CFO. Cohere will invest in agentic AI tools like North, designed to automate enterprise workflows, while maintaining a security-first, on-premises deployment approach. Jack Dorsey: I think of the CEO role as "Chief Editor" of the company (3 minute read) Jack Dorsey runs Square like a magazine editor - thousands of ideas flood in from engineers, support, and designers, but only one or two really push the company forward. The CEO's job is to review everything and cut ruthlessly. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Aligning Product and Go-To-Market with Metrics (3 minute read) Dan Teodosiu and a fellow Balderton Capital EIR discuss aligning product and go-to-market teams using customer-value metrics. The article outlines historical examples where companies faltered by neglecting customer value. It encourages leaders to focus on why customers would repeatedly choose their product. Your Playbook to Forecasting Pipeline at Scale (7 minute read) Rubrik hit $1 billion ARR with a forecasting process that starts five days before executives see numbers. Wednesday: reps validate every deal with managers. Monday: the CRO digs into each opportunity. Friday: C-suite gets a forecast that's been pressure-tested multiple times. It marks deals as "in-path" (leadership believes it) or "out-of-path" (risky). When a rep commits a deal but their manager disagrees, that's an automatic investigation. Quotas come from actual data - Rubrik calculates exactly how many calls turn into meetings or deals, so reps believe their targets are achievable. The company forces sales, marketing, and finance into weekly accountability forums where nobody can dodge revenue questions. Time to Exit: Are Startups Getting Older? (5 minute read) Startups are taking longer to exit, with the median age at IPO now at 13.5 years due to increased private capital availability, market pressures, and a focus on profitability. This trend results in longer holding periods for investors, a shift towards later-stage investing, and more emphasis on secondary sales for liquidity. Investors may adapt by extending fund lifecycles, diversifying investment stages, and prioritizing sustainable growth. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Dolphin (Tool) Turn your customer calls into short, ready-to-share highlight reels. Shor (Tool) Affordable payroll for global teams. Knowlify (Tool) Transform any content into explainer videos. 🎁 Miscellaneous When Building Gets Easy, Winning Gets Hard (6 minute read) Sam Altman says we're entering the "fast fashion era of SaaS," and he's right. AI split software into two camps - the hard builds like infrastructure and foundation models, and the easy builds you can ship in a weekend with Cursor. When building gets easy, winning gets exponentially harder because everyone has the same product. The margin reality is brutal. Replit swings between -14% and +36% gross margins, while OpenAI sits at 45% and Nvidia at 75%. The Red Queen Effect - Mental Model: Work Smarter, Not Faster (4 minute read) The Red Queen told Alice she must run twice as fast just to get anywhere because in Wonderland, running at full speed only keeps you in the same place. This perfectly describes startups that chase every AI update and framework just to avoid falling behind. Teams exhaust themselves implementing every new tool while competitors do the same, so nobody actually pulls ahead. We're so busy keeping up that we forget to ask if we're running in the right direction. Three questions cut through the panic: Are you making real progress or just matching competitors? Why are you moving in this direction? What would actually happen if you paused? What Do You Mean? (6 minute read) We all use the same words, but mean completely different things. Your engineer says the feature is "done" (it's not deployed). Your designer says it's "done" (first draft). Your co-founder calls the new strategy "risky" (exciting!). You hear "risky" (we're gonna die). This stuff kills startups. One person's "by end of day" is 5 pm, another's is midnight. One founder's "love" for the company means 60-hour weeks, the other's means sustainable passion. The fix is stupidly simple: just define your terms. What does "shipped" mean at your company? What's "good enough"? ⚡️ Quick Links India's First AI Unicorn Fractal Files for $560 Million IPO: A Strategic Deep Dive into AI's Emerging Market Potential (3 minute read) Fractal Analytics, India's first AI unicorn, is preparing for a $400-500 million IPO that will value the company at $3 billion, highlighting AI's growing importance in emerging markets. Google vet raises $8M for Continua to bring AI agents to group chats (3 minute read) Continua, founded by ex-Google engineer David Petrou, raised $8 million to integrate AI agents in group chats on platforms like SMS and Discord, enhancing collaboration with automated features like reminders and polls. Profound secures $35M to connect brands with one new customer: Superintelligence (2 minute read) Profound secured $35M in Series B funding led by Sequoia Capital to develop a platform that connects brands with AI (superintelligence) as a new customer base. title: TLDR Founders 2025-08-18
text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-18 Tech IPOs are back 💰, Opendoor CEO resigns 👋, usage-based pricing 💲 5 hard-learned lessons about scaling pricing (Sponsor) Remember when you could ship a pricing change in an afternoon? Then you scaled, and suddenly every pricing tweak needs an engineering ticket, a sprint, and a prayer that nothing breaks in production. This blog post reveals key pricing insights by three high-growth companies (DataStax, Fly .io, and Starburst) — and what they did when their legacy billing infrastructure started breaking. Read the blog post: 5 Lessons DataStax, Fly.io, and Starburst Teach Us About the Modern Pricing Stack Want to go even deeper? Metronome's Monetization Operating Model whitepaper is coming soon! Get it before everyone else 📈 Headlines & Trends Tech IPOs are roaring after 'years of Prohibition' (6 minute read) Tech IPOs are booming with Bullish's IPO, backed by Peter Thiel, surging 84% on debut. Figma and Circle also saw significant first-day gains, reigniting interest in tech IPOs after a lull due to previous market conditions. Concerns arise over potential mispricing and the sustainability of these gains, reminiscent of the dot-com era. Opendoor CEO resigns following investor pressure campaign (3 minute read) Opendoor CEO Carrie Wheeler resigned following investor pressure after failing to reassure a turnaround in the company's quarterly earnings report. Hedge fund manager Eric Jackson, a vocal critic, and co-founder Keith Rabois influenced the decision. Opendoor named CTO Shrisha Radhakrishna as interim leader while searching for a new CEO, as the stock rose significantly since its June low. Galaxy Lands $1.4B for Helios Data Center Expansion as CoreWeave Commits to 800 MW (2 minute read) Galaxy secures $1.4 billion to expand its Helios data center in West Texas for AI and HPC capabilities. CoreWeave commits to using all 800 MW of power capacity, with operations starting in 2026 under a long-term deal. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Focus on full funnel SEO not just the top (5 minute read) SEO teams obsess over rankings and traffic, but ignore what happens after someone clicks. This kills SEO's value when clicks decline. The fix is full-funnel SEO that follows the entire customer journey. Activation SEO targets searches like "how to import contacts into [product]" - boring queries that determine if trials convert to paid. Retention SEO mines support tickets for friction points. When frustrated users Google problems before calling support, you'd better rank for those queries or Reddit will. Expansion SEO helps existing customers upgrade by ranking for tier comparisons and referral searches. The real measure of SEO is about being present with helpful answers at every critical moment. Getting Started with Usage-Based Pricing (6 minute read) AI features create a pricing nightmare because some customers barely touch them while others 10x their usage overnight. Kyle Hart built Toast's pricing playbook from $0 to $100M ARR and says customers claim they want usage-based pricing but actually hate the variability. His approach is similar to how you'd test a new feature. First, audit what you have - which features do reps discount most? What are customers actually using? Then, form 2-3 mock pricing pages to test in real sales calls. Slowly build your conviction over time before making the swap. What To Do More (And Less) Of (6 minute read) Most teams know what good product development looks like, but can't figure out why they're not doing it - the answer isn't what to add, it's what to stop. The author studied high-performing teams and found they create space for the right behaviors by killing the wrong ones. They weave context into every discussion instead of jumping straight to solutions and pretending they know everything. They ship meaningful progress weekly instead of getting stuck in analysis paralysis for months. They question year-old commitments instead of blindly executing plans made when everything was different. For your next product planning session, try asking, “What would we need to stop doing to make room for what matters?” ⚒️ Tools & Resources Get your startup found in AI results and Google — and be secure, too (Sponsor) OpenVPN is featuring startups that use their business VPN on the OpenVPN website . It's a great chance to get a link from a high-authority domain, expose your brand to 3M+ monthly visitors, and show that you care about data security. All you need to do: Sign up (and you'll get a 50% discount, too ($6/user) Macaulay (Tool) Macaly turns conversations into working apps and websites, letting you build by simply describing what you want—no coding required. Schematic (Tool) Schematic is a modern monetization platform for SaaS that powers usage-based billing, customer portals, and pricing flows to help teams ship pricing faster and grow revenue. Den (Tool) Den is a multiplayer AI agent builder that centralizes your chats, docs, and agents so humans and AI can collaborate and automate work in one shared space. 🎁 Miscellaneous Product won't end at chat (5 minute read) AI capabilities have run so far ahead of product design that we could stop all model development today and still have years of work left. Everyone's bundling everything into chat apps, but chat sucks for depth, workflow, and longitudinal data. There's a reason AI code generators are IDEs, not text boxes. We'll see the same unbundling that happened with Craigslist - horizontal chat platforms will disaggregate into deeper, purpose-built apps. Any guidebook or professional guide is a candidate for standalone AI apps that go beyond chat. The optimal product has yet to be discovered, but chat won't cut it. Why the Venture Capital Secondary Market Is Booming in 2025 (7 minute read) While everyone waits for the IPO window to reopen, secondaries quietly hit $152 billion in 2024, up 39% year over year. What started as a liquidity hack is now essential infrastructure. LPs tired of waiting can sell their fund stakes. Founders can give employees partial cash-outs without forcing an IPO. GPs spin their best companies into continuation vehicles to hold them longer while still returning cash. The numbers are wild. VC secondaries jumped from 16% of deal activity in 2020 to 29% in 2024. Secondaries aren't a workaround anymore. They're how venture capital actually works when traditional exits take over a decade. SF tech CEO offers buyouts to let workers flee 'extreme' work culture (4 minute read) Cognition's CEO, Scott Wu, offered buyouts to Windsurf employees after acquiring the company, emphasizing a demanding work culture with 80+ hour workweeks. He clarified that those rejecting the buyout must commit to an all-consuming environment. Wu accelerated share vesting schedules and laid off 30 staff, leaving 200 workers to decide on the buyout. ⚡️ Quick Links "Could work" vs. "Weird if it didn't work" (3 minute read) Don't do things that "could work," do things that would be "weird if they didn't work." To Improve LLMs, Coach Them Like Athletes in an Arena (3 minute read) You can dramatically improve any LLM's performance by coaching it like an athlete. Burn. Growth. Valuation (3 minute read) The most important framework in startups is the triangle between burn, growth, and valuation - constantly calculating what you can invest to grow at rates that justify your next raise. text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-20 Databricks valued at $100B 💰, Saronic’s pitch 📣, storytelling for founders 📚 Delve generated $1M in pipeline from TLDR newsletter ads (Sponsor) Delve ran 4 ads across 2 TLDR newsletters and brought in 66 leads, with a meaningful chunk from enterprise. The result: $1M in attributed pipeline and a 52x ROI. This case study breaks down their strategy, results, and example ads. Read the Delve case study. 📈 Headlines & Trends See How Naval-Systems Unicorn Saronic Positions Itself as VCs Ramp Up Defense-Tech Bets (7 minute read) Saronic is positioning itself in the defense-tech sector with autonomous surface vessels, securing $600 million in Series C funding and targeting military contracts amidst a US Navy shipbuilding crisis. Despite some skepticism about its strategy, Saronic focuses on landing smaller contracts and developing prototypes to eventually secure a major program of record. The company's revenue projections for 2025 have jumped to $400 million. It plans to expand beyond the DoD to clients like the Coast Guard and international allies. Databricks says it's valued at over $100 billion in latest funding round (2 minute read) Databricks announced a new funding round that valued the company at over $100 billion, surpassing last year's $62 billion valuation. CEO Ali Ghodsi revealed the round exceeded $1 billion, enabling investment in AI-driven products. Rival Snowflake anticipates $4.5 billion in annual revenue, with a market cap of $65 billion. Don't Ship Your Pitch Deck to Your Website. Here's How to Translate Your Story for Customers (14 minute read) Founders often mistakenly use their investor pitch deck for customer marketing, ignoring that investors and customers have different priorities. Effective early B2B marketing requires tailored messaging that focuses on a specific audience's immediate needs, not the broad vision in a pitch deck. Translate pitch deck messages into clear customer-focused website content by addressing specific pain points, differentiating the product, and building trust with social proof. Ensure communication is relevant, precise, and customer-centric. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How Wispr Flow found PMF through a pivot: Wispr Flow Discussion Forums (9 minute read) Wispr Flow pivoted from a hardware focus to a software-centric voice dictation platform upon realizing there was limited market demand for its initial product. It cut its workforce substantially and launched Wispr Flow, which quickly gained traction with strong user adoption and a high paid conversion rate. The pivot emphasized listening to customer needs and moving swiftly to achieve product-market fit. How to Make a Viral Launch Video (7 minute read) Six founders who went viral share how they turned product launches into millions of views and instant waitlists. The pattern across all winners? Show, don't tell, make viewers say "I can't believe this didn't exist," and treat launches as weapons, not your end goal. Silicon Valley now treats B2B software like entertainment products because everyone's fighting for attention. Early investors literally write checks based on viral tweets - social media has become social currency. 4 AI Workflows to Help You Ship Better Products (8 minute read) This post drops four prompts that actually move the needle for product teams. The first prompt uses computer control models to crawl your actual product and build a feature taxonomy from the user's perspective. The second prompt turns boring product strategies into narratives that sell your vision. The third prompt frames every PRD within your overall strategy so initiatives aren't just random features, but steps toward your vision. The fourth prompt evaluates your plans like a brutally honest CPO who'll call out second-order effects and hidden risks. These aren't just prompts, but a framework for decomposing tasks and bringing expertise to get outputs worth shipping. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Wonda (Tool) Create and edit content by chatting. Lark (Tool) Transform your billing operations with AI agents that handle support tickets, fight chargebacks, and detect fraud 24/7. Overtype (Tool) OverType is a transparent textarea over rendered markdown. 🎁 Miscellaneous Capital and Industry (10 minute read) AI's intelligence layer has become a balance-sheet arms race—capital expenditures in the hundreds of billions. The door is closed for new challengers. Investment returns will concentrate on a handful of at-scale companies. The Case for Staying Put (5 minute read) After three years in one apartment, this author discovered what digital nomads miss: real connection only comes from dealing with a place's boring parts. He spent years hopping between cities, thinking each Instagram-worthy location would make him happy. However, constant movement meant never belonging anywhere. The case for staying put is simple - you can't build meaningful relationships or impact when you're always leaving. The daily annoyances, the negotiations with neighbors, the commitment to local problems - that's what creates actual community. RAG is Dead, Context Engineering is King (8 minute read) Chroma's founder says RAG is dead because it bundles three concepts poorly - the real job is context engineering, figuring out what belongs in the context window for each LLM generation. The best teams now use a two-stage approach: first-stage retrieval (vector search, text search, and metadata) culls 10,000 candidates down to 300, then LLMs re-rank those to the final 20-30. This matters because context rot is real - despite perfect needle-in-haystack marketing, models degrade as you add tokens. The future looks radically different: models will stay in latent space without converting back to text and retrieve continuously during generation instead of once at the start. What we call "memory" is just context engineering with better branding. ⚡️ Quick Links Obsidian's CEO on why productivity tools need community more than AI (37 minute read) While many competitors rush to integrate AI, Obsidian remains hesitant, valuing user privacy and secure data storage. Just One More Prompt (3 minute read) The revelation that you can finally build every idea you've ever had becomes a curse. Is There Any Room Left For The 40-Hour B2B Executive? (3 minute read) B2B executives fall into two camps: the "Cracked," working 70-80 hours to build the next billion-dollar company, or the "Slow Roll," choosing balance but building nothing “extraordinary.” Get our free, 5-minute newsletter read by 200,000 startup founders, entrepreneurs, and CEOs Subscribe Join 360,000 readers for one daily email Privacy Careers Advertise

text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-22 Character.AI explores sale 🧾, Claude Code for enterprise 🧑‍💻, respectful performance management 🏆 Delve generated $1M in pipeline from TLDR newsletter ads (Sponsor) Delve ran 4 ads across 2 TLDR newsletters and brought in 66 leads, with a meaningful chunk from enterprise. The result: $1M in attributed pipeline and a 52x ROI. This case study breaks down their strategy, results, and example ads. Read the Delve case study. 📈 Headlines & Trends An obligation to independence (1 minute read) If you own an independent company and you're not greenlighting weird stuff, you're wasting one of life's true pleasures. Independence isn't just about freedom - it's an obligation to try things no board would ever approve. Public companies worth billions can't do what you can do. They need to justify every move, run it past committees, and worry about quarterly earnings. You can fund the oddball ideas, the "this will probably never work but..." experiments that occasionally catch fire. Very few people get to provide cover for strange attempts without asking permission. So if you're in that position and playing it safe, you're missing the entire point of being independent. Character.AI Explores Sale or New Funding Amid Rising Costs (4 minute read) Character.AI has been holding discussions with potential buyers, banks, and staff over the past few weeks about raising a few hundred million dollars at a valuation of more than $1 billion. The startup's founders left the company last September to join Google, leaving employees to take over ownership. If sold, the buyer would get Character.AI's app and website, which hosts chatbots that resemble anime characters, celebrities, and historical figures. Character.AI makes most of its revenue by charging for premium features with its chatbots. It expects to reach $50 million in annualized revenue by the end of the year. Claude Code and Admin Controls for Enterprise (3 minute read) Anthropic has expanded its enterprise offering with Claude Code, bundled under premium seats for business plans. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Adding Payments to your SaaS Product: A Tactical Playbook (5 minute read) Payments is the holy grail for SaaS companies - your model says you'll make 2% of millions in transaction volume, and suddenly every board meeting becomes about when you're launching it. This playbook shows why most companies shouldn't even try. You need actual money changing hands in your customers' workflows, which sounds obvious until you realize how many analytics platforms are trying to wedge payments into their product. The winners are boring - software for dentists processing copays, auto mechanics sending invoices, and nonprofits taking donations. This guide covers the math on what you'll actually make, realistic adoption rates, and how to expand from basic payment processing into the lucrative stuff like lending. How to build an AI company in a new market (4 minute read) Most AI startups just automate existing workflows in finance or healthcare. Delphi is doing something weirder - building digital versions of people's minds that others can talk to. The founder fed his grandfather's memoir into an AI so he could still ask him for business advice. Now thousands of coaches, consultants, and professors are paying to create their own digital minds. Respectful performance management (4 minute read) Performance management is the thing everyone whispers about but nobody wants to discuss openly. This manager shares the approach she uses when someone's not meeting expectations. The decision is less about whether someone is "good" or "bad," but rather whether their strengths match what the role needs right now. She gives people two projects over four weeks to demonstrate their skills - one tactical, one strategic - and checks in weekly. Sometimes people just need clearer feedback and turn out to be excellent. If, after giving someone a real chance, it's clear they can't do the job, keeping them in that role isn't kindness. They know they're failing, the team's frustrated, and everyone's performance drops. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Chance AI: Visual Reasoning (Tool) Chance AI turns your camera into a visual reasoning engine that not only recognizes images, but instantly explains them with context and insights. Framer Workshop (Tool) Framer Workshop turns text prompts into powerful, customizable website components you can tweak in real-time and deploy instantly without writing code. Stitch (Tool) Stitch by Google Labs turns text prompts and images into UI designs and frontend code, with seamless export to Figma and code files. 🎁 Miscellaneous Convoke raises seed for AI biopharma tool (1 minute read) Convoke raised $8.6 million in seed funding for an AI tool aimed at reducing drug development timelines and costs. This highlights the increasing interest in AI solutions within the pharma industry. You Will Never Have Enough (4 minute read) Constraints in business, like limited time, team size, or budget, force creativity and focus. These limitations should be seen as opportunities to innovate and gain competitive advantages. Embracing constraints can lead to significant achievements with efficient resource use. No, AI Progress is Not Grinding to a Halt (4 minute read) Everyone's declaring GPT-5 a disappointment that proves AI progress is hitting a wall, but they're measuring it wrong. When GPT-4 launched in 2023, it was compared to technology from 2020 - of course, it looked revolutionary. Now GPT-5 is being compared to models released last month. If GPT-4 came out today, it would be dead on arrival. It handled 8,200 tokens versus GPT-5's 400,000, cost 10x more to run, and scored 25/100 on benchmarks where GPT-5 gets 69. On real-world coding tests, GPT-4 solved 2.8% of problems while GPT-5 solved 65%. GPT-4 can handle 5-minute human tasks - GPT-5 can handle tasks that are more than two hours. However, because everyone expected another ChatGPT moment and OpenAI bungled the release, the narrative has solidified in DC that AI is stalling. ⚡️ Quick Links 7 mental models Jeff Bezos uses to run Amazon (3 minute read) You won't become a billionaire from knowing these, but you might make better decisions. Someone please make channel sales easier (2 minute read) Channel sales is where 75% of all tech gets sold - that's $3.9 trillion flowing through 600,000 partner companies - yet it's a complete nightmare to manage. Product Fidelity (2 minute read) This founder noticed his apps still take 2-3 months to build, same as before AI tools - the initial build is faster, but the "missing details" eat up all the saved time. text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-25 AI bubble 💭, Anthropic advisory board 👥, AI lead investors 💰 📈 Headlines & Trends Anthropic Launches AI Advisory Board (4 minute read) Anthropic has formed a Higher Education Advisory Board, chaired by former Yale president Rick Levin, to guide Claude's development for teaching and research. The drop came shortly after the release of MIT research showing 95% of organizations get no return on AI investments and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman suggesting that AI investment was in a bubble. As Funding To AI Startups Increases And Concentrates, Which Investors Have Led? (5 minute read) AI companies secured $118 billion by mid-August, with funding increasingly concentrated among eight firms, including OpenAI's $40 billion raise. SoftBank, Greenoaks, and prominent corporate backers like Meta and Google are driving the largest investments. U.S. venture industry dry powder is declining, reflecting the intense competition and high valuations in the AI sector. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The perfect Product Pages for SaaS founders (4 minute read) Your homepage alone stops working once your product does more than two things well - that's when you need dedicated product pages. These pages sit under 'Product' or 'Solutions' in your nav bar and come in three flavors: feature pages (how specific features work), use-case pages (how to accomplish specific goals), and customer-segment pages (tailored for specific audiences like "for Marketing Teams"). The most common structure includes a hero, social proof, benefits and features combo, how it works section, case studies, additional resources, FAQ, and CTA. Strategy & Urgency (4 minute read) None of the popular prioritization frameworks (RICE, ICE, or MoSCoW) explicitly address how time affects value, which is bizarre because timing is everything in strategy - if you're racing to be first to 30% market share to "tip" the market, every month matters massively, but if you're adding upsell features, missing this year just means trying next year. We've been trained to see urgency as anti-strategic, associating it with desperate salespeople demanding features or last-ditch quarterly saves, when actually many companies complaining about "no strategy" are trapped in regrettable reactive urgency because the ship sailed years ago. From Founders To Funders: Lessons On Scaling Deep Tech Startups (6 minute read) Jesse Coors-Blankenship and Gregg Hill, founders turned VCs, focus on scaling deep tech companies like SandboxAQ by solving real-world problems and securing smart capital. They emphasize the importance of picking investors who understand the technology and leveraging anchor investors to attract a strong syndicate. Effective scaling requires educating the market on innovations, aligning vision with execution, and demonstrating progress through clear milestones. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Synch (Tool) Automates Salesforce updates from emails and calls so sales reps never touch CRM again. Roundtable (Tool) Stop bots and fraud without slowing real users. The Prompting Company (Tool) Customers now get recommendations from AI. Be in those answers. 🎁 Miscellaneous Slow Ventures cuts first check from $60M creator fund into woodworking founder (4 minute read) Slow Ventures' Creator Fund, aimed at supporting creators in building sustainable businesses, invested $2 million in Jonathan Katz-Moses, a woodworking content creator with a significant online following. This is the first investment from the fund since its launch. The investment will help Katz-Moses expand his tool business, product development, and educational content on YouTube. We should all be doing less (4 minute read) What would actually happen if you stopped trying to be productive for just one moment? This writer's therapist asked her that question, and she became furious because the answer terrified her. She'd turned creativity from joy into a chain around her neck, clawing for likes and relevance, measuring worth entirely by output. GitHub just got less independent at Microsoft after CEO resignation (3 minute read) GitHub CEO Thomas Dohmke has resigned. The company's leadership will now report directly to Microsoft's CoreAI team, led by Jay Parikh. GitHub, acquired by Microsoft for $7.5 billion in 2018, will integrate further with Microsoft's AI platform efforts without appointing a new CEO. Dohmke plans to guide the transition until the end of 2025 before pursuing new opportunities. ⚡️ Quick Links Winner-Take-All Has Taken Over Venture (3 minute read) Seed rounds in the 99th percentile are now hitting $161 million valuations - that's not a typo, and it's 123% higher than even the 95th percentile. Smart Batching (2 minute read) Jack Dorsey ran both Twitter and Square simultaneously using one weird trick - he themed his days. text: TLDR Founders 2025-08-27 Klarna IPO 💰, Claude for Code 💻, guide to social media use 📚 The Monetization Operating Model: How to think about pricing, billing, and growth in the AI era (Sponsor) Many startups are struggling to find a sustainable pricing model for AI-powered software. Grab your free copy 📈 Headlines & Trends AI's labor impact, and how to not lose our minds (3 minute read) Stanford researchers analyzed payroll data from 25 million American workers and found AI is already replacing early-career workers in specific fields. Software developers aged 22-25 have seen 6% job losses since ChatGPT launched, while developers over 30 gained 9% more jobs in the same period. Customer service shows the same pattern - young workers losing jobs, older workers gaining them. The data suggests AI isn't coming for all jobs or even most jobs, but it's systematically eliminating the entry-level positions in fields where experience matters less and tasks can be automated. Klarna to seek valuation of up to $14 billion in IPO next month (2 minute read) Klarna plans to go public in the U.S. next month, seeking a valuation of $13-$14 billion after pausing its IPO in April. The fintech company aims to raise nearly $1 billion, with shares priced at $34-$36. This reflects a significant drop from its 2021 valuation target of $50 billion. xAI has Open-sourced Grok 2.5 (1 minute read) xAI open-sourced the model weights for Grok 2.5, its leading model from last year, on Hugging Face. Elon Musk noted that Grok 3 is expected to be open-sourced in about six months. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics 5 Interesting Learnings From Google Cloud at $54 Billion in ARR (3 minute read) Google Cloud's ARR reached $54 billion, with a 31.7% growth driven by AI advancements. Key drivers include increased infrastructure demand, new revenue from Gemini AI models, and competitive AI-native positioning. Google also reported 38% backlog growth, a 35x increase in Gemini customer adoption, and larger deal sizes, indicating robust enterprise adoption and confidence in sustained growth. Preventing Churn (4 minute read) Many tech companies enforce long-term contracts to mask churn by preventing dissatisfied customers from leaving. This tactic hides dissatisfaction rather than addressing root problems, leading to customer resentment and lost future revenue. Instead, companies should prioritize customer satisfaction and renewals through excellent product and service, fostering loyalty without forced commitments. The Founder's Guide to Effective Social Media Use (4 minute read) An investor says he won't take meetings from founders who don't post on X because he needs to see how they think before deciding if they're worth his time. Without warm referrals, investors have no way to verify you're legitimate, so they check your social presence to see how you think and whether you're making progress. The strategy is to post 5-10 times daily about non-obvious insights, commenting early on industry posts to get noticed, and relaunching your product announcement every two weeks. The point isn't to go viral, but to build a consistent record showing you can ship and think clearly over time. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Claude for Chrome (Tool) Claude has released a new browser extension for Chrome. Inconvo (Tool) Build ChatGPT-style analytics into your product. Rubbrband (Tool) Create movies with AI. 🎁 Miscellaneous Zero-Waste GTM (3 minute read) Public SaaS companies are spending between $2 and $8 to acquire $1 of revenue. Most of this money goes to the same place: trying to get leads into the top of the funnel. Monday.com learned this lesson painfully when its stock dropped 30% in a single day after admitting organic search had dried up. Without a cheap pipeline, you grow slower or pay more. Now, everyone's betting AI will save them, but AI SDRs are producing the same mediocre results as human SDRs, only faster and in greater volume. The math suggests something different: use AI to cut your sales team in half while keeping revenue flat, then pour those savings into finding new customers. Rillet raises $70M Series B from Andreessen Horowitz and ICONIQ (5 minute read) Rillet raised $70M in a Series B funding round led by Andreessen Horowitz and ICONIQ, bringing its total funding to over $100M in less than a year. The AI-native ERP platform, designed for modern finance teams, is rapidly growing with over 200 customers, including strategic partnerships with top accounting firms. Rillet aims to redefine enterprise accounting by offering faster insights, leaner teams, and improved decision-making through native integrations and AI-powered financial systems. To Learn a New Market, Start by Building Your Product (5 minute read) Othman Laraki left Twitter to build a $250 genetic test because the one that could have saved his grandmother cost $4,000. He knew nothing about healthcare but figured the technology was simple enough. After two years of talking to doctors and insurance companies, he learned that healthcare doesn't work like normal markets - even if you make something 90% cheaper, insurance companies can't buy more of it from just you because doctors want tests from specific labs, so they restrict the whole category instead. The cheap test was the wrong product anyway. Big employers who actually pay for healthcare don't want to buy individual tests, they want to cut their cancer costs by millions. So Laraki built a virtual cancer clinic instead, which is now worth a billion dollars. Most technical people think everyone before them was incompetent, but usually those people were just responding to incentives you don't understand yet. ⚡️ Quick Links The Illusion of Explanatory Depth (2 minute read) Ask someone to explain how a toilet works and watch them stumble. The web design platform, popular among Y Combinator startups and major clients like Scale AI, expects $100 million in ARR next year. Framer offers unique features compared to competitors like Figma, including A/B testing and enterprise-level security. The Top 100 [Gen AI] Consumer Apps (10 minute read) The Gen AI ecosystem is stabilizing. There were only 11 new names on Andreessen Horowitz's most recent Top 100 Gen AI Consumer Apps list, compared to 17 newcomers in the March ranking. There were significantly more mobile newcomers as app stores have cracked down on ChatGPT copycats, opening up room for more original apps. ChatGPT is still the leading general assistant, but Google, Grok, and Meta are closing the gap. More on the top Gen AI consumer apps is available in the article. Nvidia reports record sales as the AI boom continues (2 minute read) Nvidia, the world's most valuable company, reported $46.7 billion in revenue this quarter, a 56% increase compared to the same period last year. AI-dominated data center business largely fuelled that growth. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Stop Selling a SaaS Solution (4 minute read) Companies keep telling founders to stop selling features and start selling solutions, then outcomes, then transformations, but they're all missing what actually matters - buyers don't trust that software alone will get them results. Don't make your tool the hero of the story and make your team the hero instead. Instead of saying "our magical tool solves your problem," say "we are the team that will solve your problem, and this tool is our weapon." SEO is a waste of time if it doesn't focus on scale (4 minute read) It's easy to create SEO busywork that feels productive - fixing meta tags on pages nobody visits, chasing keywords with zero searches, updating old blog posts - but these won't change anything. What works is fixing problems across thousands of pages at once: repairing broken links everywhere, updating all your category templates, and rebuilding how your site connects pages. Start with pages already getting traffic to prove you can improve them, then fix the bigger structural problems. The scariest framework to evaluate your team (4 minute read) Managers lie to themselves about their teams - they convince themselves someone is "pretty good" to avoid replacing them, or haven't updated their opinion since that person was great two years ago. The solution is plotting everyone on a 2x2 matrix of performance (Do they deliver on time and understand business impact? Can they work without creating friction?) versus potential (Can they think strategically and hold their own with senior leaders? Do people follow them?). ⚒️ Tools & Resources How ThreatSpike added up to $75K in pipeline per campaign with TLDR Newsletter ads (Sponsor) ThreatSpike ditched underperforming channels like Google and LinkedIn for TLDR. The results? Consistent $25K to $75K in pipeline per campaign High-intent leads from enterprise companies with 30K+ employees Direct reach to decision-makers actively looking for solutions We break down ThreatSpike's exact strategy, share their results, and reveal the ads that drove performance. Read the ThreatSpike case study. Mocke (Tool) Know your reply rate in 1 minute without launching. Wireframer AI (Tool) Wireframer AI generates clean, responsive website layouts from a simple prompt, prioritizing hierarchy and flow while leaving styling up to you. Japan's First Residency Program "AG1" (4 minute read) AG1 is Japan's first full-scale residency program for early-stage startup founders. Offering 10 weeks of free housing and workspace in Tokyo, the program aims to overcome structural barriers for global talent and domestic entrepreneurs. It will provide mentorship and support for product development and market strategy, targeting startups in the AI and consumer technology sectors. Applications are open worldwide. 🎁 Miscellaneous Chickens Talking to Ducks (4 minute read) There's a Cantonese saying about chickens talking to ducks - both speaking their language, both making sense, neither understanding the other. When the author had breast cancer, her husband kept saying "you'll be fine" while working through the night closing his company sale, which felt like dismissal until she realized he wasn't reassuring her - he was reassuring himself. This same dynamic happens constantly between founders and investors, founders and employees, founders and spouses - each person speaking their own language of fear or control, thinking they're communicating when they're actually talking past each other. Her husband, watching both her parents die of cancer, needed to believe she'd be fine to function. She needed him to acknowledge her fear to feel heard. She finally said, "I know statistically I'll be fine, but right now I need you to hear that I'm scared" - and everything changed. Mass Intelligence (3 minute read) A billion people now use AI regularly, but until recently, only 7% of paying ChatGPT users even knew to choose the best models from the menu. The cost of running AI has collapsed - GPT-5 nano costs 14 cents per million tokens versus $50 for the original GPT-4. Every institution built for scarce, expensive intelligence now has to figure out what happens when a billion people have tools that outperform humans at intellectual tasks. What Are The Top 10 Things To Put in a Series A Deck? (3 minute read) A successful Series A pitch deck should include a compelling one-slide summary, clear problem-solution articulation, and key traction metrics. Highlight the market opportunity and explain why your team and product are uniquely positioned to succeed. Ensure the financial plan is realistic, make a clear funding ask, and present a polished, visually appealing deck sent to investors in advance. ⚡️ Quick Links Overnight Success (1 minute read) Behind every "instant" success is a decade of almost dying. The key isn't just defining values but turning them into visible behaviors - celebrate when someone lives them, call out when someone doesn't - because as the military says, doing something below standard and saying nothing sets a new standard. Culture drifts about every six months during rapid growth, so plan regular resets at off-sites where you reconnect on what values look like in practice. Junior Talent Dilemma (4 minute read) AI is increasingly replacing junior roles, giving rise to strategic risks for companies.
