Eliminating juniors also forfeits potential AI advancements driven by their creativity and adaptability. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Rowflow (Tool) Make your Google form talk. Riveter (Tool) Thousands of AI search agents running in parallel. Fulcrum Research (Tool) Debugging for AI Agents. 🎁 Miscellaneous How Costco Won In Japan (4 minute read) People waited eight hours to enter Costco's new Okinawa store - five to park, three more to get inside - in a country where Walmart, Tesco, and Carrefour all failed and fled. Costco succeeded by adapting everything: building up instead of out with escalator ramps for carts because land costs too much, selling 30% fresh food, including restaurant-quality sushi at $20 for 48 pieces, and paying 60% above minimum wage, which forced local shops to match or close. The real genius was understanding Japanese shopping culture - customers split bulk purchases with friends and neighbors, giving Costco 162,000 members per warehouse versus 112,000 in America, plus it let corner stores resell Kirkland products during COVID at 20% markups because spreading the brand mattered more than controlling distribution when membership fees generate half your profit. Surviving COVID, PMMC Fit, & Scaling To $200M ARR (4 minute read) Product-market fit isn't enough - you need PMMC fit, which means having a product, market, profitable model, and channels that actually work. Super.com nearly died during COVID when refunds exceeded bookings. It burned money on TikTok ads, assuming viral content drove bookings, but people searching "hotel tonight" use Google, not social media. The same blindness almost killed its product - it thought lower-income households wanted cheaper vacations. After making 200 employees cash paychecks at payday lenders and shops at Walmart, it learned these customers needed everyday financial help, not travel deals. It pivoted from discount hotels to a membership program for credit building, grocery cashback, and basic financial services - now it's at $200 million revenue. Flounder Mode (3 minute read) Kevin Kelly has built clocks that tick for 10,000 years, co-founded WIRED, wrote the "1,000 true fans" essay that launched the creator economy, and advised Spielberg on Minority Report - but he's never started a company and doesn't want to. He works "Hollywood style," jumping between projects that interest him, which Silicon Valley sees as unserious compared to grinding toward unicorn status. While tech culture fetishizes founders who get scurvy from eating only ramen or "torture people into greatness," Kelly just seems happy, following interests not for money or dominance but because the well of curiosity is bottomless. The real question isn't whether you can be great without suffering - it's why we insist greatness requires misery. ⚡️ Quick Links Facing Fear (2 minute read) Every company has a moment where the CEO realizes they're completely screwed and has to tell investors and employees who definitely don't want to hear it. Circle is the standout performer, with shares up over 5x since its IPO, sparking interest in public offerings from other crypto and tech companies. How To Tell Good Advice From Bad Ones (3 minute read) The universe operates on simple laws, and so does advice. First Law: Good advice is simple. We complicate basic truths to feel superior, yet "show up consistently" remains true whether you accept it or not. Second Law: Good advice is boring. Bad advice promises overnight transformation. Good advice says show up at the dojo every Thursday for years until one day you're doing handstand pushups without thinking. Third Law: Good advice is constructive. It points toward futures worth creating instead of listing what to avoid. These laws work like gravity - they don't care if you find them pedestrian. Stuff costs money (6 minute read) Open source software companies keep learning the same lesson. You start by building open source tools for the community, putting everything on GitHub, and promising it'll stay free. You build in public, create Discord channels, and respond to every issue. Then the bills come due - servers, salaries, and the basic costs of existing as a company. So you launch a paid tier, swearing it's just for a tiny fraction of users. Your community gets angry. The whole industry acts surprised when free software eventually costs money, even though software is expensive to build and maintain. Nobody expects free movie tickets just because trailers are free, but somehow charging for software after giving away the core version makes you a sellout. ⚡️ Quick Links Smarter Decisions [10-10-10 Rule] (2 minute read) Before any decision, ask yourself how you'll feel about it in 10 minutes, 10 months, and 10 years. Your Company Isn't You (1 minute read) As founders, we tend to see our startups as extensions of our identity. Go faster (2 minute read) The universe operates on simple laws, and in Silicon Valley, the simplest law is velocity. Snapchat+ subscriptions generate over $700 million annually, marking a significant direct revenue source. Snap is also developing AR glasses, aiming to revolutionize human-centered computing. Bootstrapping vs Fundraising (6 minute read) Bootstrapping allows founders to maintain control and minimize equity dilution using personal funds, but it poses financial risks and may limit growth. Raising venture capital provides cash, expertise, and validation but involves equity dilution and loss of control. With a hybrid approach, companies can bootstrap initially to prove their concept, then seek external capital for accelerated growth. 996 Is Real, and It's Becoming Common (7 minute read) Ramp's data shows the "996" workweek, a 9 a.m. to 9 p.m., six-day-a-week schedule, is becoming prevalent in San Francisco, affecting multiple sectors beyond just tech. This shift creates intense competition, demanding faster product development and heightened responsiveness, especially for B2B companies. Leaders must decide whether to adopt 996, focus on efficiency, target different markets, or innovate hybrid models to maintain a competitive edge. ⚒️ Tools & Resources How SCIM works: the (minimally) technical guide (Sponsor) SCIM lets identity providers like Okta communicate user changes to your B2B app. This developer guide covers the real-world gotchas. Want to add SCIM to your app without building it? Talk to Tesseral Lightbox (Tool) Cursor for content creation. Arcimus (Tool) Scan thousands of signals to surface the people who actually need you. Shellcast (Tool) Stream your vibe coding. 🎁 Miscellaneous Why retention is so hard for new tech products (5 minute read) You might think fixing retention at a startup works like it does at big companies - run A/B tests, optimize onboarding, and send more notifications. However, that's attacking the wrong problem entirely. The truth is that bad retention means you built something people don't want, and no amount of optimization changes that. If your day-one retention is 10% instead of 40%, you need a complete pivot - redesign the home screen, change the core loop, and target different users. The only path to good retention is building something people already need to use frequently, then making it genuinely better than what exists. Aven raises $110M Series E to expand homeowner credit platform (3 minute read) Aven secured a $110M Series E round at a $2.2B valuation to cut consumer credit costs, focusing initially on HELOC-backed credit cards. It has saved customers over $215M in interest and expanded credit lines to $3B. Aven plans to scale mortgage refinancing to provide a comprehensive financial platform for American homeowners. Usage Based Models: Why They Suck as a Customer (4 minute read) Software companies sold us on usage-based pricing with a simple promise - pay only for what you use. In practice, you buy a year's worth of cloud credits for $100,000, use only $50,000, then discover those unused credits disappear unless you spend another $100,000 next year. One founder called it "infrastructure hostage-taking" after going through exactly this. You ask to pay less since you're using less, they say no, and your unused credits become leverage for them to force a renewal. ⚡️ Quick Links The AI Agent Reality Check: Why Managing Always-On AI Workers Is Your Next Big Challenge (6 minute read) Managing 24/7 AI agents presents significant challenges despite their potential. Klarna targets U.S. growth, rivaling credit cards with its "pay-in-4" plan as regulatory scrutiny of buy-now-pay-later services increases. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How AI Is Rewriting Inbound Conversion (4 minute read) Inbound conversion used to mean SDRs racing to respond within minutes, but AI agents are proving to be the perfect use case - they qualify instantly, respond 24/7, and buyers actually prefer them to pushy salespeople. Rho cut cost-per-meeting from $570 to $45, Demandbase booked 4x more meetings, and Hubspot's PLG motion finally converted browsers into buyers - all by replacing human SDRs with AI agents. $-Impact of Zero-Waste GTM (3 minute read) Zero-Waste GTM means eliminating all non-essential work from your go-to-market teams using AI, and some companies are claiming 90% revenue lifts without adding headcount - here's how the math actually works. When AI handles the 70% of tasks that aren't selling (data entry, follow-ups, and research), your best reps can take on twice as many deals, and here's where it gets interesting: those top performers close 10% better, get 10% higher prices, and move 10% faster - multiply those together and you get 1.1 x 1.1 x 1.1 = roughly 1.33x improvement per deal, times twice the deals, which equals nearly double the revenue. OpenAI's Head of Product Reveals the 15 AI Distribution Plays That Build Real Moats (2 minute read) Distribution determines survival in AI because features replicate in weeks while distribution compounds over years - Perplexity won through shareable outputs, Clay invented the job category "GTM Engineer," and Cluely turned outrage into free marketing. The framework splits into three paths that actually work: bundling into existing platforms where users already live, embedding invisibly at the moment of workflow intent, or creating unfair advantages competitors won't copy like provocative narratives. The 15 tactics range from designing outputs people want to share to pricing that grows revenue faster than costs - master three to five of these, and you will survive when GPT-5 makes everyone's features identical overnight. ⚒️ Tools & Resources AI-native analytics for founders (Sponsor) Get insights from your data regardless of data maturity or team size. Fabi.ai combines AI, SQL, and Python so you can build data apps, create workflows, and easily explore your data. Get started in 5 minutes & use code FOUNDERS for 50% off your first month. Retool Agents (Tool) Retool Agents lets you turn your APIs and business data into autonomous AI systems that execute workflows with human-like reasoning and decision-making. 🎁 Miscellaneous Why language models hallucinate (3 minute read) OpenAI has published a paper explaining why AI models make up facts. It comes down to how we test them - models get points for guessing right but zero for saying "I don't know," so they learn to confidently invent answers rather than admit uncertainty. If a model doesn't know your birthday and guesses "September 10," it has a 1-in-365 chance of scoring, but admitting ignorance guarantees failure, which trains even advanced models like GPT-5 to hallucinate rather than be honest. How to Build Mental Strength (4 minute read) Clear thinking requires four strengths that most people lack: self-accountability (taking responsibility instead of making excuses), self-knowledge (knowing where you're actually competent versus where you're just confident), self-control (managing impulses so you're not sabotaging your long-term goals), and self-confidence (the balance between earned competence and aspiration). These aren't abstract ideals, but muscles you train daily. Positive habits become automatic defaults that compound over time, while the right role models show you what's possible without you needing to compete with them directly. Build Places, Not Products (4 minute read) Software design is beginning to look like conference rooms under fluorescent lights - functional but soulless - because teams optimize for efficiency over atmosphere. When AI can generate competent interfaces in seconds, one defensible position is creating an atmosphere that makes people choose your product, not because it works (everything works), but because it feels like somewhere they want to be. ⚡️ Quick Links You Grow By Trying Stuff (1 minute read) Growth follows the laws of experimentation, not templates - you throw things at customers, observe what sticks, then amplify the signal. Why We Buy: The Psychology of Incentivization (3 minute read) You're at a gas station planning to spend $30 on fuel when you spot a sign offering a $10 gift card if you spend $45. Next thing you know, you're filling the tank completely and feeling like you won. B.F. Skinner discovered why we fall for this when he gave rats a lever that sometimes dispensed food - the unpredictable rewards made them press it obsessively because dopamine spikes at anticipation, not just the reward itself. Sephora tosses in free samples knowing you'll come back for full sizes, Asana randomly shows unicorns flying across your screen when you complete tasks, and smart marketers reveal surprise bonuses mid-launch to tip fence-sitters into buying. The right incentive at the right moment turns "maybe" into "yes" and keeps people coming back for more. Sales KPIs for Startups (4 minute read) Sales KPIs for startups should link directly to revenue, focusing on metrics like lead conversion through the sales funnel. Key distinctions include marketing-qualified leads (MQLs), who show interest but aren't ready to buy, and sales-qualified leads (SQLs), who are prepared to purchase. The author quit smoking by declaring himself a non-smoker first, then acting accordingly, and now deliberately cultivates 50+ identities from "publishes imperfect art" to "returns shopping carts" - each one chosen rather than accidentally accumulated through habit. For startups, this means replacing goals with identity: instead of trying to reach product-market fit, become "the type of founder who talks to customers daily"; and instead of trying to raise funding, become "the type of CEO who builds inevitable companies" - because once you define who you are, the actions follow automatically. AI Will Not Make You Rich (6 minute read) Generative AI won't create the investor fortunes that personal computers did - it's more like shipping containerization in 1956, which revolutionized global trade but made almost nobody rich. Unlike the PC revolution that surprised everyone and gave startups time to build moats before IBM noticed, AI faces immediate competition from tech giants who control the models and limit experimentation. The real money won't be in AI companies themselves but downstream in businesses that use AI to cut costs and expand into new markets, though even these will face brutal competition. Most of the $1-7 trillion in value AI creates will flow to consumers through cheaper services, not investors. RIP Digital Marketing To Humans (4 minute read) AI agents are replacing humans in digital marketing by autonomously making purchasing decisions using structured data. title: TLDR Founders 2025-09-22
text: TLDR Founders 2025-09-22 H-1B restrictions 🇺🇸, Navan files to IPO 📚, robots in 2025 🤖 📈 Headlines & Trends Restriction on entry of certain nonimmigrant workers (2 minute read) A new proclamation blocks most foreign tech workers from entering the US on H-1B visas unless their employers pay a $100,000 surcharge per petition. The move responds to claims that companies have replaced Americans with cheaper labor, often forcing laid-off staff to train their replacements. Exceptions exist for “national interest” cases, but the order raises labor costs sharply and signals a push to reserve entry-level IT jobs for domestic graduates. What robots can (and can't) do in 2025 (2 minute read) Robots still struggle with tasks like picking up objects due to the challenges in machine perception and manipulation. Achieving breakthroughs akin to a "ChatGPT moment" in robotics requires blending advanced AI models with traditional engineering. The future of robotics depends on combining simulation data with real-world applications to overcome current limitations. Navan Files to IPO (5 minute read) Navan is heading to the public markets with $688M ARR, 30% growth, and gross margins up to 73% after major AI-driven efficiency gains. Still, net revenue retention barely clears 110%, free cash flow margins sit at -5%, and its Rule of 40 score is just 25%, near the bottom quartile. Investors will judge whether margin progress and AI-powered support can offset middling growth and a lower-revenue scale compared to top SaaS peers. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Cluely Thesis on Virality and Hype (3 minute read) Viral content will spread if it's seen by a large enough audience and deserves attention. The true value of going viral lies in gaining user interest, especially post-PMF, rather than driving immediate conversions. How to find the perfect name (9 minute read) David Placek and his team at Lexicon Branding have named nearly 4,000 brands, including Swiffer, Sonos, and Pentium, by using a structured naming process. In today's competitive and AI-driven market, an effective name can create trust, communicate originality, and be globally accessible. The Lexicon Framework advises generating thousands of names, considering sound symbolism, and emphasizing trust and uniqueness over descriptiveness. How to get cited in AI answers (3 minute read) Did you know AI models cite brands mentioned at industry conferences? This SEO expert discovered it after ChatGPT kept recommending his competitors despite his better domain authority and more backlinks. Turns out LLMs scan transcribed conference talks, YouTube presentations, and LinkedIn recaps - places his competitors were getting namedropped while he bought Reddit mentions. The models also track user corrections. A pizza shop vanished from AI suggestions after enough people typed "don't show me that again" in follow-up prompts. His competitors weren't trying to game AI at all. They were presenting case studies at conferences, getting discussed in customer Slack channels, and showing up in YouTube tutorials. These mentions trained the AI to trust them. ⚒️ Tools & Resources How Snowflake built its Monetization Operating Model (Sponsor) Who really owns pricing? At Snowflake, the answer lies in a  Monetization Operating Model that connects product strategy to execution. Join the upcoming webinar with Metronome and Snowflake to learn how Snowflake designs pricing for predictability, visibility, and control in usage-based and AI-driven models. Register here. Spine (Tool) Iterative deep research reports you can actually use. Atla (Tool) Find and fix AI agent failures. Dart (Tool) Plan projects by chatting with Dart, then hand tasks to AI agents to get things done. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Self Is The Platform (5 minute read) AI has inverted the fundamental equation of technology - individuals can now rival entire teams, and private tools are beating public platforms. One person with ChatGPT and Cursor can ship what used to require 50 people. The last tech boom was about coordinating people at scale, but AI flipped it - personal leverage beats social leverage now. These aren't social networks, they're supertools that multiply individual capacity without needing anyone else. The individual is now the base unit of scale. The IPO Market Is Opening Up. These 14 Companies Could Be Next (12 minute read) Crunchbase's predictive tools spotlight 14 companies across sectors like fintech, AI, and consumer goods as probable IPO candidates. Notables include Stripe, with its $91.5 billion valuation, and Databricks, which raised a $10 billion round in 2024. Emerging sectors like cybersecurity and space tech spotlight firms like Ledger, likely to IPO due to its crypto-wallet prominence, and Sierra Space, backed by $3.4 billion in contracts. How to Use AI Without Becoming Stupid (6 minute read) We trust AI to draft emails, schedule calls, even write code, but the real danger isn't mistakes — it's letting it choose for us. A simple rule has quietly emerged across classrooms, trading desks, and dev teams: never give up your value judgments. The paradox is that automation works best when it leaves humans more room to decide. ⚡️ Quick Links Your Body in Founder Mode (2 minute read) A look at what actually happens to your body when you live founder mode, hustle culture, and 996. When Competitors Raise Large Rounds (4 minute read) Entrepreneurs should remain focused on customers, especially when competitors raise large funding rounds. The $20/Month Software Revolution (2 minute read) AI coding tools like Cursor and Lovable enable rapid, intuitive application development for a $20 subscription, disrupting traditional software workflows. text: TLDR Founders 2025-09-24 Greptile Series A 💰, startup salaries 💼, VCBench 📚 How Snowflake is adapting usage-based pricing for the AI era (Sponsor) Snowflake's usage-based pricing has been one key driver of its market leadership. On the agenda: Reducing friction and accelerating pricing decisions Applying rigor and experimentation to your monetization model ﻿﻿Lessons from Snowflake on designing pricing for predictability, visibility, and control Join live: How Snowflake built its Monetization Operating Model 📈 Headlines & Trends Startup Salaries, Equity, and Their Impact on Employee Retention (8 minute read) Startup pay shifted again this year. Salaries are up about 5%, with product and engineering around $189k, while equity grants are roughly 26% below pre-2022, and only 32% of vested in-the-money options got exercised. Early hires still get about 1.5% and employee five near 0.3%. Turnover fell 31% yet churn stays high. Retention improves when pay is competitive, refreshes hit year three to four, and bands are transparent. VCBench: Benchmarking LLMs in Venture Capital (9 minute read) Startup investing has long been guesswork, with even top VCs picking winners less than 6% of the time. VCBench is trying to change that: a dataset of 9,000 anonymized founder profiles built to test whether models can predict success better than humans. After scrubbing names, dates, and company info to prevent leakage, researchers ran nine LLMs. GPT-4o delivered 3.2× baseline precision, edging past tier-1 firms. Greptile Series A and Greptile v3 (5 minute read) Greptile raised $25M Series A led by Benchmark Capital to advance its AI code review agent, Greptile v3, which addresses scale issues in code validation. The revamped agent detects 3x more critical bugs than its predecessor and has reviewed over 500M lines of code for top firms, preventing 180,000+ bugs. Greptile integrates with tools like Jira and Notion for context-aware feedback and learns team practices from PR comments to enhance code reviews. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How To Build Mental Strength (5 minute read) Mental strength goes hand in hand with clear thinking. It's what lets you override your defaults instead of running on autopilot. You build it through four trainable muscles: accountability, knowledge, control, and confidence. Add high standards, rituals, and the right role models, and those muscles compound into habits. How to Launch Your Startup Out of Stealth, from Figma's First Marketer (15 minute read) Successful startup launches require strategic preparation, emphasizing feature readiness and clear positioning. Set a specific launch date to avoid perpetual delays and create artifacts like a website, announcement post, and social post to sharpen messaging and decisions. Focus on existing distribution channels, utilize personal networks for amplification, and view the launch as a starting point for growth rather than a final goal. Why Customer Support Shouldn't Handle Your Product Feedback (11 minute read) Customer support teams lack the skills and tools necessary to gather actionable product feedback, often resulting in valuable insights getting lost. Aligning customer support's incentives with quick ticket resolution rather than in-depth feedback analysis contributes to this issue. Companies should implement dedicated feedback systems to ensure customers feel heard and product teams receive quality insights, ultimately leading to improved product development and customer satisfaction. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Firecrawl (Tool) Firecrawl is an open-source tool that converts entire websites into clean, LLM-ready markdown or structured data with a single API call for developers. Skillsync (Tool) Skillsync uncovers hidden expertise and successful patterns in your codebase to help teams scale what works. ReachLLM (Tool) ReachLLM audits AI search results for your brand and rivals, uncovering high-intent prompts and brand mentions so you can win the AI answer box. 🎁 Miscellaneous Toward Computational Taste: LLMs, Aesthetics & Judgment (9 minute read) LLMs are being used to model and optimize taste in diverse fields through personalized systems like Taste Engines, Aesthetic LLMs, and Taste Tribes. Methods like LoRe and models like TAPO enable LLMs to adapt to individual aesthetic preferences, potentially revolutionizing recommendation engines and social networks. This shift towards computational taste signifies a new era where machines not only reflect but also shape human preferences, offering both cultural and economic opportunities. Narrative Cycle Entry (4 minute read) Every technology story has a rhythm. First comes obscurity, when only a few people care, and founders have to explain everything from scratch. Then comes conviction, as believers pile in and copycats multiply. Next is euphoria, when hype peaks and differentiation gets hardest. Finally comes the crash, when the same idea is written off until a few survivors prove it real again. Knowing where you enter shapes whether you're explaining an unproven idea, competing in a crowded field, or trying to rebuild belief after the hype has collapsed. The One-Person, Billion-Dollar Power Development Company (10 minute read) Power development has always been a slog: years of permits, consultants, site visits, and spreadsheets that eat entire teams. That's about to change. With new data systems, permitting software, robotics, and orchestration tools, one person could realistically manage hundreds of megawatts of projects and generate $100M in fees. This piece explores how an industry known for red tape might collapse into a one-person business model. ⚡️ Quick Links The smart and leveraged are getting richer (2 minute read) Naval breaks down why wealth is concentrating faster than ever: leverage. The Google Effect (3 minute read) Our brains don't bother storing facts we know we can Google later, which means your buyers won't remember your product details either. Stripe in talks to buy back stock from investors (2 minute read) Stripe plans to repurchase shares from VCs at a $106.7 billion valuation, offering liquidity without going public. The difference illustrates two paths to massive value - hypergrowth or steady compounding over decades. OpenAI is growing 400% at $1 billion revenue, while Atlassian grew 30% at that scale, yet Snowflake's 124% growth path built a $75 billion company versus Atlassian's $42 billion. The lesson is that reaching $1 billion revenue faster creates significantly more value due to time value of money - hypergrowth beats slow and steady even if you eventually hit the same revenue, though most companies can't optimize growth, margin, and retention simultaneously. The shift is radical - disputes that took 3 hours now take 3 seconds, AI does 100% of quality assurance on all interactions, and over 50% of customer support is fully automated. Most L1 roles managing basic tasks are gone, replaced by L2s who now prompt engineers and coach AI instead of coaching humans. Brex even changed its hiring to test AI fluency with case studies where candidates must show their prompts, not just results, because it believes operations can be 5-10x more efficient when teams have their own automation. ⚡️ Quick Links Everything you've been told about burnout is wrong (2 minute read) People have a "gratification window," the time they can work before needing tangible results. The system enforces guardrails—no public-figure use without permission, no explicit content, and shows notifications when your likeness is used. Former OpenAI and DeepMind researchers raise whopping $300M seed to automate science (3 minute read) Periodic Labs has secured a $300M seed round from notable investors, including Andreessen Horowitz and Nvidia, to automate scientific discovery through AI. Founded by former OpenAI and DeepMind researchers, the startup aims to create autonomous labs where AI scientists, aided by robots, discover new materials like superconductors. This approach seeks to generate novel data to advance AI further in material science, competing with other entities in the field. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How Do Good Product Metrics Look Like in 2025? (4 minute read) Your sales team and growth team are both optimizing for speed, yet you're still losing deals. New data shows why - top performers activate 69% of customers within one week because they ignore departmental boundaries. Most companies have sales perfecting their pitch while growth optimizes onboarding, but the fastest activation often requires mixing both. Maybe your differentiating feature works better as an interactive demo before the sales call, not during it. Maybe prospects need a brief demo, then product access, returning to sales only when ready. From managing people to managing AI: How the same leadership skills apply in the age of AI (4 minute read) Julie Zhuo emphasizes how AI turns everyone into managers, requiring traditional management skills to handle AI agents effectively. Her "diagnose with data, treat with design" framework highlights balancing intuition and data in product development. Despite AI-driven hypergrowth leading to poor data infrastructure, this isn't a significant obstacle, according to Zhuo. Growth Hacking (6 minute read) Growth hacking, a term coined by Sean Ellis, focuses on driving rapid, measurable growth through data-driven experimentation and unconventional tactics. It emphasizes metrics like acquisition, activation, retention, revenue, and referral, using the AARRR funnel to prioritize efforts. Successful examples include Airbnb's Craigslist integration and Dropbox's referral loop, highlighting creative strategies that scale effectively. Growth hacking tools like SEO, social media, and A/B testing are crucial, but must avoid over-optimization, vanity metrics, and neglecting retention for sustainable growth. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Atlas (Tool) AI-generated pricing pages with market analysis and customer engagement tools. Air Weave (Tool) An open-source tool that lets agents search any app or database. Merlio (Tool) Unified interface for using multiple AI models to generate text, images, and ideas. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Beauty of Constraints (4 minute read) Give your time unlimited time and resources and watch them build nothing useful. An engineering leader kept seeing projects die not from bad planning but from too much freedom - teams would list requirements, estimate timelines, then stumble along optimizing features that shouldn't exist. The counterintuitive fix is deliberately limiting scope, resources, and time. How to Make AI Write Less Like AI (4 minute read) A copywriter outsourced 100 blog posts to look like an agency, got paid in full, and then a week later, the client demanded every dollar back. The posts were grammatically perfect but strategically worthless. That failure taught him what most founders miss about AI - prompts don't matter if you're feeding it nothing. The fix is context engineering. Before writing anything, gather customer interviews, support tickets, and competitor messaging. Distill those into strategy documents like positioning canvases and messaging frameworks. Only after loading those as context do you start prompting. This works beyond copywriting - a founder prepping for a board meeting shouldn't ask AI for a deck but should first load market research and competitive analysis, then let AI build the narrative. text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-03 AI app spending 💻, Assort Health raises $76M 💰, Modal Labs raises $87M 💸 Don't put all your eggs in one LLM basket (Sponsor) Customers expect your AI feature to just work - but every LLM vendor will throw outages, model changes, and other curveballs at you. The solution? Diversify. OpenRouter gives you one API that connects to 500+ models: GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, Mistral, and more. When one provider has issues, requests automatically route to your backup. Your users never see an error. Add ~25ms of latency, eliminate hours of downtime. ✅ OpenAI-compatible SDK: your existing code works out of the box. ✅ Set fallback rules by price or latency . ✅ Bring your own API key (or let users bring theirs) 👉 Join 4.2M+ global users. Route your 1st prompt 📈 Headlines & Trends The AI Application Spending Report (2 minute read) a16z analyzed spending from 200,000 Mercury customers to see where startups put their AI dollars - horizontal apps beat vertical ones 60/40, with creative tools dominating as the largest category, proving AI turned design into something everyone does. Vibe coding hit enterprise hard with Replit generating 15x more revenue than Lovable because it builds full enterprise-grade apps beyond just UI. Nearly 70% of companies moved from consumer to business in under two years, getting pulled into enterprises faster than any previous software era. The platform streamlines patient access by reducing call wait times and integrating with EHR and PM systems, addressing longstanding scheduling frustrations. Assort Health's AI has improved the patient experience, delivering 98% resolution rates and driving substantial operational efficiencies for healthcare providers. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Balancing Multiple ICPs: Strategies for Solo Founders (12 minute read) Solo founders face challenges balancing multiple ideal customer profiles (ICPs). Strategies include time-boxing efforts, creating dedicated landing pages, and using smart classification during registration to target specific customer groups. Marketing and communication must be tailored for each ICP, ensuring relevant messaging and efficient resource allocation. 5 Interesting Learnings from Figma at $1 Billion in ARR (3 minute read) Figma reached $1 billion in ARR with 41% growth, $100M+ net income, and 24% free cash flow margins. The company maintains strong expansion with 1,119 customers paying over $100K ARR, driving a 129% NDR at scale. Mid-market customers at $10K-$100K ARR offer significant growth potential, while strategic investments in AI and new products support future growth. A 4-step framework for building delightful products (5 minute read) Nesrine Changuel emphasizes delight as a key business strategy, advising tech leaders to integrate emotional connection into product design. Her 4-step Delight Model includes identifying user motivators, converting these into opportunities, using a delight grid for solutions, and validating ideas with a checklist. This approach has been successfully applied in projects like Google Chrome and Spotify's Discover Weekly, illustrating the importance of balancing functionality and delight. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Openroll (Tool) AI agents that reveal competitor pay and guide every salary decision. Openfunnel (Tool) Monitor your TAM and surface companies when custom triggers fire. Rivet (Tool) Lets product designers own any UI change to the software they work on. 🎁 Miscellaneous A Research Thinking Toolkit to Speed Up the Discovery Phase (4 minute read) Founders are shipping MVPs in minutes with AI. However, a UX researcher who led discovery at Uber Eats says they're confusing motion with progress. Most teams build before figuring out if they're solving a real problem or if they're the right team to solve it. The fix is finding problem-solution fit first - who you are as a founder, what problem you're positioned to tackle, and whether your solution fits into someone's life. She breaks this into three phases. Incubate means resisting the urge to build richer ideas. Immerse means spending a focused week with customers instead of hundreds of scattered conversations. Integrate means being realistic about behavior change - your product needs to be 9X better than what people already use to overcome inertia. Reverse Impostor Syndrome (4 minute read) A coach noticed tech leaders who know they're excellent but work behind closed doors, so their job titles and external signals don't match their actual skill level. The solution isn't building confidence but external positioning - learning to speak about your strategies and present to leadership in ways that feel authentic. Focus on behaviors like framing work as strategic and building business cases rather than surface-level personal branding advice. The RAG Obituary: Killed by Agents, Buried by Context Windows (4 minute read) A founder built a financial search engine using the standard playbook - break documents into chunks, turn them into vectors, search, rerank, feed results to an AI. It took three years and constant maintenance. Then he tried Claude Code and realized it just searched files directly with grep, a tool from 1973, and got better results. The entire retrieval infrastructure was solving a problem that no longer exists. Early AI could only read 12 pages at once, so you had to find the right fragments. Now it can read 6,000 pages, so it just reads everything and investigates like a human would. ⚡️ Quick Links Tim Chen has quietly become of one the most sought-after solo investors (4 minute read) Tim Chen, the solo investor behind Essence VC, closed a $41 million fund quickly due to investor enthusiasm following Databricks' $2.2 billion acquisition of his portfolio company, Tabular. title: TLDR Founders 2025-10-06
text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-06 OpenAI acqui-hires 🤝, Naveen Rao’s new startup 🚀, VC reawakening 💰 Delve generated $1M in pipeline from TLDR newsletter ads (Sponsor) Delve ran 4 ads across 2 TLDR newsletters and brought in 66 leads, with a meaningful chunk from enterprise. The result: $1M in attributed pipeline and a 52x ROI. This case study breaks down their strategy, results, and example ads. Read the Delve case study. 📈 Headlines & Trends With its latest acqui-hire, OpenAI is doubling down on personalized consumer AI (3 minute read) OpenAI acquired Roi, an AI-powered personal finance app. CEO Sujith Vishwajith will be the only team member joining OpenAI. This move continues OpenAI's trend of acqui-hires targeting personalization-focused technology, enhancing its consumer applications like Pulse and Sora. The acquisition supports OpenAI's strategy to develop adaptive AI products and boost revenue from consumer apps. Venture Capital's Cautious Reawakening (2 minute read) VCs are taking more discovery meetings, but the bar is higher than ever - they want founders with real operating experience after getting burned by younger teams who couldn't execute. AI companies are growing 2-5x faster than historical SaaS peers and command an 85% valuation premium, but investors are warning founders not to over-raise or hire ahead of milestones. Naveen Rao's new AI hardware startup targets $5B valuation with backing from a16z, sources say (2 minute read) Naveen Rao's new startup, Unconventional, Inc., aims to raise $1 billion at a $5 billion valuation to develop a novel computer system with brain-like efficiency. Andreessen Horowitz, Lightspeed, Lux Capital, and Databricks are backing the venture, which plans to compete with Nvidia by designing custom AI hardware. Rao, a former AI leader at Databricks, has a successful track record with previous startups like MosaicML and Nervana Systems. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How You.com Survived ChatGPT With a Pivot to Enterprise AI (8 minute read) You.com shifted from consumer search to enterprise AI, offering search APIs and complex data analytics to businesses following ChatGPT's launch. Competing with Google and others in the AI market, You.com leverages its own search infrastructure to deliver detailed reports and agent-based solutions like business analytics and financial analysis. The company also introduced Auto Mode for integrating multiple LLMs. It has secured $100M in VC funding to expand its agent-centric services. Base, Best, Worst: Your 2026 Planning Framework (5 minute read) Building one annual plan forces founders to pretend everything goes perfectly - sales hit quota, marketing scales efficiently, and nobody quits. Instead, build three parallel plans with different growth targets and burn rates. Your base case uses actual historical data. Best case adds upside from partnerships or new products. Worst-case accounts for churn spikes or funding delays. The power is in pre-agreeing what triggers each scenario - if you hit X revenue in Q2, unlock Y hires. If conversion misses for two quarters, cut Z budget. Bring GTM leadership together to pressure test the assumptions before finalizing, then share one plan with the company, but review all three with the board quarterly. Alpha in the age of agreement (10 minute read) Seed and Series A valuations are soaring, often starting at $100M pre-money, with investment decisions happening in mere hours. VCs are prioritizing big follow-on rounds and targeting AI applications, driving uniformity in investment strategies. The challenge is generating alpha by balancing short-term contrarianism with long-term insights in an overwhelmingly consensus-driven market. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Meet Lovable for Email - Forget drag-and-drop email builders (Sponsor) Want to create awesome emails in seconds? Halloween, Black Friday, cart abundance, Changelog, newsletters. Create emails that actually convert without drag and dropping. Import your brand, describe and send. It's free -> migma.ai. Sculptor (Tool) A UI for Claude Code. Nexus (Tool) Nexus enables Business teams to build AI agents without code. HeyHelp (Tool) An AI assistant for Gmail that sorts emails, highlights priorities, drafts replies, and blocks spam. 🎁 Miscellaneous 996 Is Not Your Competition (3 minute read) The 996 crowd posts their suffering like medals, screenshotting their commits and counting their hours - they're performing intensity to avoid asking if they even care. You can't beat someone who thinks they're playing. When you genuinely love what you're building, hours disappear, and you're not counting because you're playing. Your 0.1% equity is probably worthless, and the expected value is less than staying at Google, but the math was never the point - the joy is. Intensity without belief just leads to burnout, that weight you feel every Sunday night. The Joy of Small Markets (5 minute read) Small markets can offer a more stable path to wealth compared to large ones due to "relative scale" advantages. Being a dominant player in a small market allows a company to sustain steady earnings that aren't threatened by smaller or larger competitors. Large companies often see these markets as too costly and small to enter, making them less competitive and more lucrative for those within. Language Agnostic Programming: Why You May Still Need Code (4 minute read) People claim English will replace programming languages entirely, but developers aren't buying it. Even if AI writes all your code, someone still has to debug it when things break, and natural language is too ambiguous for that. What if AI let you read code in whatever language you want, regardless of what it's written in? The codebase is written in Rust, but you debug in Python while your teammate reads it as TypeScript, with AI handling translation. This could kill the eternal problem of onboarding engineers who don't know your stack, though it requires AI fast enough to feel instant. ⚡️ Quick Links John Collison on Three Things Founders Can Learn from Elon Musk (3 minute read) Stripe's co-founder studied the Elon Musk biography and pulled three lessons. VC Democratization: Small Check Investments (5 minute read) VCs are democratizing as individuals and smaller entities increasingly make small check investments. Saying Less (3 minute read) Telling people your plans before executing them creates a false sense of accomplishment that makes follow-through less likely - you get the social reward without doing the work. title: TLDR Founders 2025-10-08
text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-08 OpenAI Dev Day 💻, ICE invests $2B in Polymarket 💹, AMD & OpenAI partnership 🤝fa If you're building the Enterprise AI roadmap, clear your schedule for October 22 (Sponsor) The Enterprise Ready Conference is a one-day event in SF for product and engineering leaders shaping the future of Enterprise AI.Hosted by WorkOS , the event features a curated lineup of speakers with hands-on experience building for the enterprise, including leaders from Postman, Webflow, Rippling, Render, Socket, Opal, and RunReveal.You'll hear how they're driving Enterprise AI adoption in real-world environments — what's working, what's not, how they're scaling securely, and where automation is making the biggest impact.If you're a founder, exec, PM, or engineer responsible for the Enterprise AI roadmap, this conference is for you. Register today → 📈 Headlines & Trends Everything OpenAI Announced at DevDay 2025 (1 minute read) OpenAI is transforming ChatGPT from a chatbot into an operating system. The company launched Apps SDK so developers can build actual apps inside ChatGPT instead of just GPTs, meaning you can talk directly to Canva, Zillow, and Coursera without leaving the chat interface. OpenAI also released AgentKit for building agentic workflows with evaluation tools included. AMD stock skyrockets 23% as OpenAI looks to take stake in AI chipmaker (3 minute read) OpenAI will deploy 6 gigawatts of AMD Instinct GPUs over multiple years, starting with 1 gigawatt in late 2026. If the deal is fully exercised, OpenAI would acquire 10% of AMD. Two weeks ago, OpenAI agreed to a deal with Nvidia for $100 billion, with OpenAI's data center spending commitments now totalling over roughly $1 trillion over the coming years. NYSE Owner ICE Commits Up to $2B Investment in Betting Platform Polymarket (3 minute read) The NYSE owner, Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), plans to invest up to $2 billion in the prediction marketplace Polymarket, valuing it at around $8 billion. This move seeks to provide legitimacy and expand Polymarket's reach, potentially aiding its return to the US market. ICE will also distribute Polymarket's event-driven data and collaborate on tokenization projects. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics 7 Questions Every Data Team Should Ask the Business (5 minute read) Most data teams struggle with the same problem: nobody knows what to work on. The business asks for dashboards, the analysts build them, and a month later, everyone quietly stops using them. This piece argues it's a conversation problem. The author's team at Facebook used to sit down with every department twice a year and ask questions that got to the real issues: What's keeping you from hitting your targets? When was the last time data changed your mind? Where are you putting in effort without results? These questions don't sound technical, but they surface where data can actually help. If you stop asking what people “need from data” and start asking how they make decisions, you'll finally find work that matters. Elad Gil's Framework for Spotting Billion-Dollar Markets Before They Look Big (5 minute read) Markets beat teams beat ideas - great teams can't escape bad markets, but great markets give you multiple shots on goal. Wedding invitations seemed too niche, payroll too boring, file storage too late, then became Minted, Gusto, and Dropbox. Four principles for finding overlooked opportunities: use first principles to ask what's changed in cost or infrastructure, scratch your own itch with real pain, not vague ideas, build product-first because "would you use this" is weak but "would you pay now" is real, and look where smart people tinker for fun before mainstream notices. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Everyone Hates Virtual Desktops. Why? Slow, Expensive and Stuck in the Past (Sponsor) Lag. Latency. Cost. Complexity. You can do better: Blue Border™ secures work data and apps locally on any PC or Mac - no hosting, streaming, or added infrastructure. Enjoy fast, secure remote work on any PC or Mac - up to 40% less than Virtual Desktops (VDI). See why Blue Border™ is the #1 VDI alternative Databutton (Tool) AI-powered dev tool that turns business ideas into full-stack apps through natural language input. Thunai (Tool) Agentic AI platform that transforms organizational knowledge into self-learning agents for automating support, sales, and marketing tasks. Semiotic (Tool) AI-native design agency. 🎁 Miscellaneous Can AI write better? (3 minute read) A founder decided to measure what makes great writing tick, literally. By mapping the rhythm and “surprise spikes” in authors like Hemingway and Wodehouse, he found each has a quantifiable fingerprint: where sentences get weird, sharp, or musical. He trained a small model to detect those pulses, and it worked. Maybe the next step in AI isn't more scale, but learning how to imitate the texture of human thought. Build a Billion-Dollar Startup with a minimal team thanks to AI (4 minute read) Instagram sold for $1B with 13 people. WhatsApp had 55. PostHog ships with pods of two to six, and Basecamp runs profitable software with roughly 30 and no managers. The observable pattern is to design for systems over headcount. In practice, that means your engineer answers the support ticket instead of waiting for “customer success,” your designer pushes copy tweaks straight to production instead of filing a Jira ticket, and your founder still writes the onboarding emails because it keeps them close to the user. ChatGPT for competitive analysis (4 minute read) Most people use ChatGPT to ask who their competitors are. This team turned it into a newsroom analyst. They dropped in homepage links, headline lists, and keyword data from Toronto outlets and had it break down tone, coverage gaps, and what kinds of stories each publication actually wins on. It found patterns humans miss, like which sites dominate “how/where/when” searches or bury strong topics under weak headlines. It's the first real glimpse of AI used not for shortcuts, but for curiosity at scale. ⚡️ Quick Links Why AI Agents will be the death of the B player (2 minute read) The “good enough” era of hiring is ending. Learning to let go: care vs. laissez-faire (3 minute read) VC Gil Dibner reflects on the hardest part of investing: knowing when to step back. Jeff Bezos on why wandering matters (1 minute read) Jeff Bezos reminds us not to forget the value of “wandering.” Composite gets backing from NFDG for its cross-browser agent tool (4 minute read) Composite secured $5.6 million in seed funding led by NFDG to develop its cross-browser AI agent tool. text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-13 Claude Code plugins 💻, ChatGPT app store moment 📱, Kalshi raises $300M 💰 📈 Headlines & Trends The ChatGPT App Store Moment (2 minute read) OpenAI just launched apps inside ChatGPT where you can say "Spotify, make me a playlist" or "DoorDash, order my usual" and it handles everything conversationally. This looks like early iPhone web apps, not true native experiences yet, but the trajectory is clear. As OpenAI expands its SDK to give developers access to user memory, persistent state, and action execution, we'll see actual native ChatGPT applications designed for conversational interfaces. Kalshi Raises $300 Million, Valuing Prediction Market Startup at $5 Billion (2 minute read) Kalshi raised $300 million in funding, valuing the prediction market platform at $5 billion, with plans to expand into 140 countries. Despite Kalshi's recent growth and surpassing Polymarket for weekly transactions, skeptics note it isn't fully on-chain. Meanwhile, rival Polymarket, valued at $9 billion, is preparing to re-enter the US market after a regulatory boost. Claude Code Plugins Now in Public Beta (3 minute read) Anthropic has introduced plugin support in Claude Code, allowing users to install custom slash commands, agents, MCP servers, and hooks with a single command. These modular extensions simplify setup sharing and enable flexible customizations for development workflows. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The Pricing Ratio: A Trick to Estimate Churn in 10 Seconds (4 minute read) You can guess a competitor's churn rate by dividing their annual price by monthly price. Most subscription companies eventually price their annual plan right above where average users quit paying monthly. Codecademy struggled with 4-5 month retention, so it priced annual at 6 months of monthly payments and saw huge wins. Hemingway Editor charges $25 monthly but $100 yearly, a ratio of 4, suggesting most users bounce after 3 months. ChatGPT has a ratio of 12, Canva sits at 10. The math works because companies learn to lock in users just before they'd naturally churn. Lazy Startup Marketing vs. Top 1% Growth Tactics (2 minute read) Andrew Yeung critiques weak early-stage marketing like generic posts, podcasts, or ads without strategy. He highlights top operators who build personal brands, communities, and email funnels while capturing and converting mass attention. The best marketers engineer virality, create amplification networks, and even design negative CAC channels that pay them to promote. How a 13-person startup scaled to millions in ARR (5 minute read) A 13-person startup hit seven figures without product managers, tickets, or hiring people with fancy resumes. Credal's founders worked at Palantir and noticed almost nobody there started with four years at top companies, so they hired hungry people instead of credentialed ones. Engineers own business results directly, set aggressive weekly goals, and review everything at Friday meetings, where nothing is off limits. Zero enterprise customers have churned, they keep over 100% of revenue from existing clients, and landed MongoDB and NBC Universal. They figure out what outcome they want first, then work backwards to get there, ignoring whatever startup advice is popular on Twitter. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Claybird (Tool) An agent that produces AI video ads. Tiptap pages (Tool) Tiptap Pages brings page awareness to your editor. Inconvo (Tool) Prepare existing data for AI apps like ChatGPT to use. 🎁 Miscellaneous These Are The Speediest Companies To Go From Series A To Series C (5 minute read) Generative AI startups top the list of fastest fundraisers going from Series A to C in under a year, with Anysphere and Cognition leading the pack. Fintech and spacetech companies also see rapid funding, with firms like NomuPay and Impulse Space securing hefty investments quickly. Other sectors such as vertical AI, robotics, and biotech are also witnessing accelerated fundraising, highlighting investor enthusiasm across diverse tech domains. Tiny Recursive Models for Very Specific Problems (3 minute read) Samsung just showed you can beat massive LLMs on structured puzzles using a 7 million parameter model trained on only 1,000 examples per task. Its Tiny Recursive Model uses one small 2-layer network that loops over itself, refining both its reasoning state and current answer each pass. It hits 87% on extreme Sudoku, 85% on hard mazes, and 45% on ARC-AGI benchmarks, often beating models thousands of times larger. Nvidia to the rescue (3 minute read) Intel just got a lifeline from an unlikely source. After handing Uncle Sam a 10% equity stake to unlock government grants, Intel will see Nvidia buy $5 billion of shares at $23.28 each. The deal spikes Intel's stock 27% and pairs Nvidia's GPUs with Intel's CPUs in data centers and PCs. ⚡️ Quick Links Uncomfortable/unspoken (1 minute read) The easiest way to create change is to find topics people don't want to talk about. Airtable Expands AI with DeepSky Acquisition, New CTO (4 minute read) Airtable has appointed former OpenAI leader David Azose as CTO and acquired DeepSky, an AI superagent. The move transforms Airtable into a multi-product, AI-native company by enhancing its AI capabilities. DeepSky will operate as a standalone product and integrate with Airtable's platform to streamline research and application development. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics 32 things we've learned about building a startup that scales (4 minute read) Everyone thinks scaling kills speed, but what if it's the opposite? When PostHog grew from 11 to 150 people, teams capped at six shipped faster. One pessimist kills momentum faster than bad code, and compromising on a hire always backfires. They replaced interviews with “SuperDays” to see candidates actually work, and built around simple goals like “ship stuff,” not OKRs. They learned PMF isn't permanent, agencies disappoint, stretch goals die, and even enterprise buyers just want fun, human brands. The False Consensus Effect (3 minute read) Marketers often assume customers think like they do – and pay for it. The “False Consensus Effect” explains why: we mistake our own tastes for the norm. Netflix proved the power of testing assumptions when it dropped House of Cards all at once and watched viewers binge six episodes in days. Amazon built its empire on A/B tests, not opinions. Sometimes you're not your customer, so stop building for yourself. Assessing a Startup's Defensibility (6 minute read) Assessing a startup's defensibility focuses on evaluating its economic moat, product-market fit, and competitive advantage. Key factors include cost advantages, intangible assets, network effects, switching costs, and efficient scale for economic moat; strong retention rates, NPS, and usage metrics for product-market fit; and intellectual property, team capability, and scalable business models for competitive advantage. Investors use these criteria to determine if a startup can sustain market share and scale effectively against competition. ⚒️ Tools & Resources How I Built TLDR (TLDR's Inc. Profile, 5 minute read) This is the first time I've told the backstory behind building TLDR, hope you find it interesting! Slashit App (Tool) Text automation tool with AI commands and templates to speed up writing and replies. Alai (Tool) Generate high quality presentations with AI. LetMeCheck.ai (Tool) AI tool that analyzes codebases to provide quick, reliable quality insights. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Epidemic of Wasted Talent (5 minute read) Most jobs don't need you, they just need someone. Alex McCann argues that high salaries often mask a deeper cost: companies pay you to ignore your own potential. He contrasts corporate comfort with a Colombian birdwatcher who found purpose in work only he could do. The point isn't to quit tomorrow, but to start creating something that couldn't exist without you. Being replaceable pays well. Being irreplaceable matters more. The Data Pendulum Swings to Consolidation (3 minute read) The “modern data stack” once promised freedom through specialization, i.e, tools like Fivetran for ingestion and dbt for transformation. However, with compute giants Snowflake and Databricks now controlling 72% of the market's revenue, gravity is pulling everything back together. Their scale lets them eat margin across the stack. The Fivetran–dbt merger shows how survival now means scale, two startups combining to stand a chance as data recenters around the giants. The Discomfort of Focus (5 minute read) What if focus isn't about discipline but about trust, as in trusting that saying no won't close doors, it'll open the right ones faster. Most founders chase breadth to feel safe, but this team learned real momentum came from committing to one painfully specific ICP. The paradox was that narrowing created freedom. Once they stopped hedging and chose, everything else (clarity, conviction, growth) finally followed. ⚡️ Quick Links Pursuit of Pain (3 minute read) This founder thought grit was his superpower until it almost killed his company. Street-Smart Business (3 minute read) Real business isn't learned in classrooms, it's learned on the street: selling candy bars, closing scrappy deals, taking hits, and learning to think fast. Reducto Studio (Tool) Reducto Studio is a platform for building, optimizing, and deploying production-ready document pipelines with performance insights and confidence-scored extractions. title: TLDR Founders 2025-10-17
text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-17 Thrive Capital’s Josh Kushner 🏆, General Intuition raises $134M seed 💰, founder moat 🚀 📈 Headlines & Trends The Next Product (1 minute read) Products often collapse under their own weight. Every competitor keeps adding features until users feel exhausted, then something simple shows up and feels new again. Innovation isn't about adding more but about knowing when to stop and when to return to the version that simply works and makes sense again. General Intuition lands $134M seed to teach agents spatial reasoning using video game clips (4 minute read) General Intuition raised $134M in seed funding to train AI agents using a massive dataset from video game clips on Medal's platform, focusing on spatial-temporal reasoning. The startup aims to apply this technology to gaming, search-and-rescue drones, and autonomous systems. Notably, OpenAI previously showed interest in acquiring Medal for its valuable data, which is key to their unique training approach. Josh Kushner, Thrive, and the New World (56 minute read) Josh Kushner's Thrive Capital has grown into a major VC firm, with investments including a $2 billion stake in Stripe and $150 million in OpenAI leading to substantial returns. Despite Thrive's success, Kushner sought guidance from Rick Rubin on maintaining his intuitive decision-making, reflecting concerns about losing this edge as decisions often rely on non-explainable intuition. Thrive's strategic investments, such as in OpenAI during its leadership crisis, demonstrate its ability to capitalize on opportunities, even under challenging circumstances. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics When the Founder Is the Moat (6 minute read) What happens when the most valuable part of a company can walk out the door? In the AI boom, big tech isn't buying startups, they're hiring their founders. Data can be copied and models can be rebuilt, but conviction can't be replaced. A founder's mindset is the real moat. Those who play the long game build lasting companies. Congratulations, Publicly (6 minute read) You can't just be happy for someone anymore. Even small moments of support now demand an audience. A text of genuine congratulations doesn't feel complete until it's echoed online, where friendship doubles as performance. We call it community, but it's really choreography. Each like, quote, and comment is part of an endless loop of reciprocal praise. For founders, it's the same trap in different clothes. The pressure to show momentum, post wins, and stay visible can quietly replace the work itself. Map User Journeys to Search Queries and Prompts (6 minute read) Most SEO still looks like shooting arrows in the dark: picking keywords and hoping they hit a target. Search today is closer to a conversation than a list of results. People ask questions the way they think, and AI tools answer them that way too. Treating search like a guessing game misses how users actually move. Map it instead. Know when someone's just exploring, when they're comparing, and when they're ready to buy. ⚒️ Tools & Resources The Context Company (Tool) Observability for your AI agents in just 10 lines of code. Hyperspell (Tool) Give your agents automatic context by connecting it to your users' data. Pally - AI Relationship Management (Tool) Pally is an AI-powered relationship manager that unifies your social connections, researches their activity, and helps you prep, track, and engage smarter. 🎁 Miscellaneous The AI Growth Endurance Problem (7 minute read) AI startups are growing faster than any SaaS wave before them, but speed hides cracks. Today's AI boom is like a sprint run on borrowed stamina: margins are thin, retention untested, and switching costs low. Early growth looks great because customers are new and competition is still forming. The real test comes when renewals hit. Growth endurance, not growth speed, decides who survives once the hype cools. Andreessen Horowitz Has Returned at Least $25 Billion Net to Its Backers Since the Firm's Founding in 2009 (2 minute read) Andreessen Horowitz has returned at least $25 billion to its backers since 2009, with its $900 million 2012 Fund III hitting a 9.4x net TVPI. The firm invests heavily in emerging tech sectors and defense, with significant stakes in companies like Databricks. Despite its influence, much of its fund performance remains under wraps, though new decks reveal insights into IPO candidates and fund performance. Lesser Apes (7 minute read) As AI gets smarter, people may stop thinking as hard. We are like animals that lose their instincts when life gets too easy. Each time we let machines handle a task, our minds become a little weaker. Use the tools, but keep doing some work yourself, or one day you might forget how. ⚡️ Quick Links Motion Raises $60M at $550M Valuation to Build the Agentic Work Suite for Businesses (5 minute read) Motion raised $60M, led by Scale Venture Partners, to enhance its agentic work suite for SMBs at a valuation of $550M. Legal Tech Investment Hits All-Time High With Filevine Funding (4 minute read) Legal tech investment reached a record $2.4 billion in 2025, bolstered by AI-driven automation in the legal field. Distyl AI raises $175M in funding at $1.8B valuation to scale AI consulting platform (3 minute read) Distyl AI, founded by ex-Palantir engineers, raised $175 million at a $1.8 billion valuation to scale its AI consulting platform. title: TLDR Founders 2025-10-20
text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-20 Top 50 US startups 🇺🇸, startups investing in startups 💰, AI adoption slow down 🤖 AI pricing strategies from Andreessen Horowitz's Martin Casado (Sponsor) If you're struggling to figure out your AI pricing, this live webinar is for you. Martin Casado, General Partner at Andreessen Horowitz, will share new monetization strategies that go beyond flat fees and subscription models. This session will look at how companies like OpenAI are building monetization infrastructure , and why AI is forcing every SaaS company to reinvent its own pricing and billing. Topics include: Why AI is accelerating the shift from pure subscription models to results-based and hybrid monetization models How fast-growing AI companies are aligning product, finance, GTM, and engineering How to treat pricing like a product to enable experimentation and scale Register today 📈 Headlines & Trends LinkedIn reveals 2025 Top 50 US startups (10 minute read) LinkedIn released its 2025 Top 50 US Startups list, ranking companies by employee growth, engagement, and talent demand. AI-focused firms like Anthropic, Character.AI, and Replit dominate the top spots, reflecting hiring momentum across the sector. Security startup Chainguard secured the No. 18 position after raising $356 million and reaching a $3.5 billion valuation. Startups Investing in Startups (5 minute read) When startups start acting like VCs, something's shifted. Vercel, barely past $200M in revenue, is now using investor cash to fund other startups, calling it ecosystem building. Maybe it's smart positioning in the AI infrastructure race, or maybe it's the oldest sign of a bubble. Is AI Adoption Slowing Down? (3 minute read) What if the AI boom isn't cooling off, just maturing faster than expected? Ramp's latest data shows paid AI adoption dipping again, even as retention jumps from 60 to 80 percent. Tech and finance lead the pack, but most industries still haven't caught up. The surprise is that companies aren't buying less, they're buying smarter. For founders, the gold rush phase is ending. The next wave belongs to products that prove lasting, repeatable value. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Building Outbound from Scratch: A Founder's Playbook (5 minute read) Three weeks perfecting cold email copy. Zero emails reaching inboxes. Salesmotion learned outbound the hard way - messaging doesn't matter when everything bounces to spam. Once they fixed deliverability, the actual sequence became clear. Build your account list first, maybe 200 companies that look like your best customers. Then work on infrastructure - validate emails, warm domains, send 10 per day max while you test. After that works, test messaging in small batches against specific personas. Track who responds from your target accounts, not just total replies. Investor Churn (4 minute read) Your failed round might not be about your product - it could be about your process. Sixty percent of interested investors drift without ever saying no. They stop replying, delay calls, and ghost quietly. Investors see hundreds of deals quarterly. If your communication rhythm breaks, you slide from active to maybe-later. In healthtech, this gets worse because investors already expect slow timelines, so any process sloppiness signals you'll struggle with execution too. Attention decay is the real enemy - momentum leaks silently between interactions, and once that energy disappears, it's almost impossible to reignite Everyone should be using Claude Code more (5 minute read) Claude Code offers versatile AI capabilities for non-technical users, running locally to manage tasks like file organization, image enhancement, and video downloads. It excels in handling large files efficiently and performing tasks beyond cloud-based AI tools. Users share creative applications, including generating domain names, synthesizing customer call transcripts, and creating self-driving documentation. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Promptless (Tool) Promptless is an AI teammate that proactively drafts and updates customer-facing docs based on product changes and team activity. Cekura (Tool) Cekura helps AI teams launch and monitor voice and chat agents 10x faster with automated QA, simulation, and real-time performance monitoring. Pythagora (Tool) Pythagora is an all-in-one AI dev platform that builds full web apps from scratch through natural language, handling specs, frontend, and backend in hours. 🎁 Miscellaneous Momentum != Moat (6 minute read) An a16z partner just claimed momentum is the only moat that matters - get to $2M ARR in ten days or you're not interesting. Sounds exciting until you notice this mindset is poisoning an entire generation of companies. When billion-dollar funds only care about triple-digit growth rates, they're effectively declaring that 95% of successful business outcomes shouldn't exist. Wealthfront took 17 years to hit $308M revenue and $123M profit - that's a fantastic business, but to momentum investors it's a failure. The worse problem is that momentum attracts competition like blood in water. Legal AI, code gen, AI copywriters - every hot category now has five companies racing to $100M ARR while burning unsustainable amounts on compute and customer acquisition. How To Build Core Values That Mean Something (4 minute read) Dolly Parton accidentally wrote the best business advice ever - find out who you are and do it on purpose. Many companies build culture by measuring employee satisfaction scores or forming wordsmithing aspirational phrases about who they want to become someday. That's backwards. Your core values already exist in how your best people behave currently. Who Is Governing AI Companies? For Nearly Half Of AI Startups In California, The Answer Is Only Men (10 minute read) A study of 140 AI companies in California revealed that women occupy only 15% of board seats in private AI startups, and 43% of these companies have no women on their boards. The lack of gender diversity stems from structural biases favoring executives and investors, areas where women are underrepresented, and behavioral biases that prioritize familiar candidates over diverse talent. The study suggests that appointing independent directors and expanding search networks can help improve board diversity. ⚡️ Quick Links The Internet's Next Business Model: A Conversation with Cloudflare's Matthew Prince (3 minute read) Matthew Prince of Cloudflare highlights a shift from search engines to AI-driven "answer engines," challenging the current internet business model. Life Rewards Repetition (1 minute read) Founders romanticize novelty, but most progress comes from compounding small, repeatable motions. A Process for Having it All with Jason Jacobs (4 minute read) Jason Jacobs, a successful founder of Runkeeper, is developing processes to balance entrepreneurship with personal fulfillment. title: TLDR Founders 2025-10-22
text: TLDR Founders 2025-10-22 ChatGPT Atlas 💻, Sesame raises $250M 💰, LangChain raises $125M 🚀 Report: ecommerce friction is costing SaaS companies billions in lost revenue (Sponsor) 98% of software vendors sell online, with over half generating the majority of of their revenue from ecommerce channels. But new research from Cleverbridge shows that SaaS companies are lagging at checkout and beyond - leaving billions on the table. Addressing these friction points proactively can unlock a massive growth opportunity for SaaS leaders. Download the full (ungated) report today and stop friction before it stops you. 📈 Headlines & Trends OpenAI launches an AI-powered browser (3 minute read) OpenAI launched ChatGPT Atlas, an AI-powered browser, starting on macOS, with other platforms soon. The browser features built-in chat capabilities and a sidecar feature for seamless user interaction. It offers an AI agent for task automation but is limited to certain subscription tiers at launch. Lean Startup Rebooted (1 minute read) For years, startups followed two playbooks: Lean Startup and Blitzscaling. Blitzscaling won once AI arrived and VCs flooded the space with capital. Now, as the AI bubble debate grows, the original lean approach is making a comeback. Sesame, the conversational AI startup from Oculus founders, raises $250M and launches beta (3 minute read) Sesame, founded by Oculus alumni, raised $250 million in Series B funding and launched a beta for its conversational AI app. The startup aims to embed AI agents into smart glasses with a natural voice interface. Sesame's tech is praised for its realistic dialogue, with its beta iOS app offering features like search and text interaction. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Only 100 Metrics Matter (2 minute read) Companies track thousands of numbers, but almost none of them change decisions. In reality, about 100 metrics explain 90% of what's happening. Once you break the business into equations - growth, engagement, revenue - you see which levers actually matter. Everything else lives in the long tail: useful for edge cases, not for running the company. How Struggle Becomes Strategy (3 minute read) Pejman Nozad reflects on his 32-year journey from living above a yogurt shop to becoming a top Silicon Valley investor, framing early hardship as the foundation of empathy and insight in venture capital. He highlights lessons on perseverance, resourcefulness, and the power of community in supporting founders through isolation and uncertainty. His message centers on transforming struggle into strategy and using personal adversity as an enduring advantage in investing and leadership. Why We Buy: The Omission Bias (3 minute read) People judge harmful actions more harshly than harmful inactions - this is known as the “Omission Bias.” Buying feels riskier than not buying because action carries blame if it backfires. Amazon and others flip that bias by showing the cost of doing nothing. When inaction feels like “the bad decision” - whether it's a higher cart price, lost ROI, or missed deal - spending suddenly feels like the safer move. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Clarify (Tool) Clarify is an autonomous CRM that automates pipeline building, deal tracking, and sales tasks for founders and early-stage teams. Tusk (Tool) AI testing tool for API testing, unit testing, and integration testing Integral (Tool) Integral is a modern Slack/Discord alternative built for large teams and communities, focused on deeper discussions and fewer interruptions. 🎁 Miscellaneous Anthropic Is Catching Up With OpenAI (1 minute read) Anthropic quietly went from $1B to nearly $7B in run-rate revenue this year, a six-fold jump that puts it within striking distance of OpenAI. While OpenAI dominates headlines, Anthropic's API-driven business is growing faster and more efficiently, relying less on free users and more on enterprise integrations. Beneath the public fights about AI ethics and regulation, the real story is simpler: Anthropic might actually win on execution. Prioritization Starts With Strategy (4 minute read) Most teams treat prioritization as a to-do list problem when it's really a strategy problem. Every decision about what's “valuable” reflects a bet on where leverage lives and where decay is setting in. The real work is mapping the forces at play: what increases or protects revenue, what reduces or protects cost, and which advantages will actually last. Once you see those dynamics, prioritization stops being about urgency and starts being about power. Twelve Years, 400 Startups, and a new $87,654,321 Fund (3 minute read) Boost VC launches an $87M Fund 4 to invest in 150 Deep Tech startups, focusing on nuclear energy, genetics, and space travel. The fund boosts Boost VC's assets under management to over $300M, with notable investments in companies like Coinbase, Radiant Nuclear, and Colossal Biosciences. Previous funds have shown strong returns, with Fund I achieving a 4.35× DPI and 7.02× TVPI. ⚡️ Quick Links Andreessen Horowitz DPI Numbers Show Pre-2015 Vintages & Crypto Driving Returns (2 minute read) Andreessen Horowitz's pre-2015 and crypto funds drove strong returns, with its first crypto fund achieving a 5x DPI. Hidden AI (1 minute read) “Hidden AI” is when the tech stays invisible while transforming cost structures underneath. AI agent tooling provider LangChain raises $125M at $1.25B valuation (4 minute read) LangChain raised $125M at a $1.25B valuation in a Series B led by IVP, with investments from CapitalG, ServiceNow Ventures, and others.
