title: TLDR Founders 2025-12-05
text: TLDR Founders 2025-12-05 State of AI 🤖, expensive growth equity 💰, AI sales agents 🤝 State of Private Markets: early-stage valuations are back with a vengeance (Sponsor) Venture looks completely different than it did in 2023. In Q3 2025, startups on Carta raised the highest quarterly sum of new funding in three years. And that's in line with industry-wide trends. After the reset in tech valuations, founders have raised more total cash with each passing quarter. Mega-deals, sometimes worth billions of dollars, brought volume up substantially. Read the Q3 2025 State of Private Markets report to learn why early-stage valuations keep rising, down rounds are declining, and the bay area is still outperforming NY, Boston, and LA combined. Get the full report 📈 Headlines & Trends The State of AI (7 minute read) The past year in AI has seen rapid shifts in capability, adoption, and developer behavior. These changes have reshaped how we build and interact with intelligent systems. This post looks at a new study that examines these changes through more than 100 trillion tokens of real-world model usage from OpenRouter. The goal of the study was to provide a large-scale empirical picture of what people do with AI today and what that tells us about the future of the industry. A commentary on the general phenomenon of best-in-class growth companies raising successive rounds at higher and higher valuations (3 minute read) Most of the financing for best-in-class growth companies raising successive rounds at higher and higher valuations makes no sense. Growth equity capital is very expensive. Companies at this scale typically deploy a substantial majority of their expensive equity dollars into highly predictable, relatively low-risk GTM initiatives. The capital is being used inappropriately, and these companies should finance their GTM investments differently. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Transparent Leadership Beats Servant Leadership (2 minute read) A servant leader quickly becomes an overworked single point of failure. Once they leave, there is nobody else who knows how to handle the obstacles the leader moved out of the way for everyone. A good leader coaches people, connects people, teaches methodical problem solving, and explains values and principles embraced by the organization. They create direct links between supply and demand and allow their direct reports to grow their careers. Good leaders are continuously training their replacements and generally make themselves redundant. Vercel's AI Sales Agent: 10 SDRs Down to 1 in Six Weeks (3 minute read) Vercel enabled its salespeople to spend most of their time with customers by transforming its inbound sales team. It used an AI sales representative to respond and analyze leads, which condensed the number of touches it took to convert. AI enabled quicker responses, creating an unexpected advantage. Increasing human contact means salespeople can develop deeper relationships and drive more success for customers. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Dummies Guide to Payments for SaaS Platforms (Sponsor) Integrating payments in your application? Read SaaS Payments for Dummies first. This blueprint will help you transform clunky, fragmented checkout flows into brand-building experiences. Learn to how avoid known pitfalls, design smarter journeys, and launch payments that drive loyalty instead of drop-off. When you're ready to start building, contact NMI for white-label payment solutions . Fizzy (Tool) A refreshing take on cards, columns, and kanban. Honeyjar (Tool) AI co-pilot for communications and PR. Pylar (Tool) Pylar is a platform that securely connects AI agents to data sources with granular access controls and observability. 🎁 Miscellaneous Becoming great at listening (3 minute read) Your job as a listener is to consume decades of someone else's experience in minutes. This is impossible if you are busy mentally scripting your reply. The secret to world-class communication is to treat listening and thinking as mutually exclusive states. Turn off your analysis engine completely while they speak. When they finish, ask for a "thinking pause." That awkward silence is the only way to truly synthesize reality before you act on it. Think First, AI Second (4 minute read) We are starting to see the long-term cognitive cost of "AI First" workflows: our brains are literally disengaging. An MIT study found that students who used AI at the start of a task showed lower neural activity and memory retention than those who did the work themselves. The fix is not to ban AI, but to change the sequence. Always force yourself to draft the structure or outline before you open the prompt window. By treating AI as a "Devil's Advocate" that critiques your existing thoughts rather than a generator to create them, you keep your cognitive edge while still leveraging the speed of the model. ⚡️ Quick Links Why Sell Lifetime Plans, in a Default Subscription World? (10 minute read) Evangelism is worth more than money, especially early on. API-Bench v2 (3 minute read) A new study revealed that general LLMs fail nearly 40% of the time when building real-world API integrations, proving that specialized infrastructure is still required to handle outdated documentation and debugging. Loud Isn't Enough (4 minute read) You cannot displace a giant with a spreadsheet, you have to turn their entire existence into a moral problem. title: TLDR Founders 2025-12-08
text: TLDR Founders 2025-12-08 Building in AI era 👨‍💻, the agent inflection 🤖, Alfred Lin on decisions 📚 Supercharge Your Productivity with Seamless.AI (Sponsor) B2B lead gen tool providing contact info (emails, buy intent, etc.) for 1.3 billion decision makers. Sign up Today 📈 Headlines & Trends Now Is A Good Time To Sell Your Company (6 minute read) Valuations have grown faster than fundamentals, and corporate balance sheets are unusually strong. Antitrust risk is about to rise, so selling before this shift is rational. Acquirers are overlooking unhealthy financials because they fear missing out on AI or automation. IPO markets may reopen, but most major private companies are still staying away from going public. Alfred Lin on decision quality (4 minute read) Long-term relevance in an AI-dominated world comes from learning to make strong decisions under uncertainty rather than mastering any single technical field. Being right, which centers on ego and rigid beliefs, is different from getting it right, which requires curiosity, updating views, and detaching identity from ideas. Leaders who prioritize getting it right foster open debate, psychological safety, and faster progress. Ego-driven cultures slow teams and limit contribution. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics From 10 to 31 Tasks Daily: The Agent Inflection Point (2 minute read) The velocity of work changes instantly when we give agents the right tools. Businesses can now use agents to complete tasks, just like software engineers have been doing for the last several months. It is now possible to spin up multiple parallel workstreams, multiplying daily output without working longer hours. The agent inflection point is already here. Your Experts Won't Train Your AI. AI will also enhance creative tools for seamless storytelling across media, redefine data stacks with AI-native features, and push traditional systems of record into the background in favor of dynamic agent-led execution environments. Vertical AI will evolve into multi-party collaborations, agents will prioritize machine legibility over human interface design, and personalized experiences will become the norm, culminating in AI-native universities and interactive storytelling worlds. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How to Ask for Help and Actually Get It (7 minute read) Make help requests specific, finite, and actionable to increase the chances of receiving assistance. Requests should clearly define the commitment, showing preparation and ensuring the help will be impactful. Avoid open-ended asks and demonstrate you have taken steps on your own, and value the helper's input. A simple guide to validating product ideas (5 minute read) You've got an idea, and you're convinced it's great. You want to start building right now. This is how founders waste months on things nobody wants. PostHog's founders pivoted five times, and most of those ideas never made it past a single conversation. The trap is asking people if they like your idea - they'll say yes just to make you go away. Instead, find out if they've actually had the problem and tried to solve it themselves. If they built some crappy internal system to deal with it, there's probably a business there. If it's not a hair-on-fire problem for them, move on. The Founder's Playbook for Performance Culture (5 minute read) Why do brilliant teams often plateau at $20M ARR? It isn't a lack of effort—it's usually a lack of Single-Threaded Ownership. If two people own a goal, no one does. This guide details how to build performance infrastructure: hire for "capacity" (driving initiatives start-to-finish), define specific actions that violate your values (like saying "that's not my job"), and ensure every objective has exactly one owner so performance is always visible. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Tap into an affordable global workforce in 2026 with Justworks (Sponsor) Your startup needs every advantage it can get - and that means not paying $150K for roles you can offshore. text: TLDR Founders 2025-12-12 State of solo founding 🚀, AI churn wave 👋, RL moats 💼 Have you chosen the wrong pricing model? (Sponsor) Seat-based, usage-based, or outcome-based: which pricing strategy is right? Most founders pick a model based on what competitors do, not what fits their product. Find your ideal pricing model >> 📈 Headlines & Trends Hardware Is A Fruit (5 minute read) The feedback loop for physical products is speeding up. We used to assume that building hardware meant long timelines and high costs for every mistake. That dynamic is fading. Modern simulation tools and AI assistants now let engineers iterate on physical designs almost as fast as they write code. The barrier to entry is shifting from manufacturing logistics to pure design capability. 8 Takeaways from Carta's State of Seed Report (4 minute read) Seed funding is back to near-peak levels, but the money is significantly harder to touch. Investors are piling more cash into fewer rounds, essentially ignoring everything outside of SF and NY. The most telling stat is headcount: the average seed startup has slimmed down from 10 people to just 6, likely because AI is picking up the slack. However, don't let the tools fool you into going it alone: VCs still fund co-founders at double the rate of solo acts, despite the data showing that 25% of those partnerships end in a breakup by year four. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Driving Extreme Clarity (4 minute read) At your startup, it's important to recognize that a "messy yes" is often more dangerous than a hard "no." Teams tend to drift into "strategic ambiguity" - nodding along to avoid conflict without actually agreeing on the trade-offs. The fix is to force the friction upfront: make the team stack-rank priorities (no ties allowed) and write down exactly where you disagree. RL in Real Life: Durable Moats (15 minute read) Reinforcement learning (RL) has gone from being a promising path towards general intelligence to a taboo phrase over the past year. It has proved highly effective inside AI labs, but outside of the labs, its use cases remain surprisingly underdeveloped. Most efforts appear to be largely experimental and have yet to scratch the surface of the sophisticated techniques used within the frontier labs. This post looks at the intricacies of building effective RL approaches, assesses current use cases, and discusses what founders should consider when commercializing RL-based systems. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Wasting time on CRM data hygiene? This is rarely because of competition. Most of the time, their demise is self-inflicted by internal issues. Funds blindly follow others without thinking for themselves. They lack all conviction. text: TLDR Founders 2025-12-15 Systems of record vs AI 📑, composable software 🚀, employee equity 💰` The tax mistakes first-time founders keep making (Sponsor) Most first-time founders don't think about taxes until it's too late. By then, a wrong entity structure or a mishandled stock option grant can mean thousands in unnecessary tax liability. Carta put together a free webinar with CPAs from Kruze Consulting to walk through the essentials. It's designed for founders who'd rather be building product than deciphering IRS forms. The video covers: Staying compliant from day one (without a full-time accountant) Setting up your entity for tax efficiency How ISOs, NSOs, and RSAs actually work — and what that means for you and your team Watch the recording 📈 Headlines & Trends The AI Value Gap: Where Does the $7,000 Per Seat Go? (2 minute read) OpenAI claims that AI saves the average white-collar worker 54 minutes per day. Currently, vendors capture 10% to 15% of that value, leaving employers and employees with 85% to 90% value capture. This gap suggests that significant pricing power remains untapped. Bundling further complicates the picture - there appears to be a market for best-in-class vertical tools even when bundled alternatives exist. Long Live Systems of Record (8 minute read) Everyone keeps saying AI agents will kill systems of record. The opposite might be true. Ask sales what your ARR is, and you get one number. Ask finance, and you get another. Humans can talk it out - agents can't. The more we automate, the more someone needs to decide what the correct answer actually is. The Age of Composable Software (6 minute read) Software has always faced a brutal trade-off - you can have customization or affordability, never both. Affordability always won. That's why personal computers were never really personal, and most apps follow "one codebase to rule them all." AI changes this math so dramatically that the trade-off might finally break down. Instead of killing software, AI could make it infinitely personalized - built for an end-market of one. The Real State of Seed Today: The Top 10 Learnings from 50,000 Startups, Per Carta's Latest Data (7 minute read) The seed market is healthy but bifurcated. AI companies are operating in a different universe than everyone else, who is competing for a smaller share of attention with leaner teams and longer runways. SAFEs have become completely standardized, and solo founding is increasingly viable, but remains a handicap for fundraising. The fundamentals are still the same: build something people want, get to metrics that matter, and raise when you have the leverage. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Play Rigged Games (3 minute read) Unfair advantages come in three flavors: product insight, go-to-market edge, or technical moats. One common thread among successful founders is an obsession that rational people would walk away from. They solved problems they couldn't stop thinking about. Find your edge and stop fighting fair. How Much Equity Can You Give to Employees Before It's a Problem? (6 minute read) The median public tech company spends 12% of revenue on stock-based compensation. Sounds outrageous until you see that actual shareholder dilution is under 2%. The math is simple - SBC is measured against revenue, dilution against market cap. A company trading at 4x revenue spending 12% on SBC only dilutes 3% of market cap. The companies getting killed aren't spending 20% on SBC. They're the ones growing 5% while pretending they're still a growth story. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Stop renting payments from Stripe (Sponsor) Why promote someone else's brand? White-label payments let you build a revenue stream that you control, keeping your brand stays front and center - while taking the burden of compliance out of the equation. Learn more about white-label payments with NMI Clearitty (Tool) AI-powered sales intelligence with human-verified buyer signals and real-time intent scoring to target only in-market leads and close deals faster. commitify.me (Tool) AI voice agent that calls your phone like a real person to keep you accountable, focused, and on track—no app required. Macaly (Tool) AI platform for creating and managing websites and apps with built-in editing, data tools, and publishing. 🎁 Miscellaneous Essential AI Integration Best Practices for Optimal Performance (29 minute read) Implement migration patterns in AI API calls to maintain flexibility and reliability amid frequent model changes. Utilize service tiers like OpenAI's Flex for cost-efficient backend operations, cutting expenses by up to 50% without sacrificing data quality. Employ rate limiting and circuit breakers to manage resource usage effectively and mitigate unexpected costs or abuse in AI systems. The Map is Not the Territory (4 minute read) VCs treat the power law like a selection tool when it's actually just a description of what happens after the dust settles. They look at seed-stage startups and ask, "Is this a power law company?" - but that's a category error. Uber looked like a black car service for rich people. Airbnb was for travelers who couldn't afford hotels. Both got passed because investors couldn't model a $10B outcome. The irony is that power law returns almost always come from market creation, not capturing predictable share of existing markets. AI Pulls VC and PE Into the Same Orbit (3 minute read) AI is transforming labor-heavy service sectors, putting SMB and mid-market businesses within reach for tech startups and collapsing the historical divide between venture capital and private equity. PE funds are becoming distribution channels for AI products, while founders use PE portfolio pages as idea maps as AI expands TAM for legacy software categories. AI native rollups accelerate the convergence as VC-backed platforms acquire traditional services firms and rebuild them around automated operations. ⚡️ Quick Links 🆕 Salesforce launches its first free-forever CRM for startups (Sponsor) Salesforce's first perpetually free CRM helps companies ditch fragmented spreadsheets. Grow with the world's most trusted platform. No trial, no credit card - it's free. Check it out AI drives record seed funding in 2025 (4 minute read) In 2025, seed funding surged with record-breaking rounds, particularly in AI, and US companies secured nearly half of the global seed investments. Six Big Bets (7 minute read) You don't have to take more risk, just structure your life to be exposed to the upside. Silence Might Be Killing Your Culture (4 minute read) Leaders talk constantly - emails about deadlines, budget updates, workflow changes - but go months without mentioning vision or values. Vibe Coding Won't Kill SaaS (9 minute read) Vibe coding is changing how value is communicated - people will still pay for edge cases, interrogations, and the accumulated wisdom from serving real customers with real problems. Nobody "Wants" AI (2 minute read) Henry Ford's customers didn't want cars, they wanted to get from point A to point B - cars just fit that demand better than horses. title: TLDR Founders 2025-12-22
text: TLDR Founders 2025-12-22 Dashboards vs Pipes 📊, $100M ARR slowdown 📉, company culture 🫂 📈 Headlines & Trends My Biggest Fear in Investing Now: The $100,000,000 ARR Slowdown (7 minute read) The $100M ARR ceiling is real, and it's catching a lot of good companies off guard. Founders who prepare for it can still break through. Do what works now, not what worked back in 2019. Go multiproduct earlier, tap into AI budgets, and build genuine platforms. Why Do Companies Care About Culture? (6 minute read) Imagine a company where any manager can hire whoever they want with no culture screens or founder veto. This thought experiment feels intuitively wrong for a good reason. The average white-collar worker is at least as much an externality to colleagues as an input into production. When outputs can't be properly attributed, rewards for good work are capped, and punishments for laziness are attenuated. Culture steps in to resolve this by making profit-seeking the means to a greater end - something worth existing beyond any individual's tenure or payout. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Dashboard or Pipes? (2 minute read) Dashboard products are used directly by end users as their primary interface for accomplishing tasks. Pipe products are used in the background. Customers rarely interact with them directly after setup. Companies can have both types of products in their portfolio, but products have to choose which camp they're primarily in. The choice will dictate product development, growth strategy, and organizational structure. How To Raise Capital When You Don't Sound Like An Insider (5 minute read) Focus on solving real problems, not just raising capital, to attract investors. Use confident body language, like leaning forward and maintaining stillness, to convey credibility. Leverage pitch competitions for visibility and connections, aiding in building momentum and securing funding. How to Start a Sales Call (5 minute read) After watching roughly 3,000 sales calls, one thing is clear - nobody knows how to start them. The fastest-growing startup this author helped ($0-$6M in under 3 months) attributes its success to "knowing exactly what to look for in discovery." Finding Your First Value Metric (5 minute read) Stop searching for the perfect pricing metric. You need a useful approximation that works today and can evolve. The practical approach - ask customers to rank which value propositions resonate most, then rank which metrics correlate with that value. If the same pairing shows up across multiple interviews, you're onto something. Even if you can't charge on outcomes yet, start tracking them. Create a mock pricing page as a hypothesis and test how prospects react before it's live. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Stop cybersecurity from killing your momentum. 2026 is the year you close bigger deals by actually proving you're secure (Sponsor) 🆕 Enterprise-grade security on an SMB budget. 🆕 Fast-tracked security and compliance, without the headcount. Kobalt.io acts as your plug-and-play security department, managing everything from 24/7 monitoring to compliance. Win enterprise deals without the big company overhead. 🛫 Level up your security now ➡️ Close bigger deals Sparkle (Tool) Sparkle is a cold email outreach tool that handles deliverability, sending infrastructure, and verified leads without requiring manual domain or account setup. Loki.Build (Tool) Loki.Build is an AI-powered landing page builder that generates customizable pages with a live editor, built-in SEO, and hosting. Shadow (Tool) Shadow is a meeting AI that captures both on-screen activity and conversation to generate context-aware summaries and automated follow-up tasks. 🎁 Miscellaneous Creative is the Bottleneck of Modern User Acquisition (4 minute read) User acquisition is no longer constrained by bids, targeting, or channels. It's constrained by creatives. High-spend advertisers don't win because they're more creative - they win because they produce, test, kill, and refresh at a speed most teams can't match. Creative fatigue isn't a vague feeling either. CTR goes down, CPI goes up, and by the time teams align internally, the winner is already dead. Once you scale beyond a few hundred thousand in monthly spend, creative management stops being a creative problem and becomes an infrastructure problem. Understanding Marketers' Needs: A Hierarchy for Success (13 minute read) The article outlines a hierarchy of marketers' data needs, focusing on self-awareness, competitive awareness, industry trends, sentiment, and strategic projection. Podscan's AI onboarding uses this framework to streamline user alert setups by identifying what data matters most at each level. This approach aids customer understanding, improves engagement, and enhances product relevance, especially for varied customer segments like enterprises and solopreneurs. The Proof Narrative - How Investors Evaluate Calls (5 minute read) This article breaks down what investors actually look for on discovery calls. The heart of any investor call is proof - not slides, but evidence. Real customer behavior. Real traction. Scientific markers. Clinical signals. Retention patterns. Proof is anything that shows your company is not a theory. It should be delivered calmly, not urgently. Investors respond to clarity, not hype. When investors say "too early," they almost never mean time. They mean your traction is too thin, or your narrative is ahead of your evidence. The mismatch between ambition and proof creates internal tension that kills deals. ⚡️ Quick Links Timing Your Overnight Success (2 minute read) If it takes three to five years for a project to gain traction, it probably doesn't pay to start something the world knows it needs right now. What Volunteer Open Source Taught Me About Remote Teams (5 minute read) Some tips on how to make distributed volunteer teams productive. How to avoid becoming an “AI-first” company with no real AI usage (6 minute read) Many companies adopt AI in name only, creating pressure to “look innovative” rather than driving real transformation. In An Agentic Era, VC Is Buying A-Player C-Suite Execs At Any Cost (6 minute read) Venture capital now prioritizes acquiring AI-savvy executives from top tech firms, reshaping the investment landscape. title: TLDR Founders 2025-12-24
text: TLDR Founders 2025-12-24 AI context graphs 🌐, SaaS vs AI economics 🤖, coordination tax 💼 📈 Headlines & Trends The Coordination Tax (11 minute read) The structural logic for knowledge work is moving toward smaller teams with larger AI support. Companies that recognize this will operate at cost structures their competitors cannot match. Executives' value will lie in judgment, not coordination. The agent built from years of direct context that exists nowhere else is irreplaceable. Context Graphs Could Become AI's Next Trillion-Dollar Platform (12 minute read) AI agents expose a missing enterprise layer: decision traces that capture exceptions, approvals, and precedent that never make it into systems of record. Startups that sit in the agent execution path can persist these traces at decision time, forming context graphs that explain not just what happened, but why it was allowed. This shifts power away from CRMs and data warehouses toward new systems of record built around decision lineage and autonomy. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The last product to get cancelled (7 minute read) The 'vitamin vs painkiller' metaphor is a prime example of perfect hindsight, non-actionable startup advice. No founder sets out intending to build a vitamin, it's just difficult to come up with actionable tests of what's what. This article presents some concrete tests to help founders create more certainty. The advice is likely most helpful for B2B founders currently navigating product-market fit and early sales. This week on How I AI: How to get your whole team excited about AI (2 minute read) Brian Greenbaum, product designer at Pendo, became an AI advocate after a Slack message on paternity leave, leading workshops and implementing tools across teams. His approach includes rapid experimentation with AI tools and offers a playbook for organizational adoption, enhancing creativity and strategy influence. Becoming an AI champion, as Greenbaum emphasizes, significantly boosts career opportunities. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Super Agents by ClickUp (Tool) Super Agents by ClickUp are configurable AI agents that can be created, assigned, and managed directly within ClickUp workflows to automate tasks and coordination. Aident AI (Tool) Aident AI is a natural-language automation tool that converts plain English instructions into agent-based workflows and scripts across connected tools. /agent by Firecrawl (Tool) /agent by Firecrawl is an API that autonomously searches, navigates, and extracts structured data from complex websites based on a data description. 🎁 Miscellaneous $3B to Zero - How Luminar Collapsed (6 minute read) Austin Russell became one of the youngest self-made billionaires when Luminar went public in 2020. By December 2025, the lidar company filed for Chapter 11. This article digs into what happened in between - Luminar bet everything on Volvo, which kept expanding its order from 39,500 sensors to 1.1 million over three years. Then, Volvo made lidar optional instead of standard, cutting volumes by 90%. There's a lot to unpack here about concentration risk, why "lifetime volume" projections aren't the same as purchase orders, and what happens when your only customer changes their mind. The Real Economics of SaaS versus AI Companies (8 minute read) AI companies need higher revenue or pricing power to match SaaS profit per customer due to structurally lower margins. AI can tap larger budgets, but must rely on volume, pricing power, and efficiencies in costs like compute to be competitive. The math hasn't changed, only the economic inputs have, making understanding these differences crucial for evaluating financial outcomes in AI versus SaaS. ⚡️ Quick Links The Shape of The Game We Play (20 minute read) The rapid interest rate hikes in 2022 reduced global liquidity, causing many startups to struggle or fail, highlighting the shift from a low-cost capital environment. The Product Model at Google (16 minute read) Google has more than nine products, each with more than one billion monthly active users, none of which represent new categories. Startups Using AI Have a Problem: Anyone Can Copy Their Awesome Idea (2 minute read) Maor Shlomo, founder of the AI startup Base44, warns that 'vibe coding' makes it easy for competitors to quickly replicate ideas. Why $1-10M ARR is the Hardest (12 minute read) Martin Roth, revenue leader at Levelset, breaks down what actually changes as sales teams scale, where teams tend to overcorrect, and why structure and incentives still beat tools, even in the current AI era. title: TLDR Founders 2026-01-02
text: TLDR Founders 2026-01-02 College dropout founders 🎒, AI research trends 🤖, solo founders 🚀 How this AI startup closes its books by 1:35 PM - with two finance people (Sponsor) In the latest Unpack Pricing podcast , Metronome CEO Scott Woody talks with Nicolas Kopp, founder of Rillet (AI-native ERP, backed by Sequoia, a16z, and Iconiq) about how AI is overhauling finance operations. The conversation covers how companies like Windsurf scaled to $100M+ with a tiny finance team, why 20 years of experience might now be a hiring red flag , and how pricing changes that used to take 6 months can now happen in 2 hours. Kopp also shares why legacy ERPs have a –3 NPS, and what's replacing them. Listen to the full episode → 📈 Headlines & Trends The Race to Run Businesses Autonomously (2 minute read) Cofounder, by The General Intelligence Company, enables businesses to run autonomously through a network of coordinating agents that execute repeatable workflows known as Flows. The platform employs layered memory architectures to consolidate information, allowing agents to operate independently with comprehensive knowledge. This approach aims to reduce manual repetitive work and enable continuous, insightful, and autonomous business operations. 'College dropout' has become the most coveted startup founder credential (3 minute read) The trend of valuing college dropouts as startup founders has resurged amid the AI boom, with many pitching it as a credential during Y Combinator Demo Days. Despite high-profile examples and fears of missing critical AI opportunities, most successful startup founders hold degrees. VCs prioritize traits such as commitment and social networks over dropout status, with some emphasizing the value of wisdom gained from experience. Four AI research trends enterprise teams should watch in 2026 (8 minute read) Key AI research trends to watch in 2026 include continual learning, world models, orchestration, and refinement. Continual learning focuses on updating models without retraining, while world models enable AI to understand environments autonomously. Orchestration enhances efficiency by coordinating multiple tools, and refinement improves outputs iteratively through feedback. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Build the Whole Product or Lose the Customer (3 minute read) Winning startups in 2026 will replace fragmented toolchains by solving an entire end-to-end problem for a tightly defined customer segment. The strongest teams will compete across categories to deliver a single, integrated solution that beats stitching together multiple incumbents on cost, speed, and experience. Square proved the model by compressing payments, hardware, and POS into one system, cutting the total cost of ownership and capturing value across the stack. 25 Lessons from the First Year of Solo Founders (5 minute read) Solo founding is more viable than most assume. Key lessons: you can achieve significant traction alone, momentum beats co-founder searching, and early focus on the core product matters. Solo founders benefit from generous equity, contractors-to-hires, and multitasking enabled by AI. Mindset is crucial: endurance, pacing, and learning from small pivots drive success. Emotional support and realistic benchmarks prevent burnout. Fundraising, timing, and terms matter, but execution outweighs optics. Ultimately, persistence and continuous iteration define survival and growth. Is Community the Most Overlooked Edge in Venture Capital? (9 minute read) Community roles in VC are evolving beyond event planning to strategic network development, becoming essential for mapping emerging networks and unlocking investment opportunities. The need to treat community as infrastructure is driving firms to institutionalize these roles, leveraging relationships to find untapped potential before consensus forms. Measuring meaningful metrics like founders sourced and pre-empted rounds will crystallize community as a strategic discipline rather than a peripheral function. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Friendware (Tool) Friendware is a macOS productivity tool that provides system-wide tab-to-complete suggestions to reduce context switching. Creaibo (Tool) Creaibo is an AI content creation tool that helps creators develop and produce written and visual content while maintaining a consistent personal style. Foundire (Tool) Foundire is a hiring platform that automates first-round interviews using AI-driven candidate screening, interviews, and structured evaluation scorecards. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Navan IPO Reminds Us: AI is On Fire. Everything Else in B2B? Still Really, Really Hard (9 minute read) Navan's stock dropped 20% on day one when it went public in Q4 last year. It is still trading 32% below its IPO price. Navan is a legitimate B2B and SaaS category leader - but it isn't AI. Simply being a SaaS company is no longer a ticket to a premium valuation, unless you're building AI infrastructure, AI models, or AI agents. The Case Against Doomerism (4 minute read) 2026 marks the mainstream arrival of AI capabilities that feel like “magic,” making many traditional roles, like PMs, appear obsolete. Doomerism stems from outdated experiences with early AI tools, but avoiding these tools limits growth. Drawing lessons from chess, the author argues that leveraging AI enhances judgment and output, while refusal only widens the gap. Fear of dependency, not capability, is the real challenge. Participation now shapes the future; opting out leaves you behind. ⚡️ Quick Links What it's like to work at a profitable AI startup built for the trades (17 minute read) Hearth's completely automatic revenue engine allows contractors to focus on what they do best. Does valuation or ownership matter more to a seed VC? Yes (3 minute read) Seed VCs prioritize ownership over valuation, targeting 10–15% ownership per investment, but a focus on pricing can influence portfolio strategy. The Toll of Stress on Startup Teams and Its Link to Founder Well-being (2 minute read) Stress and burnout are significant but overlooked factors that affect startup performance. The Next Thing Will Not Be Big (10 minute read) Open source developers should dedicate more energy to medium- or small-scale open source projects. title: TLDR Founders 2026-01-05
text: TLDR Founders 2026-01-05 Carta’s state of startups 📚, finding technical cofounder 👋, solo founders flippening 🚀 Don't wait for leads to come to you. Find them with Seamless.AI (Sponsor) In a crowded marketplace, you need to reach your buyers first. Seamless powers B2B lead gen with accurate contact info and intent signals for 1.3 billion decision makers. 👉 1.8 billion+ emails researched. 🤙 414 million+ phone numbers. 👋 121 million+ companies identified. 🙌 Flexible plans with startup-friendly pricing. Find and close more deals in 2026 📈 Headlines & Trends Authority Is the AI Bottleneck (2 minute read) We are moving from "Assistive" to "Authoritative" software. The current bottleneck for AI isn't intelligence, but permission. For software to scale from a tool to a worker, it needs to be treated as a "closeable system object" - granted the authority to execute decisions (like issuing a refund) without human approval. The next wave of value isn't in smarter models, but in the guardrails that allow enterprises to safely remove humans from the loop. State of Startups 2025 (2 minute read) The market is moving at two speeds. Carta's massive dataset reveals a split ecosystem: capital is flowing freely again, but only to the most legible founders, while everyone else is left fighting for scraps. The report highlights a liquidity crunch where IPOs remain scarce, forcing a shift toward secondary transactions. The Solo Founder Flippening (3 minute read) One-third of all startups launched in 2025 were solo-founded. This represents a 53% increase since 2019. AI tools now allow individuals to handle technical or operational scope that previously required a founding team. Despite this, VCs still fund co-founder pairs at double the rate of solos, even though the data suggests solo founders often reach profitability with less capital. The "co-founder default" appears to be fading as software leverage increases. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Finding a Technical Co-Founder (3 minute read) Engineers convert on momentum, not vision. Technical talent is often flooded with pitches from “ideas people.” A better strategy is to pitch traction. Potential co-founders respond better when a project is de-risked - whether through secured partnerships, paid customers, or a functional no-code prototype. You aren't recruiting them to start something - you are recruiting them to scale something that already works. Focus (3 minute read) Accepting a bad fit customer destroys option value. Taking on the wrong client consumes the resources needed to find three good ones. Focus is less about prioritization and more about the active destruction of options. Stop trying to improve every metric by 1% to create the space to improve the single most important metric by 30%. How Base44 Sold for $80M in 6 Months (3 minute read) Zero to exit in 180 days. Maor Shlomo bootstrapped Base44 to an $80M all-cash exit by using AI to generate nearly 100% of the frontend code. This allowed the founder to ship a "batteries included" platform (database, auth, and backend pre-wired) where users built apps via text prompts. By focusing entirely on the magic moment - getting a user to a working app in under 60 seconds - the company hit $3.5M ARR with zero ad spend. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Altrina (Tool) Turn demonstrations into reliable browser automations in 5 minutes. Brief My Meeting (Tool) Brief My Meeting is an open-source tool that sends AI-generated briefing emails with participant context and relevant materials before scheduled meetings. Loki (Tool) Create landing pages so good no one believes they're AI‑made. 🎁 Miscellaneous Will vs. Skill (4 minute read) You are likely solving the wrong problem with your struggling employees. The Will vs. Skill matrix exposes a common management trap where founders apply the wrong remedy to underperformance. When an enthusiastic junior employee fails, you might try to inspire them, but a pep talk is useless if they simply lack the technical training. Conversely, micromanaging a bored expert who has lost interest will only drive them away. Lasers, AI, and Inertia (5 minute read) Better technology often loses to organizational fear. At SpaceX, handheld laser welders sat gathering dust for months despite being objectively superior to the old methods. The engineers were not waiting for the tech to improve, they were paralyzed by the career risk of trying something new and failing. Adoption only happened when the pain of staying the same - specifically, a production backlog and an angry Elon - finally outweighed the friction of switching. This is the missing link in AI adoption. Success does not come from having the smartest model, but from finding the specific pressure points where the fear of doing nothing finally exceeds the fear of changing workflows. How Does Brand Marketing Fit In? (6 minute read) Startups often overestimate the impact of brand marketing when growth plateaus, mistakenly believing awareness or positioning will drive significant gains. Instead, founders should focus on optimizing demand capture and measuring marketing effectiveness, like performance spend. Brand marketing should be considered only at massive scale or when competition necessitates it, not as an early-stage growth strategy. ⚡️ Quick Links A decade of building Amper — and its next chapter (12 minute read) Amper, which was founded to democratize access to modern shop-floor technology for SMBs, has joined forces with ECI Solutions to scale that mission even further. The Willingness to Start Over (1 minute read) Most founders get rich once and immediately become terrified of looking like beginners again. The Counterfactual Principle (1 minute read) Peter Thiel's ultimate test for a startup is whether the future depends on your existence. How Much Equity to Give Your First Employees: The Real Data from 50,000 Startups (5 minute read) The equity curve is steeper than most founders think: the first hire gets nearly five times what the fifth hire gets at the median. Happy New Year + Six Contrarian Predictions for 2026 (5 minute read) The biggest opportunities belong to founders and investors who are willing to trade noise for nuance and speed for depth. For twenty years, SaaS pricing was easy: count the humans and multiply by thirty dollars. AI breaks this because its entire purpose is to reduce the number of humans needed to do the work. We are returning to an era of complex, value-based pricing. Everyone Is Excited About Claude (2 minute read) We are watching the democratization of competence in real time. The excitement around tools like Claude Code is about the sudden acceleration of human potential. We are crossing a threshold where software no longer just assists but actively up-levels the user, allowing a mid-level engineer to perform like a senior architect. This signals a massive explosion in software creation from people who previously lacked the technical skill to build what they could imagine. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The AI Product Design Playbook (4 minute read) Your model is not your moat. In a world where intelligence is a commodity, the only defensible advantages are proprietary data and deep workflow integration. AI-first startups often fail because they obsess over the tech rather than the user pain. The winning design pattern is not a chatbot - it is a system that absorbs a messy, complex human workflow and makes it invisible. If your product is just a text box, you are just a wrapper. Your #1 Job For January? Deploy An AI Agent — Yourself. Hands On Keyboard (11 minute read) Sales, marketing, and GTM leaders need to learn how to deploy their own AI agents. The leaders who will win in the AI era are those who have done the work themselves. While AI jargon may be intimidating, the actual work isn't that hard. This post provides some tips on how to deploy a fully custom AI agent. How to Build a Waitlist (4 minute read) A waitlist is not a parking lot - it is a validation engine. Most founders treat waitlists as a collection of email addresses, but they are actually a tool for testing promises. You do not need a product to build one - you just need a clear, specific promise of an outcome. The goal is not to get ten thousand signups - it is to start twenty real conversations with people who feel the pain so acutely they are willing to wait for the cure. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Drive brand awareness with tech decision makers at scale (Sponsor) TLDR puts you in front of your target audience with almost no competition. Companies like Serval, Runware, Rillet, Yoodli, and FAL reported exponential increases in ARR, showcasing robust traction in the AI sector. This rapid growth underscores a shift in how startups secure capital, setting a new baseline for performance amid escalating competition. The Case for Abundance (3 minute read) Doomerism is a self-fulfilling prophecy. The current narrative around AI safety often acts as a filter, scaring away the exact thoughtful, conscientious builders we need while leaving the field open to the reckless. Abundance - of code, intelligence, and art - should be the default state of the future. You do not get a vote on how that future is shaped by refusing to participate in the present. Optimism is a moral imperative to ensure the good version gets built. This shift highlights evolving workforce demands and raises questions about future job competitiveness in an AI-driven economy. ⚡️ Quick Links Founders should trust vibes in hiring (3 minute read) Early-stage founders should explicitly value interpersonal chemistry alongside skills and interviews, since startups require deep trust and fast conflict resolution. The Joy of Delegating to Competence (2 minute read) The best management hack is hiring people who don't need managing. text: TLDR Founders 2026-01-09 AI in GTM 🤖, SaaS pricing 🏧, how to recruit A-players 📚 The first CRM built for founders, not admins (Sponsor) CRMs only work if you keep them updated. You don't have time for that. Lightfield is an AI-native CRM with a built-in call recorder that keeps every record up to date. No manual data entry, ever. Used by 1,000+ startups, Lightfield gives you: Automated meeting prep built from account history Post-call tasks and follow-ups after every meeting An AI agent that researches accounts and drafts emails A ChatGPT-like experience on top of your data Get started in minutes. Just connect your email and calendar and watch your CRM build itself. Try it for free today. 📈 Headlines & Trends What's Working Now in GTM AI: How Rally Reimagined Win Analysis with AI (5 minute read) Win-loss analysis is high leverage but notoriously hard to do well. Buyers don't tell you the full truth, sellers have blind spots, and scheduling interviews takes forever. AI can reconstruct win-loss insights from sales call recordings alone. Feed your Gong transcripts into a custom GPT, and it will surface patterns across dozens of deals faster than any manual process. What's working in SaaS pricing right now (15 minute read) Credit-based pricing exploded 126% in 2025 because it splits the difference between predictable licenses and pure usage. Customers can budget, while vendors protect margins on volatile AI costs. Meanwhile, per-seat pricing isn't dying like everyone predicted. It's evolving. OpenAI and Figma now tie seats to capability tiers and product access. Salesforce lets customers swap unused licenses for AI credits. Nobody knows what AI is actually worth yet, so the winners are offering multiple ways to pay. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Local ≠ small brand impact (8 minute read) Founders never ask how to build a local press strategy, and that's a mistake. If you're trying to reach small business owners, educators, or non-technical professionals, the Boston Globe might outperform TechCrunch. Local publications punch above their weight on Facebook, where community stories spread fast through geographic networks. The playbook is simple. Find customers in target cities, hear their stories, then pitch their story to local reporters. You get awareness, they get visibility. Local journalists don't need proof that your company matters to the world. They need proof it matters to their city. Stop Hiring for Expertise: How to Recruit A-Players (6 minute read) A polished resume from Google often can't ship anything at your startup. That's because you hired an expert, not a builder. The hiring framework that actually works: evaluate drive first, then potential, then expertise. Drive is the internal engine you can't coach or install. Potential is how fast someone climbs. Expertise only matters after the first two spike. To test for drive, don't even look at the résumé before the interview: ask what they've obsessed over, when the hardest they've ever worked was, and why. If someone has never been deeply consumed by anything, they won't suddenly become consumed by your company. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Rube (Tool) Rube is a universal MCP that lets AI agents take actions across 600+ applications directly from chat. High-agency individuals treat life as an experiment, use AI and tools as amplifiers rather than crutches, and build generalist capabilities to thrive across changing industries. Developing agency requires setting goals, experimenting, analyzing results, and creating your own processes, enabling long-term adaptability, skill acquisition, and control over your future. Why AI Startups Must Relearn Growth Every Quarter (1 minute read) Product-market fit resets every three months at Lovable because AI capabilities and user expectations shift too fast to rely on long-term scaling, even past $200M ARR. Roughly 95% of growth comes from shipping new features, while funnel optimization contributes only about 5%, forcing teams to prioritize velocity over polish. Influencer-driven demos, free credits, and large communities now outperform paid ads, making word of mouth the primary growth engine. 25 Things I Believe In to Build Great Products (11 minute read) Speed is the only moat, and focus is a superpower. Build fast feedback loops, and say no 10 times more than you say yes. The product is more important than the process, so build minimum viable plans, practice prototype-first development, obsess about the details, and simplify product reviews. Seek the truth and be willing to be wrong. Hire builders, not bureaucrats aiming to climb the career ladder. ⚡️ Quick Links Why grinding with brute force can be very dangerous for a startup (2 minute read) Taking 8-10 calls a day feels productive until you realize you're optimizing for movement instead of direction. On the retention side, AI is commoditizing the value props most SaaS companies were built on. Founders don't need to be operators anymore. They need to be systems architects. How I actually get good advice (5 minute read) Most founders take advice wrong. They ask for solutions when they should be asking for diagnoses. The person giving you advice will probably be right 50% of the time on solutions, but they'll nail the actual problem because problems don't vary as much as solutions do. Pick a topic where you have ideas but not conviction, find 4 people who've done it, and cold message them with a specific question. Before every call, write a one-pager summarizing where you are and what you want to ask. This is the single most important thing. The rapid transition from SaaS to AI intensifies these issues, as businesses struggle with outdated sales strategies, unmet AI demand, overhyped products, and leadership disconnected from fast-paced technological changes. Success hinges on building systems that address new constraints, prioritize trust, and focus on measurable outcomes rather than mere output. Don't Confuse Market Share with Winning (4 minute read) Market share is a point estimate, not a scoreboard. When the iPhone launched in 2007, Apple's share was negligible next to Nokia and BlackBerry. Today, Apple holds less than 25% of the global smartphone share. No one questions who's winning. DoorDash entered food delivery late, after Grubhub, Seamless, and Caviar. When Uber Eats tried to buy market share with heavy subsidies, DoorDash focused on building a logistics engine for suburban markets that competitors ignored. The pattern across Sequoia's portfolio is consistent: outliers don't chase market share. Pitch your fellow founder (5 minute read) Founder-led podcasts, newsletters, and livestreams are the most underutilized channels for early-stage startups. These platforms care less about scale and more about perspective. One accounting software startup runs a weekly livestream where founders announce products, share funding news, and talk through what's actually working. The bar for getting on is different than traditional press. You need a news hook, a distinct point of view, or expertise other founders can apply. One AI startup got on with a launch, a three-month demo waitlist, and a yacht party. The pitch was a casual DM. On Pricing, Pilots, and Early-Stage Enterprise Startups (5 minute read) Early-stage enterprise AI founders should focus on the type of company they want to build rather than obsessing over specific pricing models. The key tradeoff is between high-volume growth versus high-price, low-volume niches, which shapes GTM strategy, sales motions, and churn risk. Design pilots deliberately with internal champions, scoped outcomes, and clear exit criteria, while pricing based on controllable factors like usage or memory to reduce surprises. Multiple tiers, add-ons, and explicit SLAs give startups leverage with enterprise buyers. You get production-ready enterprise capabilities without owning the complexity yourself.Trusted by OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, Vercel, and 1000+ more. Your first million MAUs are free. Add enterprise features today → 📈 Headlines & Trends The Year of Multi-Modal (4 minute read) Text was the fastest path to usefulness for AI, but it was always a middle state. Humans don't experience the world in text. Work doesn't happen in text. What's changing now is that multi-modal models are good enough, inference costs are low enough for always-on perception, and cameras, microphones, and sensors are making the physical world legible to machines. Once AI can see, hear, and react continuously, text-only AI will feel as constrained as desktop-only software felt once mobile took over. Will AI Kill Fintech SaaS? (15 minute read) AI advancements challenge Fintech SaaS by enabling rapid, cost-effective software development. However, real value remains in data, regulatory relationships, and handling complex edge cases at scale—categories agents can't easily replicate. Fintech companies with strong data moats and network effects will thrive, but must adapt quickly to maintain their competitive edge. The Hamster Wheel Has Engineers (13 minute read) Some products will eventually mature to the point where forward-deployed engineers (FDEs) are no longer required. Others, particularly in AI, might have FDEs permanently as a feature. Products where value comes from applying judgment, recognizing patterns, and doing things that can't be reduced to a configuration screen may require human expertise to be part of the product forever. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Stripe's Lighthouse Hiring pattern (4 minute read) Lighthouse Hiring is the idea that hiring well-connected people makes subsequent hires both easier and higher quality. Spending the extra time to land one 'lighthouse hire' can improve the quality and velocity of any hiring push. Not all of these people will be well-known - they are just widely-connected. This strategy isn't without its downside - it takes some nuance to deploy effectively. Brex and The Pros and Cons of Hubristic Fundraising (18 minute read) Hubristic fundraising is where companies raise so aggressively that they are essentially betting the entire future of the company on hitting an extraordinary outcome. These deals attract the best talent and capital, create customer and partner gravity, generate relentless press coverage, and demoralize competitors, but the talent is usually mercenary, any stumble becomes a 'failure', decision-making is warped, and the bar for success becomes close to impossible. The Age of AI is seeing hubristic fundraising return at a scale that makes 2021 look quaint. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Data Leap (Tool) You describe the work, and this tool turns it into automation. Content creator skills translate a lot to quality marketing and growth. Hiring someone with a big audience is practically bringing in a new distribution channel. How to build a startup people actually care about (8 minute read) The next era of tech brands that will win will be the ones that become something akin to a lifestyle brand. They will stand for something clear, and that something will show up everywhere. Tech has historically been bad at this, but it is now integrated deeply in people's lives, and the market and user demand have grown. People will soon associate themselves with tech brands as they do with brands like Nike. Soon, brands will do more of the work than the products themselves. ⚡️ Quick Links The future of software engineering is SRE (5 minute read) Software engineering is shifting towards Site Reliability Engineering as operational excellence becomes crucial. Meetings are exploding (and stealing maker time): over 3 years, meetings are ~2x per person and ~6x per org. Tool sprawl is real: people use 18 apps/day on average — a direct driver of context switching and fragmented attention. Read the report 📈 Headlines & Trends The Future of AI Creative Tools (20 minute read) FLORA, an AI-powered creative tool company, raised $42 million in Series A funding to give professional creatives more control over their workflows. Founder Weber Wong criticized Silicon Valley for misunderstanding creatives' needs, emphasizing the importance of taste and control in creative work. The platform's node-based interface supports companies like Nike and Lionsgate by integrating generative models into customizable workflows, enabling both manual and autonomous creation. Entire industries are now decided in <2 years (3 minute read) Becoming the industry default used to take 20 years. That's how long it took for "no one gets fired for buying IBM" to become true. With AI, that timeline is under two. Harvey signed A&O Shearman when it was four people, and that one deal made it credible enough to become the default in legal AI. Landing a big customer and building industry credibility aren't separate phases - they feed each other. Most startups treat them as sequential. The ones who lock in the default do both at once. Trends I'm seeing in AI pricing (4 minute read) AI pricing is getting harder as agents are running longer and more autonomously. People dislike usage-based billing because it is unpredictable, and per-seat pricing doesn't map cleanly to AI value. One pricing model that has prevailed in 'credit', where customers pre-purchase tokens for work. Credits will likely stick around, but they still don't make AI spend feel predictable, as agents can do wildly unpredictable amounts of work. AI products prioritizing billing observability would make AI feel more like software and less like a casino. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Management as AI superpower (11 minute read) An experimental class at the University of Pennsylvania demonstrated how management and AI tools like Claude Code and ChatGPT can accelerate startup development. Students rapidly created diverse prototypes by leveraging AI for market research, financial modeling, and ideation, effectively cutting down traditional startup timelines. Successful delegation to AI hinges on balancing task complexity, AI success probability, and evaluation time, emphasizing the value of management skills in optimizing AI productivity. My 3 Criteria for Success in Early-Stage Companies (3 minute read) Netflix co-founder Marc Randolph argues that the single biggest determinant of early-stage startup success is the team, emphasizing execution, adaptability, and persistence over product or funding. He explains that talent operates on a power curve and shares practical advice on attracting and retaining exceptional people, including hiring for future impact, cutting toxic high performers, and treating employees with trust and honesty. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Reach real buyers in TLDR instead of fighting for attention on paid social (Sponsor) TLDR puts you in front of your target audience with almost no competition. It sounded ridiculous but worked brilliantly. The role attracted high-agency, high-EQ engineers who might otherwise have seen client-facing work as beneath them. The title gave the work prestige, and Palantir got a hiring moat. Now, Harvey is doing the same thing with "legal engineer." The test for whether this is real or just title inflation: does the new title describe work that would have been unrecognizable five years ago? "Prompt engineer" already feels dated because the work turned out to be a feature of every job rather than a job itself. Is Your Margin My Opportunity in Software? (3 minute read) AWS's operating margins ranged between 25% to 38% between 2015 and 2025. The company prints money at scale even after a decade of cloud commoditization. Its moat is capex, and few other companies have the balance sheet to compete. text: TLDR Founders 2026-01-30 AI fundraising resets 💸, AI GTM reality check 📊, PMF is lagging indicator 🤝 📈 Headlines & Trends What's working in AI for GTM (10 minute read) 53% of GTM leaders are seeing little to no impact from AI. This report interviewed 30 who are and compiled 40 of their best workflows across content, prospecting, and sales. Most can be built with ChatGPT or Claude plus off-the-shelf tools. The report walks through step-by-step examples with exact prompts. The New Rules for Fundraising in the AI Era (8 minute read) Vivek Ramaswami and colleagues at Madrona outline how AI has disrupted traditional fundraising benchmarks. Rapid spikes in user growth and revenue no longer signal durable success, so investors now focus on momentum, unit economics, clear expansion pathways, and customer dependence. Founders must prove that growth is sustainable, customers are genuinely locked in, and their business has a defensible trajectory rather than just hitting arbitrary ARR milestones. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics PMF is a Lagging Indicator. Start With Habit Formation Instead (5 minute read) Grant Lee argues that Product-Market Fit is a lagging metric and advises founders to focus first on whether they are building recurring habits rather than one-off solutions. By targeting frequent, low-friction problems and designing products that users invest in quickly, startups can create sticky behaviors that drive long-term engagement. Examples like Slack and Gamma illustrate how habit-focused design outperforms solutions that only address infrequent or high-friction needs. Building AI products: Lessons from Productboard Spark (22 minute read) The fundamentals of product management haven't changed. Building AI products is still about solving real problems for real people. Start by matching customer pain to tech capabilities, build prototypes fast, measure quality rigorously, and evolve your team as you scale. The teams that ship great AI products will be the ones that learned fastest from what didn't work. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Imagine (Tool) Imagine is an AI-powered builder that turns ideas into fully functional, secure, and scalable applications with built-in cloud infrastructure and compliance features. Ada (Tool) Ada is an AI data analyst that creates professional reports from any dataset. Decipher (Tool) Decipher AI generates your E2E tests, maintains them as you ship, and catches issues before customers do. 🎁 Miscellaneous Software in a post-abundance world (10 minute read) AI is transforming software production by making it cheaper and faster, leading to smaller teams, reduced margins, and shifting value from code creation to decision-making and integration. This shift challenges old economic models where large teams thrived under abundant capital and growth incentives, pushing the industry towards leaner operations and disposable software. Capital now focuses on leveraging AI in areas like healthcare and manufacturing, where software integration with domain expertise is harder to replicate, refining the industry's focus from quantity to strategic depth and adaptability. The Fundability Trap (17 minute read) Fundability is the measure of how well a startup fits with the current venture consensus. Investability is a measure of potential outcome and financial return. VCs that conflate the two or don't recognize the difference risk building an entire ecosystem that rewards fundability. Chasing fundability over investability has never been a reliable path to outlier outcomes and fund returns. ⚡️ Quick Links AEO: Optimize for the follow up (4 minute read) Traditional SEO treated search as a transaction. Re-pricing the SaaS Bond (4 minute read) Software will either converge to something bond-like or something closer to a normal operating business. $281b From One Customer (2 minute read) Microsoft's Q2 FY2026 earnings impressed with $81.3 billion revenue and Azure's 39% growth, though the stock fell due to capacity constraints. Don't solve problems that don't exist. (7 minute read) Avoid solving nonexistent problems by focusing only on what's essential for progress and value creation. Investor confidence in the SaaS business model is fracturing as AI disruption risks threaten the retention rates and terminal value assumptions that powered the last decade of software investing. Teams need to shift investment toward deeper customer insight and research to answer the fundamental question before building anything. The Best AI Startups Do the Training For You. It's On You to Make the Agent Awesome — Not Your Customers (10 minute read) The old model was to sell the software and let the customer figure it out. Businesses now need to do the work to make their AI great before they hand over the keys. AI agents will fail if improperly contained. It is up to the AI vendor to make it work. Customers shouldn't have to get agents to work. What TAM Should Actually Tell You (5 minute read) Stop calculating “potential customers × potential price” and start with what the world currently spends solving your problem. That's your actual market today. Everything beyond that isn't market size, it's a thesis about growth. The unwritten oath between founders, employees, and investors is dead. When picking your next company, ask your VC who else they've backed in your category and what happened to employees at the founder's last company. The playbook isn't “envision a radically different future and build it,” it's figure out a real need by selling, learn by iterating on what doesn't work, avoid trying to predict the future or convince markets, and stay in the game long enough to catch a demand wave when it hits. ⚡️ Quick Links B2CC - Claude Code is your customer (13 minute read) We are now shifting into an 'agents as customers' era - companies need to be thinking about how to become the vendor that AI agents prefer. Growth-stage software no longer requires or generates capital in the way early SaaS or consumer internet companies did, and AI companies increasingly need infrastructure financing, not conventional venture funding. Institutional VC is constrained by LP mandates, fund timelines, and deployment expectations, forcing investment even when better opportunities would be to wait. As a result, funds invent narratives to justify current positions while real liquidity remains scarce due to limited IPOs and conservative M&A markets. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Finding GTM alpha (10 minute read) 'Alpha' is an investment's outperformance over a market benchmark. It is what separates market-beating investors from the rest. GTM alpha starts with having better data than your competitors. Unique data is a competitive edge. Top teams stay ahead by experimenting, learning, and launching better plays faster. How to Build AI Product Sense (12 minute read) Product managers and founders can develop AI intuition by using tools like Cursor for non-technical work. This interactive guide walks through practical exercises that help you understand AI capabilities and limitations firsthand. The best way to know what AI can do for your product is to experience it yourself rather than relying on demos or documentation. When to Raise VC (3 minute read) Venture capital requires extremely high returns, often 50x, which rules out businesses in small or focused markets that can still generate strong profit and exits. Forcing a VC narrative onto a business misaligned with hypergrowth pressures leads to wasted time and compromised product decisions. Founders should match their financing strategy to market opportunity, using angels or bootstrapping for steady growth and VCs only when capital materially changes outcomes. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Polyvia (Tool) Polyvia is a Visual Knowledge Index that turns large collections of images into a queryable, disambiguated source of truth for multimodal agents and knowledge workflows. moltbook (Tool) moltbook is a social network for AI agents where agents can share, discuss, and upvote content. Early capital from angel investors results in more viable companies that are more likely to succeed. The fully loaded cost including media, production, talent, and additional spend is $16–29M. Success is measured both short-term through customer acquisition and traffic spikes, and long-term through brand awareness and marketing efficiency. The upside is asymmetric and compounding while downside is capped. Ro chose this moment because the product is ready, the market is large and underserved, and Serena's story aligns with the brand. ⚡️ Quick Links We've been quietly building our own 0 -> 1 revenue stack internally at @tryramp - Ramp Revenue (2 minute read) Sellers at Ramp don't have to worry about switching between dozens of systems just to figure out who they should reach out to or what they should say. Get Started 📈 Headlines & Trends How Markets Price AI Risk (3 minute read) Vertical software stocks are down 43% from their highs. DevTools are down 21%. The gap doesn't track with which sectors AI can actually replace - it correlates almost perfectly with growth rates. Slow growers (8-11%) are getting crushed while faster growers (21-22%) are holding up. The market isn't pricing in AI disruption. It's pricing in who can outrun it. Churn/Retention in the Era of AI (2 minute read) AI companies are fighting churn and facing a retention apocalypse. The real retention curve starts when the hobbyists leave, leaving only the people who've integrated AI into actual work. AI churn is higher because it's easy to buy and cancel, and many trials reflect curiosity more than long-term commitment. Companies that win move upmarket, build for habitual workflows, and add services/FDEs on top of AI. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Uplevel or Replace? A CEO's Dilemma at Series B (5 minute read) ZoomInfo was built from $0 to IPO, and its founder has a nuanced take on the "when do you upgrade your early team" question. His answer: every exec was a first-timer once, and external hires aren't automatically better. The real question is whether someone can do what the business needs for the next 12-24 months, not whether they've done it before. Endgame Play (8 minute read) Modern systems optimize by simulating terminal states, collapsing openings and middlegames into scripted execution, from chess engines to modern warfare and AI strategy. This endgame-first reasoning treats real-world uncertainty as a series of bottlenecks to skip, rewarding fluency in simulation over engagement with unresolved complexity. The failure mode emerges when actors mistake the ability to model outcomes for proximity to them, abandoning the middlegame where risk, ambiguity, and real consequences still concentrate. Two Modes of Storytelling: Studio and Newsroom (6 minute read) Studio mode focuses on slow, deliberate storytelling with deep craft and novel perspectives, producing rare, timeless content that gains momentum through careful refinement. Newsroom mode prioritizes fast, responsive storytelling, engaging with ongoing conversations and surfacing insights while the moment is alive. The most effective storytellers cycle between the two, letting newsroom responsiveness inform studio work and studio depth amplify newsroom relevance, creating a feedback loop that balances craft with timeliness. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Stop losing deals to forgotten follow-ups. The best operators don't start confident - they iterate until they actually know what they're doing, and the real confidence follows naturally. You can build a $10B+ company fully remotely (16 minute read) All large companies work remotely - the only people truly working 'in person' at any of these companies are the ones sitting within earshot. Everyone else is collaborating through screens. Hire people with agency who want to work hard. Employees who prefer remote work just want to do their best work in an environment that suits them. The fear of the future is directly proportional to the smallness of your ambitions. If your plan is to maintain the status quo, AI is terrifying. text: TLDR Founders 2026-02-13 PE exit closes 🚪, AI monetization 💵, lighthouse playbook 💡 📈 Headlines & Trends PE Is No Longer Coming to the Rescue (4 minute read) $587B in tech M&A flowed in 2025 - the highest in a decade. However, strip out five megadeals and average deal sizes actually dropped 17%. The tech bond distressed ratio hit 9.5%, the highest since Q4 2023. If you're building a solid-but-not-spectacular SaaS company, the PE escape hatch is closed. You can't build to $20M ARR and assume someone will buy you anymore. Growth is currency again - and "efficient growth" no longer substitutes for actual growth. Why IQ and EQ Aren't Enough Anymore. The Age of AI Demands AQ. (9 minute read) The rise of AI demands a focus on Agency Quotient (AQ), the ability to actualize and execute effectively, which surpasses traditional metrics like IQ and EQ. High AQ individuals transform intentions into reality, essential in an AI-driven world where passive reliance on technology risks overshadowing human initiative. As AI democratizes intelligence, those who direct and build, rather than simply consume, will thrive, making AQ a critical skill for future productivity and innovation. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics The Opening, Midgame, and Endgame in Startups (5 minute read) Startups should simultaneously strategize for the opening, midgame, and endgame stages to avoid pitfalls and ensure growth. Successful founders balance initiating new ideas, achieving product-market fit, and envisioning future impact. This holistic approach enables nimble decision-making and sustained momentum, making businesses adaptable and resilient. The Lighthouse Playbook (6 minute read) In environments of abundance, the strongest signal isn't what you broadcast - it's what others say about you. This playbook flips standard growth advice: instead of building your brand, find people already on your team who are respected in networks you care about and invest in building their brands. Not influencer marketing - the opposite. Linear tells the world something about you because of how it positions craft. Stripe didn't say "payment processing" - they said "increasing the GDP of the internet. AI has lowered the cost of building products toward zero, but it hasn't done anything to change the cost of attracting people to you. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Reach over 6 million tech professionals where they are paying attention (Sponsor) TLDR puts you in front of your target audience with almost no competition. Each newsletter features only three advertisers max, giving your message the space it deserves. Millions of developers, product managers, CEOs, and other tech decision makers actually see what you have to say! Learn more about running a test campaign. Major (Tool) Major allows you to vibe code internal tools with enterprise security built in. Tensol (Tool) Hire AI Employees for your business. TierZero AI (Tool) AI agents for debugging production. 🎁 Miscellaneous Things Aren't Changing That Fast (6 minute read) New announcements are coming out of the AI industry every day, but not much has actually changed for most people. The people talking about AI are the people whose daily experience of AI is genuinely transformative, because they're the ones building and using it at the frontier. This creates a situation where the discourse is dominated by people for whom AI has changed everything. While the technology is advancing at a remarkable pace, the diffusion of that technology into the broader economy is proceeding at a much more normal pace. AI is subject to the same frictions as every other technology. The AI pricing and monetization playbook (30 minute read) AI isn't free to deliver, unlike traditional software. Compute and inference costs weigh heavily on AI monetization strategy. Every AI query incurs a non-trivial expense, which pricing must account for while capturing the value services create. This article looks at patterns and pitfalls from the experiences of dozens of AI teams to see how best to price an AI product. ⚡️ Quick Links The Best Product Onboarding a Team of Behavioral Scientists Has Ever Seen (3 minute read) A team of behavioral scientists analyzed Wispr Flow's onboarding and called it unanimously the best they've ever seen. The first time is never your fault (7 minute read) Obvious mistakes made by new team members are often due to a lack of communication rather than the person. MVP Pricing UX: Why Your ‘Free Trial First' Strategy Is Killing Activation (11 minute read) Users don't invest real effort and build meaningful habits because there's no commitment. Built by Agents, Tested by Agents, Trusted by Whom? (3 minute read) If a security product is entirely written and tested by AI agents, who is liable when something breaks?
