It's the only CRM with an agent that runs code on your data, turning a single prompt into competitive analysis, account plans, and detailed reports. 3 minutes to get started. Connect your inbox, and ask it anything. Try Lightfield – use code TLDRP13 for 3 months free. 📈 Headlines & Trends 2026 Tech Private Markets Analysis (8 minute read) Caplight analyzed ~5,000 closed secondary trades, and the numbers should make every founder rethink their timeline. AI startups are reaching $1B valuations in 3.4 years - half the 7-year average. Secondary volume hit $3.5B in 2025, up 75% YoY. Cursor, Perplexity, and ElevenLabs hit liquid secondary markets within 3 years of founding. Of the 20 largest IPOs in 2025, 14 were below their IPO price by year-end. The AI Acqui-Hire Wave (5 minute read) Between 2020 and 2025, there were 5,700 AI acquisitions - and 79% of them never disclosed a price. That's 4,500 quiet talent grabs where small teams got absorbed into bigger companies with zero fanfare. Accenture leads with 21 AI acquisitions (only three disclosed). Apple has 17 (two disclosed). The 75th percentile deal size tripled from $82M to $248M. Forget the billion-dollar headlines - the acqui-hire wave is the real story of AI M&A, and almost nobody is paying attention. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Tracksuit's Rocket Ride: From Bootstrap to Series B Success (15 minute read) Two Kiwi founders watched McKinsey-type firms charge $200K+ for brand tracking and decided to kill that market. They talked to 100 marketers with zero product and refused to build anything until 10 committed as beta customers. Their first product was a white-labelled Power BI dashboard. The move that defined them was hand-delivering gray tracksuits to early customers. Not merch - it was the "t-shirt theory." Customers started posting LinkedIn photos wearing them, creating an organic referral flywheel nobody planned. A few years later, they reached $42M ARR. Clarity of Thought (7 minute read) Clarity of thought is the strongest founder signal because it drives judgment across hiring, sales, and fundraising, independent of near-term outcomes. The best founders anchor ideas in first principles, define terms precisely, and present a clear conceptual framework that makes complex decisions feel obvious and coherent. This clarity enables fast, credible evaluation, lets others remember the company as a distinct idea, and compounds trust even when details are still evolving. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Startup Taxes 101 With Carta (Sponsor) Understanding your tax situation can save you from (costly) mistakes. People are leaving events early just to go back to work. They're waking up and checking their agents' progress before their coffees and texts. The fact that developers can do more now is causing anxiety, as the window to be first at anything feels like it is shrinking by the day. The 2026 SaaS Crash Isn't AI Killing Software - It's Budget Reallocation (8 minute read) Hyperscalers will spend $470B+ on AI infrastructure in 2026, and that money is coming straight from software budgets. The threat isn't AI replacing the product - it's AI reducing the headcount that uses the product. If 10 agents do the work of 100 reps, you don't need 100 Salesforce seats. CIOs are consolidating, not expanding. The "best of breed" era is over. This isn't the death of SaaS - it's the end of easy SaaS. Intention Is All You Need (10 minute read) The next 30 years will belong to the systems that own the shared intent that's required to orchestrate action and outcomes. These systems will define, coordinate, and accomplish the work to be done. Today's systems will become inputs that feed a system organized around an outcome. Each industry must discover abstractions and create legibility - the platforms that do so will win. ⚡️ Quick Links 10 Quick GTM Fixes You Can Ship This Week (3 minute read) Ten tactical fixes for early-stage founders that don't require an engineering sprint. (3 minute read) Before Uber, some people were taking taxis and hating it (coping). The Supply Side of AI (8 minute read) Every platform shift creates a new marketplace, and the supply side always follows the same pattern. title: TLDR Founders 2026-02-18
text: TLDR Founders 2026-02-18 Vertical AI playbooks 🧭, Selling to agents 🤖, navigating paradigm shifts 🧠 The Hidden Stack Behind AI Apps That Make It to Production (Sponsor) Most AI apps fail to reach production not because of models, but because of the invisible infrastructure layer underneath. Enterprise customers expect identity that works with their provider, permissions that reflect their org, directory sync that provisions their users, and security that responds in real time. WorkOS is the enterprise infrastructure layer powering the AI wave. OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, and hundreds of the fastest-growing AI companies build on WorkOS so they can focus on their product, not the stack beneath it. Learn how leading AI teams do it → 📈 Headlines & Trends You've been kicked out of the arena, you just don't know it yet (6 minute read) Humans can't compete with AI anymore, and most won't notice until it's too late. Companies are now competing with startups that can do everything they can do, but with AI, and without thinking about or working too hard. To compete, companies need to get everyone up-skilled immediately, start trying new tools, and remove barriers and the employees who are resistant to change. They should try to rebuild their product from scratch - if it takes less than two weeks, ask what that means for their company. Three dominant models of today's vertical AI horserace (6 minute read) Public SaaS market caps continue to free-fall while VC keeps investing in vertical AI at frothy valuations. There are three models emerging, and they require completely different playbooks. Model 1 sells AI-powered software to incumbents - de-risked GTM, but innovation ceiling. Model 2 acquires businesses and deploys AI to improve unit economics across a portfolio - immediate revenue, but integration nightmares. Model 3 builds the business from scratch as an AI-native replacement - maximum value capture, but cold start and regulatory hurdles. Many companies are choosing a model whose physics don't match their market. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Building on Sand: The Hidden Cost of AI Model Churn (13 minute read) AI pushes technical complexity upstream into the problem space, where the PM lives. This means the PM must become the architect because architecture is now a product artifact. An AI model update can invalidate assumptions, making it impossible to define products, user experience, feasibility, or cost. The only way to build durable AI products is to design systems that assume the model will change. How to Sell to Agents (7 minute read) In 1937, Ronald Coase asked why firms existed if markets are so efficient. His answer was transaction costs. AI agents are about to collapse those costs to near zero. An agent can discover a service, check its price, and call it in a single HTTP round-trip. HTTP 402 - "Payment Required" - has been reserved for future use since 1997. We're finally finding that use. The web was built for humans to browse. The next layer will be built for agents to buy. The Future of B2B Marketing is AI Agent Recommendations. And AI Agents Play Favorites (9 minute read) 87% of B2B software buyers say AI chatbots are changing how they research. LLM traffic is projected to overtake traditional search by the end of 2026. Economic party is expected even sooner due to significantly higher conversion rates from AI-referred traffic. The companies that figure out how to become the recommended answer will have an almost insurmountable distribution advantage. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Win AI search visibility before your competitors. (Sponsor) Search has shifted from links to AI-generated answers. This playbook breaks down what's actually working across 15M+ queries and how teams like Ramp, Carta, and Webflow build systems that earn citations, increase share of voice, and turn AI visibility into measurable pipeline. Get Report Now Figr AI (Tool) Figr is an AI product design agent for UX analysis and prototyping. Canary (Tool) Canary is an AI QA engineer that reads your codebase. Samora (Tool) Multilingual voice agents for customer ops. 🎁 Miscellaneous Navigating a Paradigm Shift (3 minute read) The hardest part of a paradigm shift is navigating it while it's incomplete. Paradigm shifts are tectonic shifts beneath markets, technologies, and human thought. They begin quietly at first, then the world suddenly bends. Paradigm shifts begin with dissonance, not consensus, as the old ways no longer fit the new reality. The best founders understand why the current system works, but believe there are better ways to do things. A Primer on Factory Economics for Startups (20 minute read) Tesla popularized the "factory-is-the-product" thesis. It now applies to companies making drone motors, robot actuators, PCBs, and battery packs. The learning curve is central to strategy: for each doubling of cumulative production, costs fall predictably. Your competitive position depends on where you are on the curve, how fast you descend, and whether you can finance the journey. ⚡️ Quick Links The Dark Side of Building in Public (3 minute read) Building in public means the criticism is public too. Zero to One Is a Subtraction Problem (6 minute read) Zero to one involves strategic choices about what features and design to prioritize, impacting a startup's early growth and learning. High-Frequency Software (15 minute read) Software engineering will turn to focus on coding the 'machine that builds the machine' over the next five to ten years. text: TLDR Founders 2026-02-20 Fundraise early 📈, hiring for new roles 💼, on taste 🧑‍🎨 Moonlighting as HR? Justworks lets you focus on your day job (Sponsor) You didn't start your company to spend hours on payroll spreadsheets and benefits paperwork. But somehow, that's been your life ever since you hired your first employee. With Justworks , you can simplify: 💵 Payroll ✅ Compliance 🎁 Benefits 🌎 Going international Justworks keeps all of that stuff organized in one place, so you can actually focus on what you built your company to do. And whether you're hiring your first employee or scaling your team, Justworks handles the complexities for you with real 24/7 human support. Join thousands of small businesses on Justworks 📈 Headlines & Trends Fundraising starts before you need capital (2 minute read) Most founders lose the fundraising game by waiting until they need money to start pitching. The real process begins with building an investor prospect list and seeking feedback rather than capital to establish early relationships. By sending monthly updates and executing publicly, founders create scarcity and FOMO, often securing commitments before a round even officially opens. Power in the Age of Intelligence (43 minute read) Tech companies like Stripe, SpaceX, and Base Power illustrate the "winner takes more" trend, where firms leverage technological advancements to dominate industries. The focus shifts from protecting software moats to identifying industry constraints, breaking them, and seizing "High Ground" for expansion. As technology progresses, companies controlling scarce assets benefit the most, driving increased economic concentration and growth opportunities. The Software Shakeout: What Is Durable and What Is Not in the Age of AI? (10 minute read) $1T in software market cap evaporated in three weeks. This framework sorts the survivors from the dead. Software in the path of doing work (CrowdStrike, Stripe, Shopify) is durable. Software that generates paperwork about work (DocuSign, Monday.com, Zendesk) gets replaced by AI that just does it. The scary middle - Atlassian, Salesforce, HubSpot - is eroding slowly but the cliff is coming. If you're building a product that humans fill out so other humans can read, rethink the entire premise before your next board meeting. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Against “Against Taste” (2 minute read) Taste is informed judgment built through experience rather than passive curation. True taste is exercised during the creation process through invisible, compounded decisions that lead to elegance and coherence. While AI can generate outputs, humans remain the essential definers of quality, setting the standards and directions that tools cannot shortcut. Hiring for a new role/function for your first time? Do this first. (3 minute read) A bad first hire in a new function costs you a year. The fix takes one week. Before you write the JD, do 5-10 calibration calls with people who already hold the role at companies one stage ahead of yours. You'll learn what "great" actually looks like, what comp is real, and which interview questions actually separate the best from the mediocre. Most founders skip this step and end up hiring on vibes. Don't be that founder. Advice we rejected to become one of the fastest growing start-ups ever (2 minute read) Deel co-founder Shuooo Wang details how the company scaled by ignoring traditional startup "best practices," such as hiring a VP of Sales early or focusing on the SF talent pool. Instead, Deel prioritized founder-led sales to maintain customer context, hired for traits over pedigree, and invested in 100+ local entities to build a moat around compliance and reliability. By favoring frugality and vertical M&A over high burn, the company leveraged first-principles thinking to become one of the fastest-growing startups. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Layers (Tool) AI marketing agents that analyze your product and run growth campaigns. Conway (Tool) Autonomous AI agent infrastructure layer. Boost.space (Tool) Single source of truth database for automations and AI agents. 🎁 Miscellaneous In Defense of Vertical Software (12 minute read) The industry is currently asking why vertical software needs to exist when AI companies are pouring billions of dollars into building the most powerful general-purpose AI systems in the world. Enterprise software is just code, but that's not where the value of software comes from. The value of enterprise software comes from understanding the process and the organization well enough to make the software do exactly the right thing. Process engineering will continue to be the foundational advantage or vertical software. There Is No Product (5 minute read) If you remove the difficulty, you remove the product. If it only takes a weekend to build a product, then anyone else could do it in a weekend, too. You can't just add features and hope the market comes back, because there's no core product. Some software will still be an asset, but most of it will become inventory, and every model generation will push that line further. ⚡️ Quick Links Why CEOs' AI hype really isn't landing with employees (4 minute read) Business leaders are struggling to translate AI enthusiasm into effective organizational change as employee skepticism grows. The SaaS Reset: Why SaaS is changing, not going away (4 minute read) The traditional build vs. buy equation has flipped as AI drastically reduces software creation costs. The Best Companies You've Never Heard Of (3 minute read) A portfolio company went from zero to $30M+ run rate in two years. text: TLDR Founders 2026-02-23 AI hits cybersecurity 🛡️, bad SaaS instincts 🧠, missionary founders ❤️ Every new AI feature you ship makes your billing problem worse (Sponsor) Here's a quick litmus test: can you launch a new AI feature tomorrow - with pricing that varies by tokens, model, region, and complexity - without creating new SKUs, without touching CPQ, and without manually adjusting invoices? If not, every feature you add is compounding your operational overhead instead of your revenue. A new whitepaper from Metronome argues that billing for AI products needs to work like a runtime system - computing pricing continuously as usage happens, not generating invoices after the fact. It covers why legacy billing architectures break under dimensional pricing, how credit-based monetization works , and how to assess whether your current stack can keep up. Download the whitepaper → 📈 Headlines & Trends AI Building: First Principles Still Work. SaaS Instincts Don't (12 minute read) The first principles of building a great company hadn't changed. Solve a real problem, deliver value that compounds, build something that's hard to replicate, and earn customers' trust. What's changed is the operating environment in which those principles must be applied. Founders who build enduring AI companies are the ones who have the discipline to hold on to first principles. These founders are expanding the size of their categories themselves rather than just building companies. DiSaaSter Strikes Cybersecurity (10 minute read) Anthropic announced Claude Code Security on Thursday. By Friday close, the Cyber ETF dropped 5% and Qualys fell 12%. The panic is teaching us more than the product itself. Okta and SailPoint cratered 10-11% despite Anthropic not mentioning "identity" once. Check Point and Fortinet barely flinched. The market is pricing in fear, not facts. If you're building in security, here's the cheat sheet: app-layer testing is about to get steamrolled by the foundation model labs, but categories with real infrastructure moats remain wide open for the next $10B+ cyber company. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Praying for Distribution (5 minute read) Tech companies are increasingly prioritizing distribution over product, with costs appearing in unexpected areas like COGS, as seen with AI companies treating inference costs as marketing. R&D is being leveraged to create self-sufficient products, evident in Atlassian's PLG model, spending more on R&D than S&M. Rising acquisition costs, indicated by longer CAC payback periods and decreasing NRR, emphasize the need for CFOs to focus on efficient distribution strategies. Strategic choices: When both options are good (15 minute read) Here's a good test for whether you've actually made a strategic decision: if the opposite of your position sounds dumb, it's not a real choice. "We put customers first" doesn't count. This piece runs through 12 choice pairs that actually matter - bootstrapped vs. funded, platform vs. point solution, sales-led vs. product-led - and shows what you're giving up on each side. Most founders never explicitly pick. They just end up somewhere and tell themselves a story about why. Travis Kalanick's fundraising advice for startups (1 minute read) Travis Kalanick advises startups to delay fundraising until achieving maximum traction for better terms and a competitive process. For those with existing VC backing, he suggests strategic timing to leverage incoming VC interest before formally raising funds. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Sully.ai, Baseten, and Nvidia partner to ship 10x faster, cheaper & specific models for healthcare (Sponsor) After moving to open source models such as gpt-oss-120b, Sully.ai's inference costs dropped by 90%, while response times improved by 65%, setting a new speed benchmark for healthcare AI. Sully.ai has now returned over 30 million minutes to physicians. Webhound (Tool) Long-running autonomous research agent. Hyperagent (2 minute read) Hyperagent is an agents platform where every session has an isolated, full computing environment in the cloud. It provides real browsers, code execution, image/video generation, data warehouse access, hundreds of integrations, and the ability to learn any new API as a skill. The agents follow conversations, understand context, and act when relevant. Hyperagent can be deployed into Slack with one click. Do Anything (Tool) Personal AI agent for everyday tasks 🎁 Miscellaneous Mercenary vs. Missionary Startups: Choose Your Next Adventure (6 minute read) This thread contrasts two founder archetypes: mercenaries, who optimize for valuation and exit, and missionaries, who optimize for solving a problem they deeply identify with. Prioritizing trust and mission over short-term economics ultimately saved the company. Missionary founders reveal themselves in how they pitch (problem-first vs. money-first), how they act under existential pressure (rule-breaking decisiveness like Steve Jobs at Apple), and how they compound advantages over time through customer obsession and reinvestment (as seen with Jeff Bezos). The File System Is the New Database (10 minute read) No database. No API. Just 80+ markdown and YAML files running a full personal operating system for AI agents - and it works better than the fancy alternatives. Agents pull only what they need. A voice encoding system keeps your tone consistent. Memory logs build context over time. The takeaway for anyone building AI-native products is surprisingly simple - your file system might be the best data layer you already have. ⚡️ Quick Links Sketching with code (4 minute read) You can now go from rough idea to working prototype in a single sitting using LLMs as a sketching tool. The argument here is that Claude 3.5 Sonnet shipping in June 2024 was the real inflection point for AI eating software - not ChatGPT. Competitive advantages that used to last years now last months. SaaS isn't dead, but revenue durability - the thing every VC priced their multiple on - might not exist anymore. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How to Find Your "One Metric to Rule Them All" (3 minute read) The founder of HotelTonight tracked 80 metrics at his first two startups. Both failed. At HotelTonight, he picked one - total transactions - and it worked. Slack found that teams sending 2,000+ messages had 93% retention. Airbnb zeroed in on nights booked. WhatsApp only tracked MAU. Who Owns Pricing? (5 minute read) Most SaaS companies have pricing spread across 3-4 different systems. Entitlements are hardcoded. Product catalogs live in multiple places. Launching a new tier takes six months. This piece lays out four pillars to fix it: unified catalog, decoupled entitlements, real-time metering, and a GTM control plane. Whether or not you buy the full framework, the diagnosis is right. If changing a price at your company takes an engineering sprint, something is very wrong. So You Want to Hire a Forward Deployed Engineer: How to Know If You Need One and How to Get the Role Right (32 minute read) Forward Deployed Engineers (FDEs) embed directly with customers to build the 'last mile' of the product to work in production. Unlike traditional solutions consultants or sales engineers, FDEs are still very much engineers who write and debug production code. Founders are turning to the FDE model to roll out highly technical AI products to remove the blockers that stand in the way of adoption. This post looks at what makes this role successful. FDEs can be powerful when deployed intentionally, but they're not the answer for every company or customer, and it takes a ton of careful design to get a return on investment in the role. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Most ads get ignored. A new kind of startup is emerging between the big headlines: the Silicon Valley small business. These companies are tiny, extremely leveraged, and shockingly productive. AI is being used as a force multiplier to ship faster, iterate more aggressively, and hit meaningful revenue in months rather than years. The result is a quiet but profound pressure on the traditional VC model. Expectations are being warped by a small group of AI-native outliers that don't need the level of capital the industry was built around. Why AI moats still matter (and how they've changed) (52 minute read) AI is an incredible tool for differentiation. However, the AI-ness of capability is not a source of defensibility. The defensibility of software resides in owning the end-to-end workflow, in the context in which it's applied, becoming the system of record, having a network effect, and being deeply embedded within customers. The market opportunity for software today is largely labor. Patterns Are the End of Product Analytics (5 minute read) AI agents and conversational interfaces are evolving customer interactions beyond traditional product analytics, which focused on linear sequences of clicks. Instead of static metrics, businesses need to analyze patterns of behavior across various channels to accurately capture dynamic consumer journeys. Pattern-based analytics can reveal evolving customer intent and improve the overall experience by highlighting efficient paths and potential friction points. ⚡️ Quick Links Two positions that still create durable AI moats (5 minute read) Durable defensibility comes from owning the “mint position,” where data is created at the moment work happens, not just read from a system of record. Concentration in VC (3 minute read) 3% of deployed capital delivered all returns across 1,200+ investments and 350+ exits. Automating taste (4 minute read) AI systems can encode prioritization and quality standards to apply product judgment consistently across software development. Andy Rachleff on what most founders get wrong about product/market fit (2 minute read) Founders often err by focusing on altering the "what" in their value hypothesis, rather than recognizing a technological inflection point for product development. text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-02 AI agents churn fast 🔁, AI network effects 🌐, Data moats or death 📊 Eliminate the "memory wall" causing inference bottlenecks (Sponsor) Serving inference on modern LLMs often fails to scale when GPU memory is constrained. Larger shared memory capacity lets you serve more users at lower latency, with less wasted compute. But true scalability doesn't require more memory; it requires a distributed memory that behaves like it's local. Crusoe's inference engine powered by MemoryAlloy™ technology delivers exactly that. The result? Breakthrough speed, throughput, and reliability for the most demanding AI workloads. Work with our team to optimize performance for your own fine-tuned model so you can scale without compromise. Explore Crusoe Managed Inference 📈 Headlines & Trends What's My Stage Again? (6 minute read) 95th percentile seed valuations hit $80.5M last year, nearly 3x higher than six years ago. 42% of seed rounds were over $5M. One company raised a $2B "seed." The word seed is now applied to everything from $500K SAFEs to $15M equity rounds, making it functionally useless. The real shift is that institutional capital is now arriving at true formation stage for the first time - not through accelerators or angels, but dedicated inception funds writing $500K-$3M checks pre-product, pre-team, pre-traction. Before raising, founders should ask every prospective VC one question: What does success look like for your fund? The answer will reveal whether their incentives actually align with your stage. Where Data Companies Are Heading in 2026 (5 minute read) Data companies without strong research DNA or volume advantages are collapsing or getting acquired because shifting RL research directions reward either frontier research leadership or rapid data scale, not generic RLaaS. Enterprise demand is moving toward subjective, self-serve post-training infrastructure as regulated environments, bespoke workflows, and reliability thresholds block direct model adoption. The winners combine top-tier research with field deployment engineers and full-stack post-training tooling, as sparse high-quality data, unverifiable tasks, and last-mile implementation become the real bottlenecks. The Wave of AI Agent Churn To Come: Prompts Are Portable (5 minute read) A $100M+ ARR AI company is crushing it - customers love the product, pipeline is strong. However, every deal closes on one-year terms. Not three. Not two. One. The reason is structural, not emotional. SaaStr recently switched AI sales agents by copy-pasting its best prompt into a competitor's product. Migration was 50-80% done in minutes. That's the new reality for AI agent companies. When gross retention drops from 92% to 82% because of prompt portability, you need an extra $10M in annual bookings just to stay even. The moat isn't your AI anymore. It's integration depth, vertical expertise, and infrastructure nobody wants to rebuild. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Intelligence is a commodity. Context is the real AI Moat (9 minute read) The way software is shipping is changing. Instead of shipping code to solve a narrow task for all users, developers are shipping general-purpose agents that modify themselves to adapt to the environment and task. This makes the context the product. The value capture in an AI-powered software industry will come from the context and runtime layer, along with hardware and software co-design. Many companies will likely regret the investment in chips they are currently making. The phone company always wins (7 minute read) Agents will destroy aggregation moats. However, true network effects, where products improve with more participants, will become more viable in more categories and modalities. More businesses will start building for network effects because there are now new surface areas, modalities, and GTM approaches. Software isn't dead, but the business is transforming into something much different from what it is today. The Minimum Lovable Product Era (12 minute read) Products that aren't lovable aren't viable in the market. The Minimal Lovable Product is the earliest version of a product that's genuinely lovable. Minimal Viable Products (MVPs) devolved into 'minimal functioning products' because dev work was expensive. However, costs are now collapsing. Building an MVP now just means building another tool people tolerate until they re-create it or a better one comes along. Lovability may be the last defensible moat. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Already running LinkedIn ads? Add TLDR to the mix. (Sponsor) Redact advertised in TLDR and cut their CPC in half compared to LinkedIn. 6M+ tech professionals, 40%+ open rates, max 3 sponsors per issue. See how TLDR compares. PromptURLs (Tool) Turn any prompt into a shareable URL. theORQL (Tool) Vision-enabled AI that fixes frontend bugs in Chrome. 🎁 Miscellaneous Software interoperability (10 minute read) For years, the winning strategy in software was consolidation. Own the workflow end-to-end. Make it painful to leave. That model is cracking. Agents need open protocols and shared standards to do their job - they don't care about ecosystem boundaries. Users now expect to swap parts freely. The result is that Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and AWS are all sitting at the same table contributing to shared agent protocols. Your moat is no longer lock-in. It's being the thing everything else connects to. If your platform is where value gets exchanged between tools, removing you breaks the whole workflow. The defensible position is a bridge, not a wall. Will AI Actually Lower R&D as a % of Revenue? (5 minute read) Everyone assumes AI will slash R&D budgets. The math doesn't work that way. Token costs are dramatically cheaper per unit of code produced, but Jevons Paradox kicks in hard. When something gets cheaper, you use more of it. Companies aren't cutting engineers. They're shipping more features faster and hiring more people to steer AI output. R&D as a percentage of revenue is going to stay flat or rise because every competitor gets access to the same productivity gains. The savings don't hit your bottom line. They get absorbed by the market. Are "Insiders" Buying Beaten Down Software Stocks Yet? (5 minute read) Software stocks are getting crushed but almost no insiders are buying. Out of 75 public software companies, only a handful of executives have purchased stock in the open market over the past two years. Asana's CEO has bought over $1B of his own stock and lost billions in the process. The more interesting signal is at ServiceNow, where the CEO, CFO, CPO, and AI Officer all canceled their scheduled selling plans on the same day. The CEO then waited exactly six months from his last sale because of the short-swing profit rule before buying $3M worth. Sometimes the absence of selling tells you more than any purchase would. ⚡️ Quick Links How to Build a VC Firm (19 minute read) The story of how Dragonfly grew to become one of the largest VC platforms in an industry that few have managed to survive in. Industry leaders and reports claim AI advancements might render some SaaS tools obsolete, yet many firms with strong infrastructure or specialized integrations will remain unaffected. While some SaaS products face challenges, AI is also creating new opportunities, potentially reshaping how SaaS tools are developed and utilized. The AI Industry Is Spending $1 to Make $0.99 (6 minute read) $443 billion in AI infrastructure spend last year. $51 billion in traceable revenue. That's a 10.3-to-1 ratio versus cloud computing's 2.4-to-1 at the same stage. Barclays says you'd need 12,000 ChatGPT-sized products to justify current capex. MIT found 95% of enterprise AI initiatives delivered zero measurable P&L return. The $500B gap closes one of two ways - either revenue materializes at unprecedented speed, or spending corrects violently. If you're building in AI and can't answer what a token is worth to you, you're spending $1 to make $0.99. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Be Distinctive & Don't Die (4 minute read) It's easier to be typical. It requires less energy, and it offends fewer people. However, the moment businesses stop investing in what makes them distinctive, they start merging into the landscape. Eventually, they become indistinguishable and fade into irrelevancy. Moats in the Age of AI (18 minute read) There are still several sources of defensibility even in an AI world. However, the default moat of the last few decades - switching costs - is likely not sustainable. Companies right now might spend $10,000 a year for software and $120,000 for a professional to complete the task. The next legendary company will just offer to complete the task. The frontier will shift as AI systems accumulate data about what good judgments look like in specific domains. The Only Thing That Survives (6 minute read) A company raised $4M, shipped a beautiful product, got written up in TechCrunch, and died in eleven months. Three teams built the same thing within six weeks of launch and one of them was free. Only 3% of VC-backed software companies ever reach $100M in revenue. If a team with $50M cloned you tomorrow, what would they still not be able to replicate in three years? This post presents seven real sources of durability that have nothing to do with your tech stack, from proprietary data that compounds with every interaction to infrastructure trust that takes years to earn to physical atoms that no weekend prototype can shortcut. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Six Rules for Designing Company Goals (22 minute read) Design company goals by focusing on clarity to ensure alignment and prioritization. Limit goals to three, assign a single owner, and prioritize one goal over others for effective decision-making. Clearly distinguish between goals and non-goals to maintain strategic focus and adjust strategies based on organizational learning and evolution. The Brand Age (25 minute read) Brands are the moat when differences between products disappear. Making the substantive differences between products is what the tech industry naturally tends to do. Good design tends to converge, but branding, by definition, has to be distinctive. Branding sells products, but the products still have to be good enough to maintain the brand's reputation. You Just Raised $5M to Build Someone Else's Moat (5 minute read) Every week a founder pitches "we fine-tuned the best model on domain data" like that's a moat. It isn't. Every competitor has the same models at the same price per token. The real defensibility comes from one decision most teams skip - building agents that remember things between sessions. Most AI products forget everything after each conversation. The ones that retain what you changed, accepted, and rejected get meaningfully better the longer you use them. After two years of daily use, switching feels impossible - not because of a contract, but because a new product would have to relearn everything from scratch. The moat isn't the model. It's how much the system has learned about you over time. If You're Behind on AI, Put Your 10-20 Best Engineers in a Different Building. Today (6 minute read) Taking your best engineers off your core product to build an AI agent is a risky move, but if you're behind on AI, it might be the only way to catch up. The incremental approach won't work. The companies that win in the next five years of B2B were the ones that made the bet on AI in 2025 and 2026. If your growth has stalled, win rates are dropping, or product feels stuck, the answer may be a focused team that wakes up every day thinking about nothing except the agent. ⚒️ Tools & Resources agent-cli (Tool) Suite of fully local AI-powered CLI tools. text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-09 Building for agents 🚀, the token path 🤖, AI reduces headcount 📉 The Nimble Scale Playbook: How Linear Grew to $1.25B with 80 People (Sponsor) Most startups mistake growth for headcount. The companies winning in the AI era are doing the opposite: growing revenue while staying lean. Cristina Cordova has seen it firsthand — helping shape GTM at Stripe, scale PLG at Notion, and now running operations at Linear ($1.25B valuation). In this playbook , she shares Linear's frameworks for scaling without bloat: • The 3 metrics she checks daily (including revenue per employee)• The 4-question hiring filter + the “interview 5 people first” rule• A chaos vs. dysfunction diagnostic• The collapsible team model that scales without constant reorgs Download the playbook to build a lean operating system for your startup. 📈 Headlines & Trends The Sword of Damocles in Software (2 minute read) GitHub Copilot's early lead diminished as competitors like Claude Code and OpenAI Codex surged past 100,000 users within months. From 2022 to 2025, software companies' net dollar retention gradually declined, with a sharp drop in 2026. Companies with simple, easily replaceable products, such as Asana and Zoom, now face increasing threats, leading to potential contraction. Do AI-enabled companies need fewer people? (3 minute read) The startup economy is undergoing a structural transformation. Startups are substituting compute for labor at an increasing rate. This could result in a lot more companies doing a lot more things, but that hasn't happened so far. The claim that companies can get by with way fewer people due to AI seems to be true. Building for trillions of agents (7 minute read) Agents are no longer just chatbots with basic tools. They now have sandboxed environments and can write and run code for any problem they run into, interact with APIs and CLIs directly, and have their own file systems and long-term memory. These primitives offer a glimpse of agents that can handle any task that can be thrown at them. The future will see enterprises with thousands more agents than people in a company. As a result, agents will become the primary users of all software, which means developers should start considering what software to build for these agents. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Get in the Token Path (6 minute read) Every AI workload ultimately boils down to tokens being generated, processed, and consumed by models. The companies that sit closest to the generation and consumption of tokens will be the ones whose revenue scales with AI activity. The winners of the cloud era monetized the core consumption primitive. That primitive is now tokens. Own the meter, and growth will take care of itself. On Being Legible to Capital (2 minute read) Fundraising legibility breaks into three stages: exterior status, network positioning, and pitch dynamics. Founders outside the top two tiers (repeat founders, top-company alumni) need to build a public body of work, like open source projects or writing, to generate inbound interest. Warm intros from a handful of well-networked angels matter more than any deck, since being "in-network" means capital-adjacent people already vouching for you before the first meeting. In the room, the founders who close know their market cold and flip the power dynamic by making the VC feel like they are competing for the deal rather than doing a favor. Avoiding a Culture of Emergencies (7 minute read) Managers can reduce team emergencies by deeply understanding their team's tasks, setting clear priorities, and developing a strong mental model of the team's role within the company. Good managers proactively address potential issues, make informed decisions, and maintain a long-term focus on productivity rather than short-term gains. Prioritizing team well-being and clarity leads to higher productivity and improved talent retention. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Stop doing CRM data entry. (Sponsor) CRMs only work if you keep them updated. Founders don't have time for that. Lightfield is the AI-native CRM built for startups. It automatically captures emails and meetings, keeps every record up to date, and surfaces follow-ups with zero manual entry. No logging. No cleanup. No lost context. TestSprite 2.1 (Tool) Automatically generate and run software test suites. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Honest Reason AI Will Beat Many Sales Reps: They Don't Really Do A Lot Of The Work. But Agents Do and Will (8 minute read) AI will replace the sales reps who weren't really doing the job in the first place. Great sales reps own their scheduling, run real demos, know the product, industry, and competition, and solve the actual problem. They follow up relentlessly and simplify deployment. The good reps will use AI to do even more work even faster. The reps that were coasting will be replaced by systems that do the work better than they do. The Taste Rankings: Which VCs Actually Have It (3 minute read) ADIN Research has identified a bug in OpenAI's GPT-4 API that exploits confidentiality issues. There are potential risks for users relying on the model for sensitive data processing. This issue prompts discussions on securing AI applications. The Cowan Paradox (6 minute read) The Cowan Paradox, named after historian Ruth Schwartz Cowan's finding that vacuum cleaners didn't reduce housework but raised cleanliness standards and reassigned labor to fewer people, maps directly onto AI agents in B2B. A Berkeley Haas eight-month field study confirmed it: workers adopted AI, expanded into adjacent roles, stopped separating work from non-work hours, and ended up busier than before. ⚡️ Quick Links Every Bit of Effort Helps (1 minute read) Early-stage startup progress rarely feels linear, but the breakthrough usually comes from one more meeting, one more call, or one more feature pushed past the point where most founders stop. AI Made Relationships, Not Features, the Last Defensible Advantage (5 minute read) Companies should build history, context, and trust by understanding their customers, measuring the right outcomes, and continuously improving the relationship through every interaction so that switching feels like starting over. PopSockets founder David Barnett talks about building a viral business (2 minute read) David Barnett founded PopSockets after seeking a better way to hold headphones, creating a viral phone accessory. text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-11 AI headwinds 🤖, data agent context 📊, AI-native org charts 💼 Building with AI is the easy part. Now price it (Sponsor) Usage-based, workflow-based, commit-based, self-serve...the monetization complexity of AI is one of the biggest challenges facing founders. Without a unified framework, even a small product change can cascade through sales, product, engineering, and finance. But leaders like OpenAI are shifting to a new model — one that tracks value, not features. It's called the monetization operating model. In this Metronome whitepaper , you'll learn: Why the shift to value-based pricing is underway How to make it a reality What it means to treat pricing as your product Read the whitepaper (no signup required) 📈 Headlines & Trends Coding got easier. Winning with AI products didn't. Here's why (22 minute read) The best PMs are leveraging AI to absorb large amounts of context and make sense of sales call recordings, notes, messages, support tickets, and other sources of information. AI can draft experiments to test, flag patterns worth investigating, and generate hypotheses faster than any planning cycle ever could. 'What to build' will eventually become another AI-assisted workflow. The obstacles now sit downstream, mostly with what happens after shipping a product. This article looks at potential headwinds for AI products and suggests fixes for each. The Great SaaS Reset: From 30x Euphoria to AI-Driven Repricing (12 minute read) Figma lost 80% of its market cap. Salesforce trades below 4x revenue. The "10x ARR Club" used to have 60% of public SaaS companies in it. Now it's 5%. Not every SaaS company is dying the same death. There are two kinds emerging, and the difference between them is whether a customer would rather rebuild from scratch than rip you out. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Meetings are Products (4 minute read) Everyone should tend to their recurring meetings the way they would tend to any other product they were responsible for. This can reveal unnecessary meetings or issues that no one thought to question. Some meetings only exist because the highest-paid person in the room decided that's how things work. However, attendees can usually adjust the who, what, how, or when these meetings occur. Your Data Agents Need Context (14 minute read) The market is aware that a lack of context is a problem, but it is still in the early stages of building solutions. Building a proper data agent is a blend of technical challenges related to data infrastructure and engineering and human operational challenges related to tribal knowledge collection. This post proposes a modern context layer paired with an agentic data system. Not every enterprise will build this in-house, so the market is open for an external solution. The Only Pitch Prep You'll Ever Need (2 minute read) Redbud VC's pre-seed framework prioritizes how founders reason under pressure over rehearsed answers. Sharpest signals include naming credible failure modes, articulating uncomfortable market beliefs, and showing how customer feedback has changed their thinking. The strongest indicator of success is the ability to say "I don't know" and explain the plan to find out, as early companies typically fail from misjudging reality and updating too slowly. Vishal Kapoor's 10 rules for building honest products with AI (12 minute read) Building with AI is a trust challenge as much as it is a technical challenge. Affirm, a company that empowers customers with flexible payment plans, believes that earning user trust is key to its mission and enduring success, especially due to the deeply emotional nature of personal finances and the often-convoluted terms of credit card companies. This article features 10 tips from Vishal Kapoor, SVP of Product at Affirm, on how to help teams accelerate development and explore more ideas while still delivering experiences that are honest and human at their core. ⚒️ Tools & Resources 42%+ open rates. 7M+ tech professionals. Add TLDR to your media mix. (Sponsor) TLDR reaches developers and technical decision makers across 13 newsletters. Already running Meta? TLDR fills the gap it can't. See how TLDR compares. autoresearch (Tool) AI agent that runs 100 LLM experiments overnight on one GPU. Timelaps (Tool) Measure marketing effectiveness using real-time consumer insights. Gooseworks (Resource) A collection of GTM skills for your AI agents. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Org Chart Math Behind AI-Native Speed (2 minute read) Metcalfe's Law states that each new team member adds n-1 new connections. This means there are 11,175 potential communication channels in a traditional 150-person organization. An AI-enabled team producing equivalent output might need 30 people, dropping potential communication channels down to 435, a 96% reduction. AI-native startups are pulling ahead because fewer humans means fewer channels and faster iteration, compounding speed. White Collar Goes Blue (6 minute read) Your autonomy, your creative ownership, your seat at the table - none of that was ever a right. It was compensation for being hard to replace. A startup founder recently admitted their core team could just do the work themselves with AI, and dealing with ten personalities on a spectrum of competence isn't worth it anymore. You can't walk into a one-on-one and say, "Actually, we just need you to execute now and stop having opinions." So companies are resetting instead. Lay people off, restructure, and rehire the same roles but scoped as trades from the start. ⚡️ Quick Links 10x is the new floor (3 minute read) People who haven't personally built anything in years should be worried, as their careers were built on reliable competence - and the tools just caught up to it. The Enterprise Context Layer (17 minute read) The Enterprise Context Layer is the self-updating central intelligence that can answer questions on all knowledge for the company. The Greatest Alpha in VC isn't Selection - it's Category Foresight (3 minute read) If you identify the right category early enough, you don't need to be right about which company wins. Out Of Distribution (2 minute read) A study of founders behind $5B+ companies revealed recurring traits of neurodivergence, polymathic range, and early trauma that create a visceral connection to the problem being solved. text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-13 AI-native management 🤖, revenue addiction 💰, fintech moats 🛡️ 0→1 proved your idea works. Persona proves your users are real. Free for early-stage (Sponsor) A growing company attracts fraudsters. They'll often strike when you're most distracted - launches, promos, funding announcements. Strong identity verification protects your business; adjusting friction based on risk keeps your conversions flowing. To do both, you need a modern identity platform. Persona's Startup Program gives early-stage companies access to the same tool used at Etsy, LinkedIn, and Twilio – starting at $0 : → 500 free verifications/month for 12 months → No-code flow editor to instantly build branded KYC flows → Exclusive access to founder events, Q&As, live workshops and compliance guides Apply to the Persona Startup Program today→ 📈 Headlines & Trends Building Where the Buyers Are (5 minute read) For the last twenty years, the hardest part of building a tech company was the development itself. AI is dissolving that constraint. Companies no longer need to have large teams to ship products. This means Silicon Valley may no longer be the center of the tech industry. Companies needed to be near the engineers when the hard part was writing code, but they will now move to where the buyers actually are. Fintech's Moats Don't Compile (4 minute read) Fintech's primary moats reside in regulatory permissions, credit data, and risk-bearing capacity rather than replicable software code. While AI compresses margins for pure SaaS by commoditizing code, it strengthens fintechs that own their licenses and underwriting history. Competitive advantage is shifting away from UI toward those who control the "fin" side by managing money movement and proprietary risk models that AI optimizes but cannot replace. Management In The Age Of AI (4 minute read) Managers must be builders in 2026 because not building now is the bigger waste of time. They need to learn AI tools, and building is often the most efficient path for the team. The ability to build will be a key differentiator between teams that win and teams that drown in their own output. The gap between good management and bad management is now a lot wider. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Ben Horowitz on What Makes the Best Fundraising Pitch (6 minute read) The fundamentals haven't changed nearly as much as people think. Strategy is still about articulating a clear 'why'. Product management is still about shipping the right product at the right time. The best fundraising pitch is the one you actually believe. The landscape around the fundamentals has changed, but there are still massive new opportunities hiding in plain sight. How You Source Great Designers (6 minute read) If you want the best designers, you have to go where they are and be someone they want to talk to. X is a good platform for designers who are exploring startup opportunities. Look for designers who actively follow tools and communities that signal taste. Most self-aware designers move quietly through trusted networks. Founders need to be plugged into those networks before they need them. How PostHog Built a Marketing Engine That Actually Drives Growth (6 minute read) Tens of millions in ARR, $1.4B valuation, zero outbound sales calls ever. Usage-based pricing with a generous free tier means one engineer installs it for a side project and it spreads inside the company the way Figma did. Their newsletter has 75K subscribers and never mentions PostHog - just teaches engineers how to build better products. The New GTM Playbook: Outbound Alone Won't Cut It (3 minute read) AI has commoditized cold outreach, forcing a shift from outbound-only tactics to a multi-channel flywheel of content, community, and retargeting. ColdIQ nearly doubled revenue by using content to prime prospects, resulting in conversion rates 3x higher than traditional cold contacts. Success in 2026 requires building this organic distribution early, as the 6-12 month startup time creates a massive lead over competitors still relying on saturated outbound channels. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Figr (Tool) Spot UX blind spots before writing code. Fish Audio (Tool) Expressive text-to-speech and voice cloning platform. Notte (Tool) Turn any website into a callable API. 🎁 Miscellaneous Strategic choices: When both options are good (12 minute read) "Customer-first" isn't a strategy - nobody picks "customer-last." Real strategy means choosing between two good options where smart companies exist on both sides. "All-in-one" means you personally own every feature request forever. "Infinitely extensible" means your UX becomes a patchwork quilt. If the opposite of your strategy sounds stupid, you haven't decided anything. Revenue addiction kills companies (4 minute read) If revenue is your North Star, your company is destined to die. Revenue-addicted companies kill every initiative that doesn't move the number this quarter, which is exactly the opposite of what you need during the AI transition when you have to make big product bets. Lovable swapped its North Star to daily active apps and built a qualitative "Lovable Score" combining NPS, PMF score, effort score, and CSAT. Revenue still matters, but it's the output of the system, not the thing you optimize for. ⚡️ Quick Links Productive Individuals Don't Make Productive Firms (5 minute read) Individual AI gains often create organizational chaos and "slop" rather than firm-level value, mirroring the 30-year lag in productivity seen during factory electrification. Serval's Path to Product-Market Fit (15 minute read) "What's your biggest pain point?" is dead as a discovery question. Agent Skills are the recipe. Vertical agents are the cake (2 minute read) Selling skills is like selling the recipe, and you want to be in the cake business. Being a “System of Record” Won't Save You. It'll Just Make Your Decline More Comfortable (6 minute read) System of Record businesses that don't own the AI layer on top of their platforms will see spend flowing around them - while they will get renewals, they won't see growth. text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-16 How to thought lead 🧠, data is the moat 📊, SaaS metrics for AI 🤖 Technical skills are no longer the bottleneck (Sponsor) Technical skills used to be the biggest roadblock to launching a startup. Today, you just need the right idea. Prompt that idea. Build that app. Add payments. Publish it securely. All on Replit . Replit helps you start, build and scale your business with enterprise-grade security & controls — which is why it's trusted by thousands of small businesses and Enterprise companies like Stripe, Zillow, and Coinbase. 50M+ builders are bringing their ideas to life on Replit. 💬 Don't let your ideas die in a Google Doc. Bring them to life with Replit 📈 Headlines & Trends Data Is the Only Moat (7 minute read) Think about the metadata you collect when people use your platform or when you observe the consequences of using your product. For example, the times when people post from social media share links, when most people engage, or what kind of content drives the most engagement. This data is your most unique data moat. Use it to improve how people use your product and how they run their own businesses. Rethinking SaaS Metrics for AI (7 minute read) If you're still tracking ARR as your north star, it's lying to you. AI-native companies need different metrics - token consumption is the one that's hard to fake because the fastest-growing companies sit directly in the token path. Raw tokens don't tell you if you're creating value or just passing through infrastructure costs, so gross profit per million tokens is becoming the real scorecard investors are pricing companies on. DAUs stop mattering when AI agents are doing the work inside other products via MCP, and nobody trusts the LTV of any AI product right now. AI Won't Replace Innovation (11 minute read) History suggests that the future of AI will look less like a monopoly and more like an ecosystem. Frontier labs will build the infrastructure while thousands of companies emerge to turn that intelligence into products, workflows, and systems that solve real problems. Completely new jobs will likely be created. Founders and startups that are willing to deeply understand customers, build thoughtful systems, and focus relentlessly on specific workflows will see more opportunity than ever. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How to Thought Lead (2026) (18 minute read) Earn mindshare for your topic by regularly posting information, products, and mental models to be used by others. Thought leadership boils down to six principles, ordered explicitly in descending order of importance: have credibility; know what people aspire to; be authoritative, knowledgeable, and useful; be quoteable; be strongly opinionated; and be first, or be funny. This post looks deeper into these principles to provide a formula for thought leadership. The best thought leaders do the work first, earn credibility, and then let everything amplify a foundation that's already solid. Developer Relations: what it is, and how to measure it (10 minute read) DevRel is such a flexible and multifaceted role that it means very different things depending on what company you're at. The role has five primary facets: documentation, engineering, content and events, product feedback, and community management. Founding team trust matters infinitely more than founding team structure. Having the right team to make the pie big is worth more than having a larger slice of a smaller pie. The more the individual parts are acknowledged, the better off everyone will be. Large capital providers can still deploy capital to all four types of investing. text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-18 Software budget shifts 💰, founder storytelling 📚, AI terminal value collapse 💸 The AI founder's pricing playbook: lessons from scaling ElevenLabs, Runway, and Hex (Sponsor) Today's AI companies are reaching the $1M ARR milestone faster than any prior generation. But turning user growth into revenue isn't simple: pricing needs to match user value , and billing gets more complicated as you scale. To help you navigate these challenges, Stripe interviewed the founders behind the fastest-growing companies . Learn how ElevenLabs, Runway, and Leonardo AI turned their visions into global revenue powerhouses and which tools they used to monetize and achieve massive scale faster. ↗️ Get the playbook ↗️ 📈 Headlines & Trends The Marginal Cost of Entrepreneurship is Trending Towards Zero (5 minute read) Shopify is increasingly becoming the core infrastructure for modern commerce. The platform dramatically reduces the barriers to starting a business by unifying payments, logistics, and software. The company is embedding AI directly into its entrepreneur stack to lower the time and labor required to build and run a company. Shopify is making entrepreneurship increasingly frictionless, and the data reflects it. How Much of the Software Slowdown Is Just Budgets Flowing to Anthropic and OpenAI? Maybe As Much As 70% (14 minute read) Almost every major public software company has printed lower growth rates every single year since the 2021 peak. The conventional explanation is that vibe coding is eating software, but what's actually happening is simpler and more damaging: companies have finite budgets, and they're pouring an unprecedented share of those budgets into AI infrastructure and foundation model providers. The software budget pool is now shared with a new category that went from zero to over $40 billion in two years. This structural rebalancing will take several years to sort itself out, and it is going to hurt a lot of software companies before it does. Why the World Still Runs on SAP (7 minute read) Upgrading from SAP ECC to S4HANA costs $700M, takes 3 years, and requires a team of 50 from Accenture. Lidl scrapped its transition after blowing $500M. Workers toggle between apps ~1,200 times per day, and 47% can't find the information they need. Startups like Axiamatic, Conduct, and Tessera are wrapping legacy systems in AI instead of replacing them, targeting the $380B system integration market. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Why Founders Can't Delegate Storytelling (2 minute read) Storytelling is strategy, and the founder is the only person who holds the full context: why the company started, which experiments failed, what those failures taught, and where the company is heading. When storytelling gets delegated, marketing, sales, and product each fill in the gaps with their best guesses, creating subtle misalignment that compounds across every function until customers are closed on the wrong story and the product builds toward the wrong vision. Show Customers Their Wins (5 minute read) Retention is half a perception problem - customers don't churn because they stopped getting value, they churn because they stopped noticing it. The fix starts on day one by cementing their "before" state so every improvement has a visible benchmark. Rilla went from $0 to $70M ARR in 7 years as the 7th fastest-growing SaaS company, built entirely on consumer tactics like streaks and goals that most B2B companies ignore. The Most Underrated Hire Right Now Is a Great Product Person (2 minute read) When building was hard, engineering was the bottleneck, and the status hierarchy reflected that. Now that building is easy, the variance in outcomes has shifted almost entirely to judgment on what to build, how to sequence it, and how to talk about it. The rarest version of this hire sits at the intersection of culture and deep technology, genuinely bilingual between what is technically possible and which cultural currents are real versus ephemeral. ⚒️ Tools & Resources 81% of enterprise buyers audit for compliance - and they're not taking your word for it (Sponsor) Your biggest customers are no longer satisfied with a form that says you're compliant - they're looking for cold hard proof. Zip Security surveyed 301 companies with under 500 employees to understand the impact security is having on sales. Read the blog to find out why security is now a sales problem . Kipps AI (Tool) AI agents for lead qualification and outreach. OpenViktor (Tool) Open-source AI employee that lives in Slack. Refgrow (Tool) Launch affiliate and referral programs in minutes. 🎁 Miscellaneous The Collapse Of Terminal Value - What Happens If AI Makes Every Moat Temporary? (6 minute read) $58 trillion in S&P 500 market cap repriced at 5x free cash flow instead of today's 20x means a 75% drawdown - roughly $44 trillion evaporating. That's the math if AI makes every competitive moat temporary instead of durable. Newspapers went from 12-15x EBITDA to 2-4x in a decade, retail followed, and taxi medallions went to zero. The $300-500B/year in AI capex only pencils out if returns are durable, but AI itself is the thing making returns short-lived. Spending other people's time (7 minute read) Asking for someone's time is virtually free for the asker, yet enormously expensive for the person being asked. The trade is asymmetric, with the askee having to rebuild whatever mental state they were in before the interruption, while the asker just gets on with their day. The problem compounds with AI as people are effectively shifting their 'productivity' onto other people's shoulders. Organizations need to make these costs visible and put friction in the right places so that the default path protects attention instead of consuming it. ⚡️ Quick Links You Are Responsible for Your Agent (2 minute read) Companies are liable for the actions of employees' personal AI agents, akin to past challenges with personal devices. Why and How to Build a Research Org (12 minute read) This is the moment for concentrated research ambition in every vertical. 25-28% of two-founder startups lose a cofounder by year four (2 minute read) Data from 18,920 US startups on Carta shows 25-28% of two-founder teams lose a cofounder by year four. title: TLDR Founders 2026-03-20
text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-20 End of fragmentation 🧩, how to run pilots 🚀, build opinionated SaaS 💻 How the world's fastest-growing AI companies scaled revenue (Sponsor) Runway, ElevenLabs, and Leonardo AI had one thing in common: they didn't build their own billing infrastructure - they shipped product instead. Stripe's scaling AI playbook captures what these three AI leaders learned about monetizing quickly and scaling globally. Inside the playbook you'll read how: ElevenLabs scaled to a $3B valuation with just one engineer managing billing. Leonardo AI reached 18M+ users across 100+ countries. Runway monetized quickly using complex hybrid pricing. Ready to start scaling? Get the playbook . 📈 Headlines & Trends The Parasite Fallacy (2 minute read) Being a system of record is no longer enough in the age of agents. The value is quickly shifting to the 'system of action'. Databases are cost centers, whereas agents that can complete tasks are value multipliers. Companies that focus on how to tax the consumption of their data rather than how to enable better actions become more of a utility than a platform. The goal should be to create value by being the best place to execute. The End of Fragmentation (17 minute read) Many people think that AI will fragment markets because the technology makes it easier to create software, which means new companies will be built to serve every niche use case. AI will allow clones of major tech companies to emerge to carve out market share. However, it is more likely that AI will drive greater concentration of value into fewer companies and create the biggest businesses of all time. History teaches us that new technology leads to more concentration, and AI has the potential to be the most powerful example of this yet. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics Why pilots matter more than you think—and how to run them right (5 minute read) Pilots are opportunities. When structured intentionally, they can be a powerful tool in sales motion. Pilots accelerate trust, demonstrate value, and lay the groundwork for long-term partnerships. The key is to be clear and intentional, and to keep momentum high. Getting the pilot motion right will result in a far more predictable sales process and a clearer path to scalable revenue. Guillermo Rauch's 5 Lessons for Founders Building in the AI Era (7 minute read) Open source stress-tests product market fit, as people won't pay for something they won't use for free. You need a bold, boundless mission, but be focused in what you are building. AI makes it even more important to be mindful when choosing what problems deserve your attention. Choosing what to dismiss is a competitive advantage. Hire people who are on the rise rather than people who've already made it. Build SaaS with Strong Opinions (4 minute read) SaaS founders should throw out the old 'configure it all' mentality and do the hard work of figuring out what works best, then sell that as a strongly opinionated piece of software. Businesses will increasingly pay to shortcut their way to best practice rather than buy the tools to do it themselves. This will allow them to focus on their unique value and not waste time reinventing the same sales pipeline used by everyone else. Companies that combine services and software will have a unique advantage. I've Spent Over $1M on Tech Launches in the Past Year. Here's Everything I Know (14 minute read) Startups burned over $100 million on launch campaigns last year. 86% of those videos didn't crack 50,000 views. This post contains a detailed tutorial on how to engineer trending videos from the first frame to the last repost. The author's team has spent over a million dollars orchestrating launch campaigns on X over the past year, achieving an average of 1.5 million views per video. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Lightfield (Tool) CRM that builds itself from your emails and calls. Banyan AI Lite (Tool) Detect and prevent SaaS churn before it happens. Terminal Use (Tool) All-in-one hosting infrastructure for long-running agents. 🎁 Miscellaneous Micro-App Portfolios - 5% Hit Rate, Vibe-Coded Exits, Portfolio OS (12 minute read) One solo founder launched 70+ projects with zero employees and hit $3.1M ARR. The playbook is portfolio theory applied to startups - ship fast, kill fast, let a 5% hit rate cover the failures. Vibe coding tools like Lovable ($100M ARR in 8 months, $6.6B valuation) and Bolt.new ($40M ARR in 6 months) make the math work by collapsing build time from months to days. A $20-50/app/mo maintenance layer turns the portfolio's biggest liability into recurring revenue. Size of the Prize and Run the Distance (5 minute read) Nvidia took 3 decades to reach $1T but just 2 years to go from $1T to $5T. At 10% annual compounding, $10T is next. The investing variable that founders and investors consistently get wrong is the size of the prize - nobody imagined Airbnb, DoorDash, or Uber becoming what they are. An outlier founding team can compound a seed into $10B, then $100B in two decades, then $1T in three. Technical Leaders Make These 4 Common Storytelling Mistakes (5 minute read) Every time you say "well, technically..." you're about to kill a good story. The mistakes that tank most founder pitches all come from the same place - treating all details as equally important. Your audience doesn't want comprehensive, they want compelling. Short anecdotes of 5-15 seconds land better than any 12-step hero's journey, and the hook that gets someone in the door is never the same thing that gets them to stay. ⚡️ Quick Links The Illusion of Speed - Why AI Is Making Teams Faster but Not Better (3 minute read) Your engineering team is shipping more code than ever, but none of the metrics that actually matter are moving. Startup Punditry's 25 Years of Failure (22 minute read) Startup methodologies widely adopted over the past 25 years have not increased startup success rates, as shown by unchanged survival rates in government data. The Monoculture of Startup Methods (9 minute read) The widespread adoption of similar startup methodologies fosters mediocrity. title: TLDR Founders 2026-03-23
text: TLDR Founders 2026-03-23 YC W26 AI-first 🤖, AI deployment 💼, the startup circle of life 🚀 Monetizing AI: Your pricing strategy for scale (Sponsor) As your business grows, your payments and billing infrastructure can become a bottleneck. While some struggle with operational complexity, companies like ElevenLabs and Runway built systems that scale smoothly with user growth . Stripe's scaling AI playbook unpacks those companies' blueprints for hyper-growth and offers suggestions to reduce operational drag. These leaders automated billing complexity and designed pricing strategies that adapt with their business. As a result, teams can focus on building next-generation AI products, while their billing and revenue operation largely operates in the background. Ready to eliminate the scaling bottleneck? Get the playbook . 📈 Headlines & Trends I Analyzed All YC W26 Companies: Here's What It Tells Us About the Future (4 minute read) 85% of YC's W26 batch is AI-first, with 56 of 198 companies building fully autonomous agents explicitly positioned as AI employees targeting every $50-150K knowledge worker role, from AI accountants closing books without human touch to AI law firms staffed entirely by models. Healthcare is the largest vertical with 22 companies going after clinical work itself rather than EHR wrappers, while 13 robotics companies are actively deploying alongside a full picks-and-shovels ecosystem for physical AI. The Great Circle of Life (8 minute read) Every major technology era follows the same arc - consumer first, then enterprise, then infrastructure - and AI is no exception. The first wave of winners built directly for consumers, but the pattern from prior eras says the durable businesses will be the ones building tooling and infrastructure layers underneath. If you have seen this cycle before, the playbook is clear: the picks-and-shovels companies outlast the gold rushers. Recognizing where you sit in this arc shapes when to enter, who to build for, and how to time your raise. 🧠 Strategies & Tactics How to Build a Real Operating Plan: Inside a Fully Linked Startup FP&A Model (7 minute read) Revenue and bank balance stop being enough the moment an investor or board member asks a hard question. A fully linked FP&A model connects revenue forecasts to hiring plans, burn rate, and fundraising timelines in a single living document - change one input and every downstream assumption updates automatically. This is what lets you stress-test scenarios like a churn spike or a delayed round before they actually happen. It is also the kind of financial infrastructure sophisticated investors expect to see when you are defending your raise. How to vet a cofounder (7 minute read) To vet a co-founder, work directly with them on a real project to gauge compatibility and efficiency authentically. Lay the groundwork for collaboration by evaluating past outputs, seeking references, and discussing values to identify potential long-term challenges or alignments. Avoid superficial interviews, and prioritize genuine trials to make informed decisions about meaningful partnerships. The Psychology Behind Viral LinkedIn Content (5 minute read) Posting times, hashtag counts, and emojis have almost nothing to do with LinkedIn virality - emotional architecture does. The gap between posts that explode and posts that die in silence comes down to how the information makes people feel, not what information is being shared. Think identity validation, status signaling, and tribal belonging baked into every sentence. One company went from crickets for 8 months to booking 96 calls in a single month after rebuilding content around emotional psychology instead of information delivery. Spend less time on the insight and more time engineering the feeling. To Thrive Today, You Have to Become An Agentic Deployment Expert. But So, So Few Actually Are (9 minute read) Companies no longer need engineers to win. They need someone who treats deployment as a core job function - an agentic deployment expert. These hires are worth more than anyone else you can hire right now. They don't need to be technical, they just have to know how to use AI tools to get to deployment. Companies that don't hire an agentic deployment expert risk wasting years of work. ⚒️ Tools & Resources Your enterprise customers aren't asking if you have security anymore — they're asking how it's built (Sponsor) Zip Security connects your identity provider, MDM, and EDR into one enforced posture. Automatically. Deploy in two weeks, pass the audit, win the deal. No dedicated security team required. Learn why security is now a sales problem and prepare for the customer audit Fractal (Tool) Build, test, and deploy ChatGPT apps. Supermemory (Tool) Supermemory is an agent memory system that achieves ~99% SOTA accuracy. 🎁 Miscellaneous 1:1s Are Being Run Wrong (5 minute read) Status update 1:1s are a waste of everyone's time, and the fix is simpler than you think. The shift that matters is moving from "what did you do this week" to questions that uncover blockers, ambitions, and candid feedback - the things people will not volunteer in a group setting. A well-run 1:1 builds trust, surfaces problems weeks before they escalate, and develops your team in ways that compound as you scale. Getting this cadence right early creates a management culture that becomes a genuine competitive advantage. The bespoke software revolution? I'm not buying it (2 minute read) Most people don't like computers and only tolerate them because they have to. They likely would rather not think about them at all, given the choice. These people might use AI, but they won't be using the technology to build their own systems and tools to maintain. They just want to get the task done. ⚡️ Quick Links Scale Is a Tactic, Not a Virtue (2 minute read) Silicon Valley treats scale like a moral good when it's actually just a strategic choice. Your Notes Are the Moat (4 minute read) A field report on the convergence happening across personal knowledge tools in March 2026. The Cold Start Trap: Why the Best Social Infrastructure Almost Never Succeeds (2 minute read) The "cold start" trap hinders promising social infrastructure by requiring mass initial adoption for value, causing most projects to fail. Vibecoding is Self-Expression (4 minute read) Vibecoding has quietly turned software creation into a creative act, and that shift puts a large class of consumer utilities on shaky ground.
